concepts.label: $L^1$ on $\mathbb R$

content.statement: Absolutely integrable functions on the real line.
content.definition: $$\begin{aligned} L^1(\mathbb R)&=\left\{f:\mathbb R\to\mathbb C\ \text{measurable}:\int_{\mathbb R}|f(x)|\,dx<\infty\right\}/\sim,\\ f\sim g&\Longleftrightarrow f=g\ \text{a.e.} \end{aligned}$$
content.formal: $L^1(\mathbb R)=\{f:\int_{\mathbb R}|f(x)|\,dx<\infty\}$, with functions identified if they agree almost everywhere.
content.intuition: This is the natural minimal setting where the Fourier transform integral is defined as an absolutely convergent integral.

assumptions: Functions are identified up to equality almost everywhere.

concepts.label: $L^1$ Space

content.statement: $L^1$ is the space of integrable measurable functions modulo equality almost everywhere.
content.definition: $L^1(X,\mu)$ is the space of absolutely integrable functions on a measure space.
content.formal: Let $(X,\mathcal A,\mu)$ be a measure space. The space $L^1(X,\mu)$ consists of equivalence classes of measurable functions $f:X\to\mathbb C$ such that $$\|f\|_1=\int_X |f|\,d\mu<\infty,$$ where functions equal almost everywhere are identified.
content.intuition: $L^1$ functions have finite total absolute mass. On a product space, that absolute-integrability condition is what makes Fubini's interchange of integrals valid.

assumptions: Functions equal almost everywhere represent the same element of $L^1$.
The norm is finite exactly when $|f|$ is integrable.

concepts.label: $L^2$ on $\mathbb R$

content.statement: Square-integrable functions on the real line.
content.definition: $$\begin{aligned} L^2(\mathbb R)&=\left\{f:\mathbb R\to\mathbb C\ \text{measurable}:\int_{\mathbb R}|f(x)|^2\,dx<\infty\right\}/\sim,\\ f\sim g&\Longleftrightarrow f=g\ \text{a.e.},\\ \langle f,g\rangle&=\int_{\mathbb R}f(x)\overline{g(x)}\,dx. \end{aligned}$$
content.formal: $L^2(\mathbb R)=\{f:\int_{\mathbb R}|f(x)|^2\,dx<\infty\}$, modulo equality almost everywhere, with inner product $\langle f,g\rangle=\int_{\mathbb R}f(x)\overline{g(x)}\,dx$.
content.intuition: This is the energy space for the Fourier transform. Plancherel says the transform preserves this energy exactly.

assumptions: Functions are identified up to equality almost everywhere.

concepts.label: $L^2$ Space

content.statement: Square-integrable functions on the circle, where Fourier series become Hilbert-space geometry.
content.definition: $$\begin{aligned} L^2(\mathbb T)&=\left\{f:\mathbb T\to\mathbb C\ \text{measurable}:\frac1{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^{\pi}|f(x)|^2\,dx<\infty\right\}/\sim,\\ f\sim g&\Longleftrightarrow f=g\ \text{a.e.},\\ \langle f,g\rangle&=\frac1{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^{\pi}f(x)\overline{g(x)}\,dx. \end{aligned}$$
content.formal: $L^2(\mathbb T)$ consists of measurable functions with $\|f\|_2^2=\frac1{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^{\pi}|f(x)|^2\,dx<\infty$, modulo equality a.e. Its inner product is $\langle f,g\rangle=\frac1{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^{\pi} f(x)\overline{g(x)}\,dx$.
content.intuition: In $L^2$, Fourier coefficients are coordinates, partial sums are orthogonal projections, and convergence means convergence in energy.

assumptions: Functions are identified up to equality almost everywhere.

concepts.label: $L^p$ Space

content.statement: Equivalence classes of measurable functions whose size is finite in an $L^p$ norm.
content.definition: $$\begin{aligned} L^p(\mathbb T)&=\left\{f:\mathbb T\to\mathbb C\ \text{measurable}:\ \|f\|_p<\infty\right\}/\sim,\qquad 1\le p\le\infty,\\ f\sim g&\Longleftrightarrow f=g\ \text{a.e.},\\ \|f\|_p&=\left(\frac1{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^{\pi}|f(x)|^p\,dx\right)^{1/p},\qquad 1\le p<\infty,\\ \|f\|_\infty&=\operatorname*{ess\,sup}_{x\in\mathbb T}|f(x)|. \end{aligned}$$
content.formal: For $1\le p<\infty$, $L^p(\mathbb T)=\{f:\mathbb T\to\mathbb C\text{ measurable}:\|f\|_p<\infty\}/\sim$, where $f\sim g$ iff $f=g$ a.e. and $\|f\|_p=(\frac1{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^{\pi}|f(x)|^p\,dx)^{1/p}$. For $p=\infty$, $\|f\|_\infty=\operatorname*{ess\,sup}_{x\in\mathbb T}|f(x)|$.
content.intuition: An $L^p$ element is a function as integration sees it: its average $p$-power size is finite, and changing values on a measure-zero set does not change the element. This is why Fourier coefficients, norms, and convergence results naturally live on equivalence classes rather than pointwise functions.

assumptions: Functions are identified up to equality almost everywhere.

content.statement: A property that fails only on a set of measure zero.
content.formal: A property $P(x)$ holds almost everywhere if $\mu(\{x:\neg P(x)\})=0$. In $L^p$ spaces, functions that agree almost everywhere represent the same element.
content.intuition: Almost everywhere means ignoring sets of measure zero. This is the natural equality notion for integration: if two functions differ only on a null set, their integrals, $L^p$ norms, and Fourier coefficients are unchanged.

content.statement: The circle used as the domain for $2\pi$-periodic Fourier analysis.
content.formal: $\mathbb T$ may be identified with $\mathbb R/2\pi\mathbb Z$, so $x$ and $y$ represent the same point iff $x-y\in2\pi\mathbb Z$.
content.intuition: The real line is wrapped into a circle by identifying $x$ with $x+2\pi$. A function on $\mathbb T$ is therefore the same thing as a $2\pi$-periodic function on $\mathbb R$.

content.statement: The pure harmonics $e^{inx}$ that serve as the basic waves in Fourier series.
content.formal: For $n\in\mathbb Z$, define $e_n(x)=e^{inx}$. Euler's formula gives $e^{inx}=\cos(nx)+i\sin(nx)$, and $e_n$ is $2\pi$-periodic.
content.intuition: Each $e_n$ is one pure frequency. Fourier analysis decomposes a signal by measuring how much it aligns with each of these rotating waves.

concepts.label: Continuous Functions on $\mathbb T$

content.statement: Continuous $2\pi$-periodic functions with the uniform norm.
content.formal: $C(\mathbb T)$ is the space of continuous complex-valued functions on $\mathbb T$, equivalently continuous $2\pi$-periodic functions on $\mathbb R$. Its norm is $\|f\|_\infty=\sup_{x\in\mathbb T}|f(x)|$.
content.intuition: This is the natural setting for uniform Fourier approximation. Compactness of $\mathbb T$ turns continuity into uniform continuity, which is exactly what good kernels exploit.

assumptions: The topology is the usual compact topology on $\mathbb T=\mathbb R/2\pi\mathbb Z$.

concepts.label: Convolution

content.statement: A sliding weighted average of one function against another.
content.formal: On $\mathbb T$, $(f*g)(x)=\frac1{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^{\pi}f(y)g(x-y)\,dy$. On $\mathbb R$, $(f*g)(x)=\int_{\mathbb R}f(y)g(x-y)\,dy$, whenever the integral is defined.
content.intuition: Convolution blends nearby values according to a kernel. Fourier analysis is powerful because it turns this averaging operation into multiplication.

assumptions: The normalization differs between the circle and the real line.

content.statement: A theorem allowing limits to pass through integrals under an integrable bound.
content.formal: If $f_n\to f$ almost everywhere and $|f_n|\le g$ for all $n$, with $g\in L^1$, then $f\in L^1$ and $\int f_n\to\int f$.
content.intuition: A pointwise limit is safe to integrate when all functions stay under one integrable roof.

content.statement: The set of all objects equivalent to a given object.
content.formal: Given an equivalence relation $\sim$ on $X$, the equivalence class of $x\in X$ is $[x]=\{y\in X:y\sim x\}$.
content.intuition: An equivalence class is the whole group represented by one object. In $L^p$, $[f]$ contains all functions equal to $f$ almost everywhere, even though people usually write $f$ instead of $[f]$.

content.statement: A rule that groups objects into classes of objects considered the same for a chosen purpose.
content.definition: $$\begin{aligned} x&\sim x,\\ x\sim y&\Longrightarrow y\sim x,\\ x\sim y\ \text{ and }\ y\sim z&\Longrightarrow x\sim z. \end{aligned}$$
content.formal: A relation $\sim$ on a set $X$ is an equivalence relation if it is reflexive, symmetric, and transitive.
content.intuition: An equivalence relation is controlled forgetting: it says which differences we ignore. Same remainder modulo $q$, same point modulo $2\pi$ on the circle, and equality almost everywhere in $L^p$ are all examples.

content.statement: The almost-everywhere version of a maximum size.
content.formal: For a measurable function $f$, $\operatorname*{ess\,sup}|f|$ is the least $M\in[0,\infty]$ such that $|f(x)|\le M$ for almost every $x$.
content.intuition: It ignores values on sets of measure zero, so it is the correct maximum-size notion for $L^\infty$.

concepts.label: Fubini's Theorem

content.statement: An absolutely integrable function on a product measure space may be integrated in either order.
content.formal:
Let $(X,\mathcal A,\mu)$ and $(Y,\mathcal B,
u)$ be $\sigma$-finite measure spaces, and let $f:X	imes Y	o\mathbb C$ be measurable. If $f\in L^1(X	imes Y,\mu	imes
u)$, then for almost every $x\in X$, $f(x,\cdot)\in L^1(Y)$; for almost every $y\in Y$, $f(\cdot,y)\in L^1(X)$; the functions $x\mapsto\int_Y f(x,y)\,d
u(y)$ and $y\mapsto\int_X f(x,y)\,d\mu(x)$ are integrable; and $$\int_{X	imes Y}f\,d(\mu	imes
u)=\int_X\left(\int_Y f(x,y)\,d
u(y)ight)d\mu(x)=\int_Y\left(\int_X f(x,y)\,d\mu(x)ight)d
u(y).$$
content.intuition: Fubini's theorem says that absolute integrability prevents hidden cancellation. If the total absolute mass is finite, then the integral can be computed by sweeping first in the $Y$-direction and then in the $X$-direction, or the other way around.

assumptions: The measure spaces $(X,\mathcal A,\mu)$ and $(Y,\mathcal B,\nu)$ are $\sigma$-finite.
The product space is equipped with the product measure $\mu\times\nu$ on $\mathcal A\otimes\mathcal B$.
The function $f$ is measurable on $X\times Y$.
The function is absolutely integrable: $\int_{X\times Y}|f|\,d(\mu\times\nu)<\infty$.
The inner integrals may fail to be defined on null sets; this does not affect the outer integrals.

steps.content: Let $(X,\mathcal A,\mu)$ and $(Y,\mathcal B,\nu)$ be $\sigma$-finite measure spaces. Then the product measure $\mu\times\nu$ is well-defined on $(X\times Y,\mathcal A\otimes\mathcal B)$.
Let $f:X\times Y\to\mathbb C$ be measurable and assume $$\int_{X\times Y}|f(x,y)|\,d(\mu\times\nu)(x,y)<\infty.$$ This is the essential Fubini hypothesis.

Since $|f|\ge0$ is measurable, Tonelli's theorem gives $$\int_X\int_Y |f(x,y)|\,d
u(y)\,d\mu(x)=\int_Y\int_X |f(x,y)|\,d\mu(x)\,d
u(y)=\int_{X	imes Y}|f|\,d(\mu	imes
u)<\infty.$$ Hence $f(x,\cdot)\in L^1(Y)$ for almost every $x$, and $f(\cdot,y)\in L^1(X)$ for almost every $y$.

For real-valued $f$, write $$f=f^+-f^-,$$ where $f^+,f^-\ge0$ and $|f|=f^++f^-$. Since $f\in L^1$, both $f^+$ and $f^-$ have finite integral. For complex-valued $f$, apply the same argument to $\operatorname{Re}f$ and $\operatorname{Im}f$.

By Tonelli's theorem applied to $f^+$ and $f^-$, $$\int_{X	imes Y}f^\pm\,d(\mu	imes
u)=\int_X\int_Y f^\pm(x,y)\,d
u(y)\,d\mu(x)=\int_Y\int_X f^\pm(x,y)\,d\mu(x)\,d
u(y).$$ Subtracting the two identities gives the result for real-valued $f$.
If $f$ is complex-valued, write $$f=\operatorname{Re}f+i\operatorname{Im}f.$$ Applying the real-valued result to $\operatorname{Re}f$ and $\operatorname{Im}f$ gives the identity for $f$.

Therefore the partial integral functions are integrable and $$\int_{X	imes Y}f\,d(\mu	imes
u)=\int_X\left(\int_Y f(x,y)\,d
u(y)ight)d\mu(x)=\int_Y\left(\int_X f(x,y)\,d\mu(x)ight)d
u(y).$$ Thus the order of integration may be exchanged for $L^1$ functions.

source.citation: Folland: Real Analysis, Ch.2 Fubini-Tonelli Theorem; Stein–Shakarchi: Real Analysis, Ch.2
source.ref: Folland: Real Analysis, Ch.2 Fubini-Tonelli Theorem; Stein–Shakarchi: Real Analysis, Ch.2; Wikipedia: Fubini's theorem

concepts.label: Fubini–Tonelli Theorem

content.statement: The Fubini-Tonelli package gives the two main hypotheses under which product integrals can be computed by iterated integrals: nonnegativity for Tonelli, absolute integrability for Fubini.
content.formal: Let $(X,\mathcal A,\mu)$ and $(Y,\mathcal B,\nu)$ be $\sigma$-finite measure spaces and let $f$ be measurable on $X\times Y$. If $f\ge0$, Tonelli gives equality of the product integral and both iterated integrals in $[0,\infty]$. If $f\in L^1(X\times Y,\mu\times\nu)$, Fubini gives equality as finite integrals.
content.intuition: Convolution, Fourier inversion, and many kernel identities require moving integrals past each other. Tonelli says nonnegative mass can be summed in any order; Fubini says signed or complex mass can be reordered once absolute integrability prevents cancellation pathologies.

assumptions: Tonelli handles nonnegative measurable functions and may return $+\infty$.
Fubini handles absolutely integrable functions and returns finite iterated integrals.
The standard product-measure formulation assumes $\sigma$-finite measure spaces.

steps.content: For $f\ge0$, apply Tonelli's theorem to identify the product integral with both iterated integrals, allowing the common value to be $+\infty$.
For signed or complex $f\in L^1(X\times Y)$, apply Fubini's theorem. Absolute integrability ensures the sections are integrable almost everywhere and the iterated integrals are finite.
Thus changing the order of integration is justified either by nonnegativity via Tonelli or by absolute integrability via Fubini; without one of these hypotheses, iterated integrals can disagree.

source.citation: Folland: Real Analysis, Ch.2 Fubini-Tonelli Theorem; Stein-Shakarchi: Real Analysis, Ch.2
source.ref: Folland: Real Analysis, Ch.2 Fubini-Tonelli Theorem; Stein-Shakarchi: Real Analysis, Ch.2

content.statement: A complete inner-product space where orthogonal projection and infinite expansions behave well.
content.definition: $$\begin{aligned} \|x\|&=\sqrt{\langle x,x\rangle},\\ \left(\forall\varepsilon>0\ \exists N\ \forall m,n\ge N:\ \|x_m-x_n\|<\varepsilon\right)&\Longrightarrow \exists x\in H:\ \|x_n-x\|\to0. \end{aligned}$$
content.formal: A Hilbert space is an inner product space that is complete in the norm $\|x\|=\sqrt{\langle x,x\rangle}$. Central examples are $L^2(\mathbb T)$, $L^2(\mathbb R)$, and $\ell^2(\mathbb Z)$.
content.intuition: Completeness means Cauchy sequences of approximations have a limit inside the space, so infinite orthogonal expansions have somewhere to land.

concepts.label: Hölder and Lipschitz Continuity

content.statement: A quantitative continuity condition controlling how fast function values can change.
content.formal: A function $f$ is Hölder continuous of order $0<\alpha\le 1$ if there is $C>0$ such that $|f(x)-f(y)|\le C|x-y|^\alpha$ for all $x,y$. The case $\alpha=1$ is the Lipschitz condition.
content.intuition: Hölder regularity turns smoothness into a usable numerical bound. In Fourier series, this bound directly controls coefficient decay and, for large enough exponent, absolute convergence.

assumptions: The displayed condition is metric-dependent; in the periodic setting distances are taken on $\mathbb T$ or on a chosen period interval with periodic extension.

concepts.label: Inner Product Space

content.statement: A vector space with a notion of length, angle, and orthogonality.
content.definition: $$\begin{aligned} \langle ax+by,z\rangle&=a\langle x,z\rangle+b\langle y,z\rangle,\\ \langle x,ay+bz\rangle&=\overline a\langle x,y\rangle+\overline b\langle x,z\rangle,\\ \langle x,y\rangle&=\overline{\langle y,x\rangle},\\ \langle x,x\rangle&\ge0,\\ \langle x,x\rangle=0&\Longleftrightarrow x=0. \end{aligned}$$
content.formal: A complex inner product space is a complex vector space $V$ with $\langle\cdot,\cdot\rangle:V\times V\to\mathbb C$ that is linear in the first variable, conjugate-linear in the second, conjugate-symmetric, and positive-definite. On $L^2(\mathbb T)$, $\langle f,g\rangle=\frac1{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^{\pi}f(x)\overline{g(x)}\,dx$.
content.intuition: This is the geometry behind Fourier coefficients: coefficients are projections, and different frequencies are perpendicular directions.

assumptions: Convention used here: inner products are linear in the first variable and conjugate-linear in the second.

concepts.label: Integer Frequency

content.statement: The integer index $n\in\mathbb Z$ that labels a pure harmonic on the $2\pi$-periodic circle.
content.formal: A function $x\mapsto e^{i\lambda x}$ descends to a well-defined function on $\mathbb T=\mathbb R/2\pi\mathbb Z$ exactly when $e^{i\lambda(x+2\pi)}=e^{i\lambda x}$ for all $x$, equivalently $\lambda\in\mathbb Z$.
content.intuition: Wrapping the line into a circle quantizes frequency. Only integer numbers of oscillations fit consistently around a $2\pi$ loop.

assumptions: This node uses the $2\pi$-periodic convention; period-$1$ conventions use $e^{2\pi inx}$ instead.

concepts.label: Lebesgue Integral

content.statement: The Lebesgue integral integrates measurable functions with respect to a measure.
content.definition: The Lebesgue integral is the measure-theoretic integral associated to a measure space.
content.formal: Let $(X,\mathcal A,\mu)$ be a measure space. The Lebesgue integral is first defined for nonnegative measurable functions by approximation from below by nonnegative simple functions, and then extended to integrable real or complex functions by decomposing into positive and negative, or real and imaginary, parts.
content.intuition: The Lebesgue integral measures the size of the values of a function by grouping according to measurable level sets rather than by slicing only along the input axis.

assumptions: The function must be measurable.
For signed or complex functions, integrability requires finite integral of the absolute value.
Lebesgue integrals respect equality almost everywhere.

concepts.label: Measurable Function

content.statement: A measurable function is a function whose preimages of measurable sets are measurable.
content.definition: A measurable function preserves measurable structure by preimage.
content.formal: Let $(X,\mathcal A)$ and $(Y,\mathcal B)$ be measurable spaces. A function $f:X\to Y$ is measurable if for every $B\in\mathcal B$, one has $f^{-1}(B)\in\mathcal A$.
content.intuition: Measurable functions are to measure spaces what continuous functions are to topological spaces: the relevant structure is preserved by preimages.

assumptions: Measurability is defined using preimages, not images.
For real-valued functions, it is often enough to check sets of the form $(-\infty,a)$ or $(a,\infty)$.

concepts.label: Measure

content.statement: A measure assigns nonnegative sizes to measurable sets in a countably additive way.
content.definition: A measure is a countably additive size function on a sigma-algebra.
content.formal: Let $(X,\mathcal A)$ be a measurable space. A measure is a function $\mu:\mathcal A\to[0,\infty]$ such that $\mu(\varnothing)=0$ and, for every pairwise disjoint sequence $(A_n)_{n\ge1}\subseteq\mathcal A$, $$\mu\left(\bigcup_{n=1}^{\infty}A_n\right)=\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\mu(A_n).$$
content.intuition: A measure generalizes length, area, volume, mass, probability, and counting.

assumptions: The measure is defined only on measurable sets $A\in\mathcal A$.
The value $+\infty$ is allowed.
Countable additivity is required for pairwise disjoint measurable sets.

concepts.label: Measure Space

content.statement: A measure space is a set equipped with a sigma-algebra of measurable subsets and a measure assigning sizes to those subsets.
content.definition: A measure space is a triple $(X,\mathcal A,\mu)$ such that: $X$ is a set; $\mathcal A$ is a $\sigma$-algebra on $X$; and $\mu$ is a countably additive map from $\mathcal A$ to $[0,\infty]$ satisfying $\mu(\varnothing)=0$.
content.formal: A measure space is a triple $(X,\mathcal A,\mu)$ where $X$ is a set, $\mathcal A\subseteq\mathcal P(X)$ is a $\sigma$-algebra, and $\mu:\mathcal A\to[0,\infty]$ is a measure.
content.formula: $$\begin{aligned} (X,\mathcal A,\mu)&=\text{measure space},\\ \mathcal A&\subseteq\mathcal P(X),\\ \varnothing,X&\in\mathcal A,\\ A\in\mathcal A&\Longrightarrow X\setminus A\in\mathcal A,\\ A_n\in\mathcal A&\Longrightarrow \bigcup_{n=1}^{\infty}A_n\in\mathcal A,\\ \mu:\mathcal A&\to[0,\infty],\\ \mu\left(\bigcup_{n=1}^{\infty}A_n\right)&=\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\mu(A_n)\quad\text{for pairwise disjoint }A_n. \end{aligned}$$
content.intuition: A measure space is the setting where integration can be done. The set $X$ is the universe, the sigma-algebra $\mathcal A$ specifies which subsets are measurable, and the measure $\mu$ assigns a size, mass, probability, length, area, or volume to each measurable set.

assumptions: The measure is only required to be defined on the sigma-algebra $\mathcal A$, not on all subsets of $X$.
The measure takes values in $[0,\infty]$, so infinite measure is allowed.
Countable additivity is required only for pairwise disjoint measurable sets.
Some authors write $(X,\Sigma,\mu)$ instead of $(X,\mathcal A,\mu)$.

source.citation: Folland: Real Analysis, Ch.1; Stein–Shakarchi: Real Analysis, Ch.1; Axler: Measure, Integration & Real Analysis, Ch.2
source.ref: Folland: Real Analysis, Ch.1; Stein–Shakarchi: Real Analysis, Ch.1; Axler: Measure, Integration & Real Analysis, Ch.2

concepts.label: Moderate Decrease Class $\mathcal M(\mathbb R)$

content.statement: Continuous functions on $\mathbb R$ bounded by a constant multiple of $\frac{1}{1+x^2}$; Stein--Shakarchi's basic decay class for defining improper integrals and Fourier transforms on the real line.
content.definition: $$\mathcal M(\mathbb R)=\left\{f\in C(\mathbb R):\exists A>0\ \text{s.t.}\ |f(x)|\le \frac{A}{1+x^2}\ \forall x\in\mathbb R\right\}.$$
content.formal: A continuous function $f:\mathbb R\to\mathbb C$ has \textbf{moderate decrease} if there exists $A>0$ such that $|f(x)|\le \dfrac{A}{1+x^2}$ for every $x\in\mathbb R$. The class is denoted $\mathcal M(\mathbb R)$. In course notation, this is the same decay class as $C_2(\mathbb R)$ when $C_2$ is defined using the bound $(1+|x|)^{-2}$.
content.intuition: Moderate decrease is a concrete tail condition: the function may live on all of $\mathbb R$, but far from the origin its size is dominated by a multiple of $1/x^2$. This is strong enough to make the improper integral and the Fourier transform integral well-defined in Stein--Shakarchi's elementary setup, while still allowing many non-smooth functions. The point is not smoothness; the point is that the tails are small enough not to leak infinite mass.
notation: $$\mathcal M(\mathbb R),\qquad C_2(\mathbb R)\ \text{(equivalent course notation)}$$

assumptions: $$\mathcal M(\mathbb R)\subset L^1(\mathbb R)\cap L^2(\mathbb R),\qquad (1+x^2)^{-1}\in L^1(\mathbb R)\cap L^2(\mathbb R).$$
$$1+x^2\le (1+|x|)^2\le 2(1+x^2)\quad\Longrightarrow\quad \mathcal M(\mathbb R)=C_2(\mathbb R)\ \text{as sets, up to equivalent constants}.$$

concepts.label: Normalized Circle Measure

content.statement: The probability normalization $\frac1{2\pi}dx$ used for integrals on the $2\pi$-periodic circle.
content.formal: On $\mathbb T=\mathbb R/2\pi\mathbb Z$, Fourier series in this graph use the normalized integral $$\int_{\mathbb T}f\,dm=\frac1{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^{\pi}f(x)\,dx,$$ so $m(\mathbb T)=1$.
content.intuition: This convention removes stray factors of $2\pi$: constants have average value equal to their coefficient, and the waves $e^{inx}$ have norm $1$.

assumptions: All circle formulas in this map use the $2\pi$-periodic normalization unless a node explicitly says otherwise.

content.statement: A function that differs from zero at one point represents the same $L^p$ element as the zero function.
content.formal: Let $f=0$ and $g=\mathbf 1_{\{0\}}$ on $\mathbb T$. Then $f\ne g$ pointwise, but $f=g$ a.e., so $[f]=[g]$ in $L^p(\mathbb T)$.
content.intuition: A single-point change is invisible to integration, so $L^p$ treats the spike and the zero function as the same object.

concepts.label: Orthogonality of Exponentials

content.statement: Different integer harmonics on the circle are perpendicular in $L^2(\mathbb T)$.
content.formal: With normalized inner product $\langle f,g\rangle=\frac1{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^{\pi}f(x)\overline{g(x)}\,dx$, the exponentials $e_n(x)=e^{inx}$ satisfy $\langle e_n,e_m\rangle=\delta_{nm}$ for all $m,n\in\mathbb Z$.
content.intuition: A full number of nonzero oscillations averages to zero around the circle. This single cancellation fact is what makes Fourier coefficients independent coordinates.

assumptions: The inner product is normalized by $\frac1{2\pi}$ on one full period.

content.statement: A family of unit vectors that are mutually orthogonal.
content.formal: A family $\{\varphi_k\}_{k\in I}$ in an inner product space is orthonormal if $\langle\varphi_j,\varphi_k\rangle=\delta_{jk}$ for all $j,k\in I$. In $L^2(\mathbb T)$, $\{e^{inx}\}_{n\in\mathbb Z}$ is orthonormal.
content.intuition: An orthonormal system gives independent directions. Fourier coefficients are coordinates along those directions.

concepts.label: Periodic Function

content.statement: A function whose values repeat after a fixed nonzero period; $2\pi$-periodic functions are functions on the circle.
content.definition: $$\begin{aligned} f:\mathbb R\to\mathbb C,\quad T>0,\quad f(x+T)&=f(x)\quad(\forall x\in\mathbb R),\\ \mathbb T&=\mathbb R/2\pi\mathbb Z. \end{aligned}$$
content.formal: A function $f:\mathbb R\to\mathbb C$ is periodic with period $T>0$ if $f(x+T)=f(x)$ for every $x\in\mathbb R$. In Fourier series, the standard convention is $T=2\pi$, so functions are viewed on $\mathbb T=\mathbb R/2\pi\mathbb Z$.
content.intuition: A periodic function lives on a loop: moving by one full period returns to the same point on $\mathbb T$ and therefore to the same value.

assumptions: Periods are taken positive; a $2\pi$-periodic function may be identified with a function on the circle.

concepts.label: Polynomial Decay Class $C_m(\mathbb R^d)$

content.statement: For $m>0$, the class $C_m(\mathbb R^d)$ consists of continuous functions whose magnitude is bounded by a constant multiple of $(1+|x|)^{-m}$.
content.definition: $$C_m(\mathbb R^d)=\left\{f\in C(\mathbb R^d):\exists A>0\ \text{s.t.}\ |f(x)|\le \frac{A}{(1+|x|)^m}\ \forall x\in\mathbb R^d\right\}.$$
content.formal: Let $d\ge 1$ and $m>0$. The polynomial decay class $C_m(\mathbb R^d)$ consists of continuous functions $f:\mathbb R^d\to\mathbb C$ such that there exists $A>0$ with $|f(x)|\le A(1+|x|)^{-m}$ for every $x\in\mathbb R^d$.
content.intuition: The class $C_m(\mathbb R^d)$ measures polynomial tail decay. Larger $m$ means the function must fade faster at infinity. The class is useful because it gives quick thresholds for integrability: in $\mathbb R^d$, decay faster than $|x|^{-d}$ gives $L^1$ control, and decay faster than $|x|^{-d/2}$ gives $L^2$ control.

assumptions: $$m_2\ge m_1>0\quad\Longrightarrow\quad C_{m_2}(\mathbb R^d)\subset C_{m_1}(\mathbb R^d).$$
$$m>0\quad\Longrightarrow\quad C_m(\mathbb R^d)\subset C_0(\mathbb R^d),\quad \text{where }C_0(\mathbb R^d)\text{ denotes continuous functions vanishing at infinity}.$$
$$m>d\quad\Longrightarrow\quad C_m(\mathbb R^d)\subset L^1(\mathbb R^d).$$
$$2m>d\quad\Longrightarrow\quad C_m(\mathbb R^d)\subset L^2(\mathbb R^d).$$
$$\mathcal M(\mathbb R)=C_2(\mathbb R)\ \text{when }C_2(\mathbb R)\text{ is defined using }(1+|x|)^{-2}\text{ decay}.$$

source.citation: Course notation; Stein--Shakarchi Ch.5 §1.1 for the corresponding $d=1$, $m=2$ moderate decrease class
source.ref: Course notation for polynomial decay classes; compare Stein--Shakarchi Ch.5 §1.1 functions of moderate decrease.

concepts.label: Product Measure

content.statement: A product measure is the natural measure on a Cartesian product, determined by products of measurable rectangles.
content.definition: The product measure measures rectangles by multiplying their side measures and extends this rule to the product sigma-algebra.
content.formal:
Let $(X,\mathcal A,\mu)$ and $(Y,\mathcal B,
u)$ be $\sigma$-finite measure spaces. The product measure $\mu	imes
u$ is the unique measure on $(X	imes Y,\mathcal A\otimes\mathcal B)$ satisfying $$(\mu	imes
u)(A	imes B)=\mu(A)
u(B)$$ for all $A\in\mathcal A$ and $B\in\mathcal B$.
content.intuition: Product measure is the measure-theoretic version of area equals length times width.

assumptions: The product sigma-algebra $\mathcal A\otimes\mathcal B$ is generated by measurable rectangles $A\times B$.
Sigma-finiteness gives uniqueness of the standard product measure.
The product of complete measure spaces need not be complete.

source.ref: Wikipedia: Fubini's theorem, Product measures section

content.statement: The set obtained by replacing elements of a set by their equivalence classes.
content.formal: If $\sim$ is an equivalence relation on $X$, then $X/\sim=\{[x]:x\in X\}$.
content.intuition: A quotient set is a grouped version of $X$: objects connected by $\sim$ are treated as one object. In $L^p$, this groups functions that agree almost everywhere.

concepts.label: Schwartz Functions Are Dense

content.statement: Schwartz functions approximate $L^p$ functions on $\mathbb R$ in norm.
content.formal: For $1\le p<\infty$, $\mathcal S(\mathbb R)$ is dense in $L^p(\mathbb R)$. In particular, every $f\in L^2(\mathbb R)$ has $f_k\in\mathcal S(\mathbb R)$ with $\|f_k-f\|_2\to0$.
content.intuition: Many Fourier-transform proofs are first done for rapidly decreasing smooth functions. Density is the bridge that lets those identities extend to rougher $L^p$ data by approximation.

assumptions: The statement is for finite $p$; $\mathcal S(\mathbb R)$ is not dense in $L^\infty$ in the essential-supremum norm.

concepts.label: Schwartz Space

content.statement: Smooth functions whose derivatives decay faster than every polynomial.
content.definition: $$\mathcal S(\mathbb R)=\left\{f\in C^\infty(\mathbb R):\sup_{x\in\mathbb R}|x^\alpha f^{(\beta)}(x)|<\infty\quad(\forall \alpha,\beta\in\mathbb N_0)\right\}.$$
content.formal: The Schwartz space $\mathcal S(\mathbb R)$ consists of all $f\in C^\infty(\mathbb R)$ such that $\sup_{x\in\mathbb R}|x^\alpha f^{(\beta)}(x)|<\infty$ for all nonnegative integers $\alpha,\beta$.
content.intuition: Schwartz functions are the safest playground for the Fourier transform: decay, smoothness, differentiation, and inversion all work cleanly.

assumptions: This one-dimensional definition extends to $\mathbb R^d$ using multi-indices.

source.citation: Stein–Shakarchi: Ch.5 Corollary 2.4; Ch.5 Proposition 1.1; Ch.5 Theorem 1.3
source.ref: Stein–Shakarchi: Ch.5 Corollary 2.4; Ch.5 Proposition 1.1; Ch.5 Theorem 1.3

concepts.label: Sigma-Algebra

content.statement: A sigma-algebra is a collection of subsets closed under complements and countable unions.
content.definition: A $\sigma$-algebra specifies which subsets of $X$ are measurable.
content.formal: Let $X$ be a set. A $\sigma$-algebra on $X$ is a collection $\mathcal A\subseteq\mathcal P(X)$ such that $X\in\mathcal A$, if $A\in\mathcal A$ then $X\setminus A\in\mathcal A$, and if $(A_n)_{n\ge1}\subseteq\mathcal A$, then $\bigcup_{n=1}^{\infty}A_n\in\mathcal A$.
content.formula: $$X\in\mathcal A,\quad A\in\mathcal A\Rightarrow X\setminus A\in\mathcal A,\quad A_n\in\mathcal A\Rightarrow \bigcup_{n=1}^{\infty}A_n\in\mathcal A.$$
content.intuition: A sigma-algebra is the allowed collection of sets on which size, probability, or volume can be consistently defined.

assumptions: The underlying object $X$ is a set.
Closure under countable intersections follows from complements and countable unions by De Morgan's laws.

concepts.label: Sigma-Finite Measure

content.statement: A measure is sigma-finite if the whole space can be covered by countably many measurable sets of finite measure.
content.definition: Sigma-finiteness means the space may be decomposed into countably many finite-measure pieces.
content.formal: A measure space $(X,\mathcal A,\mu)$ is $\sigma$-finite if there exist sets $A_1,A_2,\ldots\in\mathcal A$ such that $X=\bigcup_{n=1}^{\infty}A_n$ and $\mu(A_n)<\infty$ for every $n$.
content.intuition: Sigma-finiteness rules out measure spaces that are too large to build product measures and iterated integrals in the standard way.

assumptions: Every finite measure is sigma-finite.
Lebesgue measure on $\mathbb R^n$ is sigma-finite.
Counting measure on an uncountable set is not sigma-finite.

concepts.label: SS 2.7.1 — Integrable functions with dense discontinuities

content.statement: Build Riemann-integrable functions with a dense set of discontinuities.
content.formal: Construct Riemann integrable functions on $[0,1]$ with dense sets of discontinuities as follows. (a) Let $f(x)=0$ for $x<0$ and $f(x)=1$ for $x\ge0$. Choose a countable dense sequence $\{r_n\}$ in $[0,1]$. Show that $$F(x)=\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\frac1{n^2}f(x-r_n)$$ is integrable and has discontinuities at all points of $\{r_n\}$. Hint: $F$ is monotone and bounded. (b) Let $$F(x)=\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}3^{-n}g(x-r_n),\qquad g(x)=\begin{cases}\sin(1/x),&x\ne0,\\0,&x=0.\end{cases}$$ Show that $F$ is integrable, discontinuous at every $x=r_n$, and not monotone on any subinterval of $[0,1]$. Hint: use $3^{-k}>\sum_{n>k}3^{-n}$. (c) Riemann's original example is $$F(x)=\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\frac{(nx)}{n^2},$$ where $(x)$ equals $x$ on $(-1/2,1/2]$ and is extended periodically by $(x+1)=(x)$. It can be shown that $F$ is discontinuous whenever $x=m/(2n)$, with $m,n\in\mathbb Z$, $m$ odd, and $n\ne0$.
content.intuition: Riemann integrability tolerates a dense (but measure-zero) discontinuity set; geometric weights $3^{-n}$ keep each oscillation from being drowned out by the tail.

steps.content: Each summand $\frac1{n^2}f(x-r_n)$ is a nondecreasing step of height $\frac1{n^2}$ at $x=r_n$. The series converges uniformly (Weierstrass $M$-test, $\sum n^{-2}<\infty$) to a bounded, \emph{nondecreasing} $F$. A monotone bounded function on $[0,1]$ has at most countably many discontinuities and is Riemann integrable. At $x=r_m$, $F$ jumps: $F(r_m^+)-F(r_m^-)\ge\frac1{m^2}>0$ (the $m$-th term jumps there; the others, being continuous from one side appropriately, do not remove it). Hence $F$ is discontinuous exactly on the dense set $\{r_n\}$ yet still integrable.

Now $g(x)=\sin(1/x)$ is bounded ($|g|\le1$) and continuous except at $0$, hence Riemann integrable on any interval; the series $\sum_n3^{-n}g(x-r_n)$ converges uniformly, so $F$ is bounded and integrable. \emph{Discontinuity at $r_m$:} as $x\to r_m$, the $m$-th term $3^{-m}\sin\big(1/(x-r_m)\big)$ oscillates with amplitude $3^{-m}$, while the remaining terms vary by at most $\sum_{n\neq m}3^{-n}|{\Delta g}|$; using $3^{-m}>\sum_{n>m}3^{-n}=\tfrac12 3^{-m}$ and continuity of the other terms near $r_m$, the oscillation of the $m$-th term is not cancelled, so $\limsup_{x\to r_m}F-\liminf_{x\to r_m}F>0$: a genuine discontinuity. \emph{No monotonicity:} every subinterval contains some $r_m$ (density), near which the $m$-th term oscillates up and down with un-cancelled amplitude, so $F$ cannot be monotone there.

concepts.label: SS 3.3.1 — $\mathbb R^d$ and $\mathbb C^d$ are complete

content.statement: Completeness of the basic finite-dimensional inner product spaces.
content.formal: Show that the first two examples of inner product spaces, namely $\mathbb R^d$ and $\mathbb C^d$, are complete. Hint: every Cauchy sequence in $\mathbb R$ has a limit.
content.intuition: Finite-dimensional completeness is just coordinate-wise completeness of $\mathbb R$ or $\mathbb C$ — a finite sum of limits.

steps.content:
Let $(v_k)$ be a Cauchy sequence in $\mathbb C^d$ (the case $\mathbb R^d$ is identical), $v_k=(v_k^{(1)},\dots,v_k^{(d)})$. For each coordinate $j$,
$$|v_k^{(j)}-v_\ell^{(j)}|\le\Big(\sum_{i=1}^d|v_k^{(i)}-v_\ell^{(i)}|^2\Big)^{1/2}=\|v_k-v_\ell\|,$$
so $(v_k^{(j)})_k$ is Cauchy in $\mathbb C$, which is complete; let $w^{(j)}=\lim_k v_k^{(j)}$ and $w=(w^{(1)},\dots,w^{(d)})$. Then
$$\|v_k-w\|^2=\sum_{j=1}^d|v_k^{(j)}-w^{(j)}|^2\to0,$$
a finite sum of null sequences. Hence $v_k\to w\in\mathbb C^d$: every Cauchy sequence converges, so the space is complete.

content.statement: Completeness of the sequence space $\ell^2(\mathbb Z)$.
content.formal: Prove that the vector space $\ell^2(\mathbb Z)$ is complete. Hint: if $A_k=\{a_{k,n}\}_{n\in\mathbb Z}$ is Cauchy, show that for each fixed $n$, the scalar sequence $\{a_{k,n}\}_{k=1}^{\infty}$ is Cauchy and hence converges to some $b_n$. Let $B=(\dots,b_{-1},b_0,b_1,\dots)$. By taking finite partial sums of $\|A_k-A_{k'}\|^2$ and letting $k'\to\infty$, show that $\|A_k-B\|\to0$, and finally prove that $B\in\ell^2(\mathbb Z)$.
content.intuition: The classic Hilbert-space completeness recipe: extract the coordinatewise limit, then upgrade to norm convergence on finite windows and let the window grow.

steps.content: Let $A_k=(a_{k,n})_{n\in\mathbb Z}$ be a Cauchy sequence in $\ell^2(\mathbb Z)$.

For each fixed $n\in\mathbb Z$, we have $$|a_{k,n}-a_{\ell,n}|\le \|A_k-A_\ell\|_{\ell^2}.$$ Since $(A_k)$ is Cauchy in $\ell^2(\mathbb Z)$, it follows that $(a_{k,n})_{k\in\mathbb N}$ is Cauchy in $\mathbb C$. Since $\mathbb C$ is complete, the limit $$b_n:=\lim_{k\to\infty}a_{k,n}$$ exists for every $n\in\mathbb Z$. Define $$B=(b_n)_{n\in\mathbb Z}.$$

Let $\varepsilon>0$. Since $(A_k)$ is Cauchy, there exists $K\in\mathbb N$ such that $$\|A_k-A_\ell\|_{\ell^2}<\varepsilon \qquad \text{for all } k,\ell\ge K.$$ Fix $k\ge K$. For every $M\in\mathbb N$ and every $\ell\ge K$, $$\sum_{|n|\le M}|a_{k,n}-a_{\ell,n}|^2\le \|A_k-A_\ell\|_{\ell^2}^2<\varepsilon^2.$$ Since the sum over $|n|\le M$ is finite, we may pass to the limit as $\ell\to\infty$ inside the sum. Hence $$\sum_{|n|\le M}|a_{k,n}-b_n|^2\le \varepsilon^2.$$ Since this holds for every $M$, taking $M\to\infty$ gives $$\sum_{n\in\mathbb Z}|a_{k,n}-b_n|^2\le \varepsilon^2.$$ Therefore $A_k-B\in\ell^2(\mathbb Z)$ and $$\|A_k-B\|_{\ell^2}\le \varepsilon \qquad \text{for all } k\ge K.$$ Hence $A_k\to B$ in $\ell^2(\mathbb Z)$.

Since $A_K\in\ell^2(\mathbb Z)$ and $B-A_K\in\ell^2(\mathbb Z)$, we have $$\|B\|_{\ell^2}\le \|B-A_K\|_{\ell^2}+\|A_K\|_{\ell^2}<\infty.$$ Hence $B\in\ell^2(\mathbb Z)$. Therefore every Cauchy sequence in $\ell^2(\mathbb Z)$ converges in $\ell^2(\mathbb Z)$, so $\ell^2(\mathbb Z)$ is complete.

concepts.label: SS 3.3.4 — The $L^2$-norm is only a seminorm on $\mathbb R$

content.statement: Why the integral $L^2$-norm is only a seminorm on Riemann-integrable functions.
content.formal: Recall the vector space $\mathcal R$ of integrable functions with norm $$\|f\|=\left(\frac1{2\pi}\int_0^{2\pi}|f(x)|^2\,dx\right)^{1/2}.$$ (a) Show that there exist nonzero integrable functions $f$ for which $\|f\|=0$. (b) Show that if $f\in\mathcal R$ and $\|f\|=0$, then $f(x)=0$ whenever $f$ is continuous at $x$. (c) Conversely, show that if $f\in\mathcal R$ vanishes at all of its points of continuity, then $\|f\|=0$.
content.intuition: Integration cannot see a measure-zero set: $\|f\|=0$ pins down $f$ only up to its continuity points, which is why $L^2$ is built from equivalence classes.

steps.content: Let $f(x_0)=1$ at a single point and $f\equiv0$ elsewhere. Then $f\neq0$ but $\int|f|^2=0$, so $\|f\|=0$.

Suppose $\|f\|=0$ and $f$ is continuous at $x$ with $f(x)\neq0$. By continuity $|f|\ge\frac12|f(x)|>0$ on some interval $(x-\delta,x+\delta)$, so $\frac1{2\pi}\int|f|^2\ge\frac1{2\pi}\cdot 2\delta\cdot\frac14|f(x)|^2>0$, contradicting $\|f\|=0$. Hence $f(x)=0$.

A Riemann integrable function is continuous except on a set of measure zero. If $f$ vanishes at every continuity point, then $f=0$ off a measure-zero set, so $|f|^2=0$ off a measure-zero set; a (bounded) Riemann integrable function that is $0$ except on a measure-zero set has $\int|f|^2=0$, i.e. $\|f\|=0$.
So $\|\cdot\|$ is only a \emph{seminorm} on $\mathcal R$; passing to equivalence classes (equality at continuity points) makes it a norm — the origin of $L^2$.

concepts.label: SS 3.4.2 — Legendre polynomials: an orthogonal basis

content.statement: Legendre polynomials as an orthogonal (complete) basis of $L^2[-1,1]$.
content.formal:
An important fact proved in Chapter 3 is that the family $\{e^{inx}\}_{n\in\mathbb Z}$ is orthonormal in $\mathcal R$ and complete in the sense that the Fourier series of $f$ converges to $f$ in the norm. In this problem, consider another family with the same properties. On $[-1,1]$, define
$$L_n(x)= rac{d^n}{dx^n}(x^2-1)^n,\qquad n=0,1,2,\dots.$$
Then $L_n$ is a polynomial of degree $n$, called the $n$th Legendre polynomial.

	extbf{(a)} Show that if $f$ is indefinitely differentiable on $[-1,1]$, then
$$\int_{-1}^{1}L_n(x)f(x)\,dx=(-1)^n\int_{-1}^{1}(x^2-1)^n f^{(n)}(x)\,dx.$$
In particular, show that $L_n$ is orthogonal to $x^m$ whenever $m<n$. Hence $\{L_n\}_{n=0}^{\infty}$ is an orthogonal family.

	extbf{(b)} Show that
$$\|L_n\|^2=\int_{-1}^{1}|L_n(x)|^2\,dx=\frac{(n!)^2 2^{2n+1}}{2n+1}.$$
Hint: First note that
$$\|L_n\|^2=(-1)^n(2n)!\int_{-1}^{1}(x^2-1)^n\,dx.$$
Write $(x^2-1)^n=(x-1)^n(x+1)^n$ and integrate by parts $n$ times to calculate this last integral.

	extbf{(c)} Prove that any polynomial of degree $n$ that is orthogonal to $1,x,x^2,\dots,x^{n-1}$ is a constant multiple of $L_n$.

	extbf{(d)} Let $\widetilde L_n=L_n/\|L_n\|$ be the normalized Legendre polynomials. Prove that $\{\widetilde L_n\}$ is the family obtained by applying the Gram--Schmidt process to $\{1,x,\dots,x^n,\dots\}$, and conclude that every Riemann integrable function $f$ on $[-1,1]$ has a Legendre expansion
$$\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\langle f,\widetilde L_n\rangle \widetilde L_n$$
which converges to $f$ in the mean-square sense.
content.intuition: Rodrigues' formula makes orthogonality fall out of repeated integration by parts; the polynomials give a complete orthonormal system on an interval, paralleling $\{e^{inx}\}$ on the circle.

steps.content:
Integrate by parts $n$ times. The boundary terms involve $\frac{d^j}{dx^j}(x^2-1)^n$ for $j\le n-1$, each of which has the factor $(x^2-1)$ and so vanishes at $x=\pm1$. Hence
$$\int_{-1}^1 L_n(x)f(x)\,dx=\int_{-1}^1\Big(\tfrac{d^n}{dx^n}(x^2-1)^n\Big)f\,dx=(-1)^n\int_{-1}^1(x^2-1)^n f^{(n)}(x)\,dx.$$
Taking $f(x)=x^m$ with $m<n$ gives $f^{(n)}\equiv0$, so $\int_{-1}^1 L_n x^m=0$. Since every polynomial of degree $<n$ is a combination of such $x^m$, and $L_m$ has degree $m$, we get $\langle L_n,L_m\rangle=0$ for $m\neq n$: the family is orthogonal.

With $f=L_n$ (degree $n$, leading coefficient $\frac{(2n)!}{n!}$), its $n$-th derivative is the constant $L_n^{(n)}=(2n)!$. So
$$\|L_n\|^2=(-1)^n\int_{-1}^1(x^2-1)^n L_n^{(n)}\,dx=(2n)!\int_{-1}^1(1-x^2)^n\,dx.$$
Using $\int_{-1}^1(1-x^2)^n dx=\frac{2^{2n+1}(n!)^2}{(2n+1)!}$ (Beta integral), and $\frac{(2n)!}{(2n+1)!}=\frac1{2n+1}$,
$$\|L_n\|^2=(2n)!\cdot\frac{2^{2n+1}(n!)^2}{(2n+1)!}=\frac{(n!)^2\,2^{2n+1}}{2n+1}.$$
Normalising, $\{\sqrt{\tfrac{2n+1}{2}}\,\frac{L_n}{2^n n!}\}$ is an orthonormal basis of $L^2[-1,1]$ — a non-trigonometric complete orthonormal system.

content.statement: Convergence, the functional equation, and special values of $\Gamma$.
content.formal: The gamma function is defined for $s>0$ by $$\Gamma(s)=\int_0^\infty e^{-x}x^{s-1}\,dx.$$ (a) Show that for $s>0$ this improper integral makes sense, namely that both limits $$\lim_{\delta\to0^+}\int_\delta^1 e^{-x}x^{s-1}\,dx\qquad\text{and}\qquad \lim_{A\to\infty}\int_1^A e^{-x}x^{s-1}\,dx$$ exist. (b) Prove that $\Gamma(s+1)=s\Gamma(s)$ whenever $s>0$, and conclude that $\Gamma(n+1)=n!$ for every integer $n\ge1$. (c) Show that $$\Gamma\left(\frac12\right)=\sqrt\pi,\qquad \Gamma\left(\frac32\right)=\frac{\sqrt\pi}{2}.$$ Hint: For part (c), use $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}e^{-\pi x^2}\,dx=1$.
content.intuition: Gamma interpolates the factorial through the functional equation $\Gamma(s+1)=s\Gamma(s)$, and the Gaussian integral pins the half-integer values.

steps.content: Near $0$, $e^{-x}x^{s-1}\le x^{s-1}$ and $\int_0^1 x^{s-1}dx=\frac1s<\infty$ (needs $s>0$). Near $\infty$, $e^{-x}x^{s-1}$ decays faster than any power, so $\int_1^\infty<\infty$. Both one-sided limits exist; $\Gamma(s)$ is well defined for $s>0$.

Integrate by parts:
$$\Gamma(s+1)=\int_0^\infty e^{-x}x^{s}\,dx=\big[-e^{-x}x^s\big]_0^\infty+s\int_0^\infty e^{-x}x^{s-1}\,dx=s\,\Gamma(s).$$
Since $\Gamma(1)=\int_0^\infty e^{-x}dx=1$, induction gives $\Gamma(n+1)=n!$.

Substituting $x=\pi u^2$ ($dx=2\pi u\,du$),
$$\Gamma(\tfrac12)=\int_0^\infty e^{-x}x^{-1/2}dx=\int_0^\infty e^{-\pi u^2}\frac{2\pi u}{\sqrt{\pi}u}\,du=2\sqrt\pi\int_0^\infty e^{-\pi u^2}du=2\sqrt\pi\cdot\tfrac12=\sqrt\pi,$$
using $\int_{-\infty}^\infty e^{-\pi u^2}du=1$. Then $\Gamma(\tfrac32)=\tfrac12\Gamma(\tfrac12)=\frac{\sqrt\pi}2$.

content.statement: Construct compactly supported $C^\infty$ bump, ramp, and plateau functions.
content.formal: Bump functions. Compactly supported functions in $\mathcal S(\mathbb R)$ are useful in many applications. (a) Let $a<b$, and define $$f(x)=0\quad(x\le a\text{ or }x\ge b),\qquad f(x)=e^{-1/(x-a)}e^{-1/(b-x)}\quad(a<x<b).$$ Show that $f$ is indefinitely differentiable on $\mathbb R$. (b) Prove that there exists an indefinitely differentiable function $F$ on $\mathbb R$ such that $F(x)=0$ for $x\le a$, $F(x)=1$ for $x\ge b$, and $F$ is strictly increasing on $[a,b]$. (c) If $\delta>0$ is small enough that $a+\delta<b-\delta$, show that there exists an indefinitely differentiable function $g$ such that $g=0$ for $x\le a$ or $x\ge b$, $g=1$ on $[a+\delta,b-\delta]$, and $g$ is strictly monotone on $[a,a+\delta]$ and $[b-\delta,b]$. Hint for (b): take $$F(x)=c\int_{-\infty}^{x}f(t)\,dt$$ with a suitable constant $c$.
content.intuition: $e^{-1/t}$ is the canonical flat-but-nonzero function; integrating it gives smooth ramps, and multiplying two ramps gives a smooth plateau — the toolkit for cutoffs and partitions of unity.

steps.content: The one-sided model is $\phi(t)=e^{-1/t}$ for $t>0$, $\phi(t)=0$ for $t\le0$. By induction $\phi^{(k)}(t)=P_k(1/t)e^{-1/t}$ for $t>0$ (a polynomial in $1/t$), and since $e^{-1/t}$ beats every power of $1/t$, all one-sided derivatives at $0$ vanish: $\phi\in C^\infty(\mathbb R)$. Now $f(x)=\phi(x-a)\,\phi(b-x)$ is a product of $C^\infty$ functions, positive on $(a,b)$ and identically $0$ (with all derivatives) outside — hence $f\in C^\infty_c(\mathbb R)\subset\mathcal S(\mathbb R)$.

Let $c=\big(\int_a^b f\big)^{-1}$ and $F(x)=c\int_{-\infty}^x f(t)\,dt$. Then $F\in C^\infty$ ($F'=cf\ge0$), $F=0$ for $x\le a$, $F=1$ for $x\ge b$, and $F'=cf>0$ on $(a,b)$ so $F$ is strictly increasing there.

With $\delta>0$ small ($a+\delta<b-\delta$), take $g(x)=F_{a,a+\delta}(x)\,\big(1-F_{b-\delta,b}(x)\big)$ where $F_{p,q}$ is the part-(b) ramp on $[p,q]$. Then $g=0$ off $(a,b)$, $g\equiv1$ on $[a+\delta,b-\delta]$, and $g$ is $C^\infty$ and strictly monotone on the two transition zones.

concepts.label: SS 6.6.4 — Area and volume of spheres and balls

content.statement: Compute $A_d=2\pi^{d/2}/\Gamma(d/2)$ and $V_d=\pi^{d/2}/\Gamma(d/2+1)$.
content.formal: Let $A_d$ denote the surface area of the unit sphere $S^{d-1}\subset\mathbb R^d$, and let $V_d$ denote the volume of the unit ball in $\mathbb R^d$. (a) Prove $$A_d=\frac{2\pi^{d/2}}{\Gamma(d/2)},$$ so in particular $A_2=2\pi$, $A_3=4\pi$, and $A_4=2\pi^2$. Here $$\Gamma(x)=\int_0^\infty e^{-t}t^{x-1}\,dt.$$ Hint: use polar coordinates and $$\int_{\mathbb R^d}e^{-\pi |x|^2}\,dx=1.$$ (b) Show that $dV_d=A_d$, and hence $$V_d=\frac{\pi^{d/2}}{\Gamma(d/2+1)},$$ for example $V_2=\pi$ and $V_3=4\pi/3$.
content.intuition: The Gaussian integral, sliced radially, measures the sphere; the Gamma function carries the dimension, interpolating the familiar $2\pi,4\pi,\dots$ continuously.

steps.content:
Evaluate $\int_{\mathbb R^d}e^{-\pi|x|^2}dx=1$ in polar coordinates:
$$1=\int_{S^{d-1}}\!\!\int_0^\infty e^{-\pi r^2}r^{d-1}\,dr\,d\sigma=A_d\int_0^\infty e^{-\pi r^2}r^{d-1}\,dr.$$
Substituting $u=\pi r^2$, $\int_0^\infty e^{-\pi r^2}r^{d-1}dr=\tfrac12\pi^{-d/2}\Gamma(d/2)$, so $1=A_d\cdot\tfrac12\pi^{-d/2}\Gamma(d/2)$, giving $A_d=\dfrac{2\pi^{d/2}}{\Gamma(d/2)}$ ($A_2=2\pi$, $A_3=4\pi$).

$V_d=\int_0^1 A_d r^{d-1}dr=\dfrac{A_d}{d}$, and $d\,\Gamma(d/2)=2\Gamma(d/2+1)$ gives
$$V_d=\frac{2\pi^{d/2}}{d\,\Gamma(d/2)}=\frac{\pi^{d/2}}{\Gamma(d/2+1)}\quad(V_2=\pi,\ V_3=\tfrac{4\pi}3).$$

content.statement: The region where a function or distribution is nonzero.
content.formal: For a continuous function, $\operatorname{supp}f=\overline{\{x:f(x)\ne0\}}$. For measurable functions, support is usually understood up to almost-everywhere changes.
content.intuition: Support tells where an object lives. Frequency support tells which waves are present.

concepts.label: Tonelli's Theorem

content.statement: A nonnegative measurable function on a product measure space may be integrated in either order, possibly with value $+\infty$.
content.formal:
Let $(X,\mathcal A,\mu)$ and $(Y,\mathcal B,
u)$ be $\sigma$-finite measure spaces, and let $f:X	imes Y	o[0,\infty]$ be measurable. Then $$\int_{X	imes Y}f\,d(\mu	imes
u)=\int_X\left(\int_Y f(x,y)\,d
u(y)ight)d\mu(x)=\int_Y\left(\int_X f(x,y)\,d\mu(x)ight)d
u(y),$$ with equality allowed in $[0,\infty]$.
content.intuition: Nonnegative mass cannot cancel. Therefore summing or integrating it in different orders gives the same extended value.

assumptions: The measure spaces are sigma-finite.
The function is measurable on the product space.
The function is nonnegative, so the integral may be infinite.

steps.content: First prove the identity for indicator functions $1_{A\times B}$, where $A\in\mathcal A$ and $B\in\mathcal B$. This follows from $(\mu\times\nu)(A\times B)=\mu(A)\nu(B)$.
By finite linearity, the identity holds for nonnegative simple functions.
Approximate a nonnegative measurable function $f$ from below by nonnegative simple functions $s_n\uparrow f$. Apply monotone convergence to pass the identity to the limit.

source.ref: Wikipedia: Fubini's theorem, Tonelli's theorem section

concepts.label: Øving 1 · P1 — Modulus of continuity of C¹ functions

content.statement: Bound the modulus of continuity of a $C^1$ function by $\sup|f'|\cdot r$.
content.formal: For $f\in C^1(\mathbb R)$ and $[a,b]$, with $\omega_{f,[a,b]}(r):=\sup\{|f(x)-f(y)|:x,y\in[a,b],\,|x-y|<r\}$, show $\omega_{f,[a,b]}(r)\le\big(\sup_{x\in[a,b]}|f'(x)|\big)\,r$.
content.intuition: A bound on the derivative is a Lipschitz bound: the FTC turns $\sup|f'|$ directly into a linear modulus of continuity.

steps.content:
Let $M=\sup_{[a,b]}|f'|$ (finite, since $f'$ is continuous on the compact $[a,b]$). For any $x,y\in[a,b]$ with $|x-y|<r$, the fundamental theorem of calculus gives
$$|f(x)-f(y)|=\Big|\int_y^x f'(t)\,dt\Big|\le M\,|x-y|<M\,r.$$
Taking the supremum over all such pairs, $\omega_{f,[a,b]}(r)\le M\,r=\big(\sup_{[a,b]}|f'|\big)r$. $\blacksquare$

concepts.label: Øving 1 · P2 — Uniform continuity via P1

content.statement: Deduce uniform continuity of $x^2$ and $1/x$ from the derivative bound.
content.formal: Use P1 to show $f$ is uniformly continuous on $[a,b]$ when (i) $f(x)=x^2$ (any $a,b\in\mathbb R$); (ii) $f(x)=1/x$ (any $a,b$ with $0\notin[a,b]$, including $a=-\infty$, $b=\infty$).
content.intuition: Uniform continuity reduces to a bounded derivative on the set; once $\sup|f'|<\infty$, P1 delivers a modulus independent of location.

steps.content:
\textbf{(i)} On a bounded $[a,b]$, $f(x)=x^2$ has $|f'(x)|=2|x|\le 2\max(|a|,|b|)=:M<\infty$. By P1, $\omega_f(r)\le Mr\to0$ as $r\to0$, which is exactly uniform continuity.

\textbf{(ii)} If $0\notin[a,b]$, let $d=\operatorname{dist}(0,[a,b])>0$. Then $|f'(x)|=1/x^2\le 1/d^2$ on $[a,b]$ (this stays valid on unbounded intervals like $[a,\infty)$ with $a>0$, where $\sup|f'|=1/a^2<\infty$). By P1, $\omega_f(r)\le r/d^2\to0$: uniformly continuous. $\blacksquare$

(Note: on all of $\mathbb R$, $x^2$ is \emph{not} uniformly continuous, and $1/x$ fails near $0$ — the boundedness of $f'$ on the chosen set is precisely what P1 needs.)

concepts.label: Øving 1 · P3 — Computing moduli of continuity

content.statement: Compute $\omega_f$ for $\sqrt{1+x^2}$ (linear) and $\sqrt x$ (Hölder-½).
content.formal: Find $\omega_f$ when (i) $f(x)=\sqrt{1+x^2}$ on $\mathbb R$; (ii) $f(x)=x^{1/2}$ on $[0,1]$.
content.intuition: A bounded derivative gives a linear modulus ($\sqrt{1+x^2}$); an unbounded one near a point gives a genuine Hölder modulus ($\sqrt x$ has $\omega_f(r)=\sqrt r$).

steps.content:
\textbf{(i)} $f'(x)=\dfrac{x}{\sqrt{1+x^2}}$, so $|f'|<1$ with $\sup_{\mathbb R}|f'|=1$ (approached as $x\to\pm\infty$). By P1, $\omega_f(r)\le r$. The bound is attained in the limit: for large $x$, $f(x)\approx|x|$, so $f(x+r)-f(x)\to r$. Hence $\omega_f(r)=r$ — $f$ is Lipschitz with constant $1$.

\textbf{(ii)} For $0\le y\le x\le1$, $\sqrt x-\sqrt y\le\sqrt{x-y}$ (square: $x\le y+(x-y)+2\sqrt{y(x-y)}$, true). The largest gap with $|x-y|<r$ is at $y=0$, $x\to r^-$, giving $\sqrt r$. Thus $\omega_f(r)=\sqrt r$: $f$ is Hölder-$\tfrac12$ but not Lipschitz (the modulus is not $O(r)$ because $f'(x)=\tfrac1{2\sqrt x}\to\infty$ at $0$). $\blacksquare$

concepts.label: Øving 1 · P4 — Continuous on [a,b] ⟹ uniformly continuous

content.statement: Heine–Cantor: continuity on a compact interval is uniform continuity.
content.formal: Show that a continuous function on a closed bounded interval $[a,b]$ is uniformly continuous (Heine–Cantor).
content.intuition: Compactness upgrades pointwise continuity to uniform: a failing pair of sequences must accumulate, where continuity contradicts the gap.

steps.content:
Suppose, for contradiction, $f$ is continuous but not uniformly continuous. Then there is $\varepsilon>0$ and sequences $x_n,y_n\in[a,b]$ with $|x_n-y_n|<\tfrac1n$ but $|f(x_n)-f(y_n)|\ge\varepsilon$. By Bolzano–Weierstrass (compactness of $[a,b]$), a subsequence $x_{n_k}\to x^*\in[a,b]$; since $|x_{n_k}-y_{n_k}|\to0$, also $y_{n_k}\to x^*$. Continuity of $f$ at $x^*$ gives $f(x_{n_k})\to f(x^*)$ and $f(y_{n_k})\to f(x^*)$, so $|f(x_{n_k})-f(y_{n_k})|\to0$, contradicting $\ge\varepsilon$. Hence $f$ is uniformly continuous.

\emph{(Open-cover version: }for each $x$ pick $\delta_x$ with $\operatorname{osc}<\varepsilon$ on $B(x,\delta_x)$; the balls $B(x,\delta_x/2)$ cover $[a,b]$; extract a finite subcover and take $\delta$ = the smallest radius — a Lebesgue number.$)$ $\blacksquare$

concepts.label: Øving 2 · P1 — Which Lᵖ contain x^{-α}?

content.statement: Determine the $L^p(0,1)$ range of the singular power $x^{-\alpha}$.
content.formal: For $f(x)=1/x^\alpha$ on $(0,1)$, $\alpha\in(0,1)$, determine for which $p\ge1$ we have $f\in L^p(0,1)$.
content.intuition: Integrability of a power singularity is a single inequality $\alpha p<1$ — raising the power $p$ eventually fails once it overtakes the singularity.

steps.content:
$\displaystyle\int_0^1 |f(x)|^p\,dx=\int_0^1 x^{-\alpha p}\,dx$ converges iff the exponent satisfies $\alpha p<1$, i.e. $p<1/\alpha$ (for $\alpha p\ge1$ the integral diverges at $0$). Therefore
$$f\in L^p(0,1)\iff 1\le p<\tfrac1\alpha.$$
At the borderline $p=1/\alpha$ the integral is $\int_0^1 x^{-1}dx=\infty$, so $f\notin L^{1/\alpha}$. $\blacksquare$

In particular a mild singularity ($\alpha$ small) lies in many $L^p$; as $\alpha\uparrow1$ the admissible range shrinks toward $p=1$ only.

concepts.label: Øving 2 · P2 — Convolution with a box is averaging

content.statement: Convolution with a normalized box kernel is local averaging.
content.formal: For $f,g\in C(\mathbb S)$ with $g(x)=\frac{2\pi}{2h}\chi_{[-h,h]}(x)$ ($h<\pi$), show $(f*g)(x)=\frac1{2h}\int_{-h}^h f(x+y)\,dy$.
content.intuition: A normalized indicator kernel is a moving-average operator; convolving against it smooths $f$ by local averaging — the prototype of a good kernel.

steps.content:
With the circle convolution $(f*g)(x)=\frac1{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^\pi f(x-y)g(y)\,dy$ and $g=\frac{\pi}{h}\chi_{[-h,h]}$,
$$(f*g)(x)=\frac1{2\pi}\int_{-h}^h f(x-y)\,\frac{\pi}{h}\,dy=\frac1{2h}\int_{-h}^h f(x-y)\,dy.$$
Substituting $y\mapsto-y$ (the window is symmetric) gives $(f*g)(x)=\frac1{2h}\int_{-h}^h f(x+y)\,dy$ — the running average of $f$ over a window of width $2h$ centered at $x$. $\blacksquare$

content.statement: Convolution is associative (Fubini) for continuous and for Riemann-integrable data.
content.formal: Show $(f*g)*h=f*(g*h)$ when (a) $f,g,h\in C(\mathbb S)$; (b) $f,g,h$ are periodic and Riemann integrable.
content.intuition: Convolution is multiplication of Fourier coefficients, which is associative; directly, it is just Fubini on the torus.

steps.content:
Both sides are double integrals; associativity is a change of order (Fubini). Writing all factors of $\frac1{2\pi}$,
$$\big((f*g)*h\big)(x)=\frac1{(2\pi)^2}\int_{-\pi}^\pi\!\!\int_{-\pi}^\pi f(x-y-z)\,g(z)\,h(y)\,dz\,dy.$$
The integrand is bounded and (jointly) Riemann integrable on the compact torus $[-\pi,\pi]^2$ — in case (a) it is continuous, in case (b) a product of Riemann-integrable factors composed with continuous shifts, hence integrable. By Fubini's theorem we may interchange the order of integration; substituting $w=y+z$ symmetrizes the expression and reproduces
$$\frac1{(2\pi)^2}\int\!\!\int f(x-w)\,g(w-y)\,h(y)\,dy\,dw=\big(f*(g*h)\big)(x).$$
Thus $(f*g)*h=f*(g*h)$. $\blacksquare$

content.statement: Young-type bounds for convolution and continuity in the $L^1$ factor.
content.formal: For $f,g\in C(\mathbb S)$ show (a) $\|f*g\|_\infty\le\frac1{2\pi}\|f\|_\infty\|g\|_1$; (b) $\|f*g\|_1\le\frac1{2\pi}\|f\|_1\|g\|_1$; (c) $f_k\to f$ in $L^1(\mathbb S)$ $\Rightarrow$ $f_k*g\to f*g$ in $C(\mathbb S)$ and in $L^1(\mathbb S)$.
content.intuition: Convolution is bounded on every $L^p$ by the $L^1$ size of the kernel (Young); so $L^1$-perturbations of one factor produce uniformly/$L^1$-small perturbations of the convolution.

steps.content:
Throughout $\|u\|_1=\frac1{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^\pi|u|$ and $(f*g)(x)=\frac1{2\pi}\int f(x-y)g(y)\,dy$.

\textbf{(a)} $\displaystyle|(f*g)(x)|\le\frac1{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^\pi|f(x-y)|\,|g(y)|\,dy\le\frac1{2\pi}\|f\|_\infty\int_{-\pi}^\pi|g|=\frac1{2\pi}\|f\|_\infty\,\big(2\pi\|g\|_1\big)\cdot\tfrac1{2\pi}$; cleanly, $\le\frac1{2\pi}\|f\|_\infty\|g\|_1$ taking the sup over $x$ (here $\|g\|_1=\frac1{2\pi}\int|g|$).

\textbf{(b)} By Fubini and translation invariance,
$$\|f*g\|_1=\frac1{2\pi}\int\Big|\frac1{2\pi}\int f(x-y)g(y)\,dy\Big|dx\le\frac1{2\pi}\int|g(y)|\Big(\frac1{2\pi}\int|f(x-y)|\,dx\Big)dy=\|g\|_1\|f\|_1\cdot\tfrac1{2\pi}\cdot 2\pi,$$
i.e. $\|f*g\|_1\le\frac1{2\pi}\|f\|_1\|g\|_1$.

\textbf{(c)} By linearity $f_k*g-f*g=(f_k-f)*g$. Using commutativity and (a), $\|(f_k-f)*g\|_\infty=\|g*(f_k-f)\|_\infty\le\frac1{2\pi}\|g\|_\infty\|f_k-f\|_1\to0$, giving uniform (hence $C(\mathbb S)$) convergence; and (b) gives $\|(f_k-f)*g\|_1\le\frac1{2\pi}\|f_k-f\|_1\|g\|_1\to0$. $\blacksquare$

concepts.label: Øving 3 · P2 — The ℓ²(ℤ) inner product

content.statement: Verify the inner-product axioms on $\ell^2(\mathbb Z)$.
content.formal: Show that $\langle x,y\rangle=\sum_{i=-\infty}^\infty x_i\overline{y_i}$ is an inner product on $\ell^2(\mathbb Z)$.
content.intuition: The defining series converges by Cauchy–Schwarz, and the three axioms are immediate — making $\ell^2$ the model separable Hilbert space dual to Fourier coefficients.

steps.content:
\textbf{Well-defined.} By Cauchy–Schwarz for finite sums and a limit, $\sum_i|x_i\overline{y_i}|\le\big(\sum|x_i|^2\big)^{1/2}\big(\sum|y_i|^2\big)^{1/2}=\|x\|\,\|y\|<\infty$, so the series converges absolutely for $x,y\in\ell^2$.

\textbf{Axioms.} (i) \emph{Linearity in the first slot}: $\langle ax+bz,y\rangle=\sum(ax_i+bz_i)\overline{y_i}=a\langle x,y\rangle+b\langle z,y\rangle$. (ii) \emph{Conjugate symmetry}: $\langle y,x\rangle=\sum y_i\overline{x_i}=\overline{\sum x_i\overline{y_i}}=\overline{\langle x,y\rangle}$. (iii) \emph{Positive-definiteness}: $\langle x,x\rangle=\sum|x_i|^2\ge0$, with equality iff every $x_i=0$, i.e. $x=0$. Hence $\langle\cdot,\cdot\rangle$ is an inner product. $\blacksquare$ (Together with completeness — SS 3.3.2 — $\ell^2(\mathbb Z)$ is a Hilbert space.)

concepts.label: Øving 4 · P1 — The integral inner product

content.statement: Conjugate symmetry and linearity of the $L^2$ integral form on $\mathcal R(a,b)$.
content.formal: Check that $(f,g)=\int_a^b f(x)\overline{g(x)}\,dx$ has conjugate symmetry and is linear in the first argument on $\mathcal R(a,b)$ (Riemann-integrable $f:(a,b)\to\mathbb C$).
content.intuition: The Hermitian form $\int f\bar g$ inherits its algebra from the integral; only definiteness is subtle, which is exactly why $L^2$ is built from equivalence classes.

steps.content:
\textbf{Linearity (first slot).} For $\alpha,\beta\in\mathbb C$ and $f_1,f_2,g\in\mathcal R(a,b)$ (products of Riemann-integrable functions are Riemann-integrable, so all integrals exist),
$$(\alpha f_1+\beta f_2,g)=\int_a^b(\alpha f_1+\beta f_2)\overline g=\alpha\!\int_a^b f_1\overline g+\beta\!\int_a^b f_2\overline g=\alpha(f_1,g)+\beta(f_2,g),$$
by linearity of the integral. \textbf{Conjugate symmetry.}
$$(g,f)=\int_a^b g\overline f=\int_a^b\overline{\overline g f}=\overline{\int_a^b f\overline g}=\overline{(f,g)}.$$
$\blacksquare$ (Positive semidefiniteness also holds, but $(f,f)=0$ only forces $f=0$ at continuity points — see SS 3.3.4 — so this is a \emph{semi-}inner product on $\mathcal R$.)

content.statement: Riemann-integrable functions are not $L^2$-complete (the $x^{-1/4}$ example).
content.formal: For $f_n(x)=x^{-1/4}\chi_{(1/n,1)}(x)$, show $\int_0^1|f_n(x)-x^{-1/4}|^2dx\to0$, and conclude $(\mathcal R(0,1),\|\cdot\|_2)$ is not complete.
content.intuition: A Cauchy sequence of honest (bounded) functions chases an unbounded target: Riemann integrability is not closed under $L^2$-limits, which is why we pass to $L^2$.

steps.content:
$\displaystyle\int_0^1|f_n-x^{-1/4}|^2\,dx=\int_0^{1/n}x^{-1/2}\,dx=\big[2\sqrt x\big]_0^{1/n}=\frac{2}{\sqrt n}\to0.$

Each $f_n$ is bounded and Riemann integrable, so $f_n\in\mathcal R(0,1)$. Since $f_n\to x^{-1/4}$ in $\|\cdot\|_2$, the sequence is $\|\cdot\|_2$-Cauchy: $\|f_n-f_m\|_2\le\|f_n-x^{-1/4}\|_2+\|f_m-x^{-1/4}\|_2\to0$. But its only possible $L^2$-limit is $x^{-1/4}$, which is \emph{unbounded} near $0$ and therefore not Riemann integrable on $[0,1]$ — so no limit exists \emph{within} $\mathcal R(0,1)$. (If some $g\in\mathcal R(0,1)$ were the limit, then $g=x^{-1/4}$ a.e., contradicting boundedness of $g$.) Hence $(\mathcal R(0,1),\|\cdot\|_2)$ is not complete; its completion is $L^2(0,1)$. $\blacksquare$

concepts.label: Øving 4 · P3 — Real orthonormal trig bases

content.statement: The sine/cosine and half-range systems are orthonormal bases.
content.formal: (a) Show $\{\sqrt2\sin nx\}_{n\ge1}\cup\{\sqrt2\cos nx\}_{n\ge0}$ is an orthonormal set and a basis in $\mathcal R(-\pi,\pi)$. (b) Show $\{\sqrt2\sin nx\}_{n\ge1}$ and $\{\sqrt2\cos nx\}_{n\ge0}$ are orthonormal sets and bases in $\mathcal R(0,\pi)$.
content.intuition: Sine/cosine systems are the real and half-range repackagings of $\{e^{inx}\}$; completeness is inherited from mean-square convergence via odd/even extension.

steps.content:
Use the normalized inner product $\langle u,v\rangle=\frac1\pi\int u\overline v$ on the relevant interval.

\textbf{Orthogonality.} For integers $m,n$, the product-to-sum identities give $\int_{-\pi}^\pi\sin mx\sin nx=\pi\delta_{mn}$, $\int_{-\pi}^\pi\cos mx\cos nx=\pi\delta_{mn}$ (for $n\ge1$; $2\pi$ for $n=0$), and $\int_{-\pi}^\pi\sin mx\cos nx=0$. Dividing by $\pi$, $\{\sqrt2\sin nx,\sqrt2\cos nx\}$ have unit norm and are mutually orthogonal — an orthonormal system (the constant $\sqrt2\cos0=\sqrt2$ is rescaled to norm $1$ appropriately).

\textbf{Completeness on $(-\pi,\pi)$.} This system is the real form of $\{e^{inx}\}_{n\in\mathbb Z}$ via $e^{\pm inx}=\cos nx\pm i\sin nx$; since $\{e^{inx}\}$ is complete in $\mathcal R(-\pi,\pi)$ (mean-square convergence of Fourier series), so is the real system: every $f$ equals its sine–cosine series in $\|\cdot\|_2$, i.e. it is a basis.

\textbf{Half-range on $(0,\pi)$.} Extending $f\in\mathcal R(0,\pi)$ \emph{oddly} to $(-\pi,\pi)$ shows $\{\sqrt2\sin nx\}_{n\ge1}$ alone is complete (the odd extension's series uses only sines); extending \emph{evenly} shows $\{\sqrt2\cos nx\}_{n\ge0}$ alone is complete. Orthonormality on $(0,\pi)$ follows from $\int_0^\pi\sin mx\sin nx=\tfrac\pi2\delta_{mn}=\int_0^\pi\cos mx\cos nx$ (so $\sqrt2$ normalizes with $\frac1\pi\int_0^\pi\cdot 2=1$). $\blacksquare$

concepts.label: Øving 7 · P1 — Scaling of the integral (SS Prop 1.1 iii)

content.statement: Dilation/scaling property of the integral on moderate-decrease functions.
content.formal: Prove property (iii) of SS Proposition 1.1 (§5.1.1): for $f\in\mathcal M(\mathbb R)$ (moderate decrease) and $\delta>0$, $\;\delta\int_{-\infty}^\infty f(\delta x)\,dx=\int_{-\infty}^\infty f(x)\,dx$.
content.intuition: Stretching the variable by $\delta$ rescales the integral by $1/\delta$ — the change of variables that becomes the Fourier scaling law.

steps.content:
Both integrals exist because $f$ has moderate decrease ($|f(x)|\le A/(1+x^2)$). For finite $N$, substitute $u=\delta x$ ($du=\delta\,dx$):
$$\delta\int_{-N}^N f(\delta x)\,dx=\int_{-\delta N}^{\delta N}f(u)\,du.$$
Letting $N\to\infty$, the right side converges to $\int_{-\infty}^\infty f(u)\,du$ (the limit defining the integral of a moderate-decrease function), so
$$\delta\int_{-\infty}^\infty f(\delta x)\,dx=\int_{-\infty}^\infty f(x)\,dx.\qquad\blacksquare$$
This dilation rule underlies the scaling law $f(\delta x)\to\delta^{-1}\hat f(\delta^{-1}\xi)$ of the Fourier transform.

concepts.label: Øving 7 · P2 — Continuity of translation in L¹ (SS Prop 1.1 iv)

content.statement: $L^1$-continuity of translation for moderate-decrease and $L^1$ functions.
content.formal: Show property (iv) of SS Proposition 1.1, $\int_{-\infty}^\infty|f(x-h)-f(x)|\,dx\to0$ as $h\to0$, holds for (a) $f\in\mathcal R_m(\mathbb R)$ (Riemann-integrable, moderate decrease); (b) $f\in L^1(\mathbb R)$.
content.intuition: Translation is continuous in $L^1$: trivially true for smooth bumps, then transported to all of $L^1$ (and to moderate decrease) by density and the translation isometry.

steps.content:
\textbf{Continuous compactly supported core.} If $g\in C_c(\mathbb R)$, then $g$ is uniformly continuous and supported in some $[-R,R]$; for $|h|\le1$, $|g(x-h)-g(x)|<\varepsilon$ uniformly and the integrand is supported in $[-R-1,R+1]$, so $\int|g(\cdot-h)-g|\le\varepsilon\,(2R+2)\to0$.

\textbf{(b) $f\in L^1$.} $C_c(\mathbb R)$ is dense in $L^1$: pick $g\in C_c$ with $\|f-g\|_{L^1}<\varepsilon$. Translation is an $L^1$-isometry, so
$$\int|f(\cdot-h)-f|\le\int|f(\cdot-h)-g(\cdot-h)|+\int|g(\cdot-h)-g|+\int|g-f|=2\|f-g\|_{L^1}+\int|g(\cdot-h)-g|<3\varepsilon$$
for $|h|$ small. Hence the limit is $0$.

\textbf{(a) $f\in\mathcal R_m(\mathbb R)$.} A Riemann-integrable function of moderate decrease lies in $L^1$ and is approximable in $L^1$ by continuous compactly supported functions (truncate the tails using $|f|\le A/(1+x^2)$, then approximate the bounded RI piece by continuous functions). The same three-term estimate applies, giving $\int|f(\cdot-h)-f|\to0$. $\blacksquare$

concepts.label: Øving 8 · P4 — Moderate decrease ⟹ uniformly continuous

content.statement: Continuous moderate-decrease functions are uniformly continuous.
content.formal: Show every $f\in C_m(\mathbb R)$ (continuous, moderate decrease) is uniformly continuous on $\mathbb R$.
content.intuition: Decay tames the non-compact tails (values are tiny there), while compactness handles the middle — together giving a single $\delta$ that works everywhere.

steps.content: Let $\varepsilon>0$. By moderate decrease choose $N$ with $|f(x)|<\varepsilon/2$ for $|x|>N$. On the compact $[-N-1,N+1]$, $f$ is uniformly continuous (Heine–Cantor), so there is $0<\delta<1$ with $|f(x)-f(y)|<\varepsilon$ whenever $x,y\in[-N-1,N+1]$ and $|x-y|<\delta$. Now take any $x,y$ with $|x-y|<\delta$: if both lie in $[-N-1,N+1]$ we are done; otherwise at least one of them has modulus $>N$ and (since $|x-y|<1$) both do, giving $|f(x)-f(y)|\le|f(x)|+|f(y)|<\varepsilon$. Hence $f$ is uniformly continuous on all of $\mathbb R$. $\blacksquare$

concepts.label: Øving 8 · P6 — L¹ approximation by moderate-decrease functions

content.statement: Moderate-decrease functions are $L^1$-dense.
content.formal: For $f\in L^1(\mathbb R)$, show there exist $f_n\in C_m(\mathbb R)$ with $\|f-f_n\|_{L^1(\mathbb R)}<1/n$.
content.intuition: Compactly supported continuous functions are both $L^1$-dense and trivially of moderate decrease, so $C_m$ is dense in $L^1$.

steps.content: Continuous compactly supported functions are dense in $L^1(\mathbb R)$: given $f\in L^1$, there is $f_n\in C_c(\mathbb R)$ with $\|f-f_n\|_{L^1}<1/n$ (standard approximation — truncate, then mollify). Any $f_n\in C_c(\mathbb R)$ has moderate decrease: it is continuous and vanishes outside a compact $[-R,R]$, so $|f_n(x)|\le \|f_n\|_\infty\le \frac{A}{1+x^2}$ for a suitable $A$ (take $A=\|f_n\|_\infty(1+R^2)$). Hence $f_n\in C_m(\mathbb R)$ and $\|f-f_n\|_{L^1}<1/n$. $\blacksquare$

concepts.label: Bessel's Inequality

content.statement: The squared Fourier coefficients of an $L^2$ function cannot carry more energy than the function itself.
content.formal: If $f\in L^2(\mathbb T)$, then $\sum_{n\in\mathbb Z}|\hat f(n)|^2\le \|f\|_2^2$.
content.intuition: Projecting onto orthonormal directions cannot create energy. Equality requires that the chosen directions are complete.

assumptions: This is an $L^2$ statement; it uses the normalized inner product on $\mathbb T$.
This is an $L^2$ statement with the normalized inner product on $\mathbb T$.
The proof only needs finite orthogonal projections before passing to the monotone limit over frequencies.

steps.content: Let $f\in L^2(\mathbb T)$, and fix a finite set $F\subset\mathbb Z$. Put $$p_F=\sum_{n\in F}\widehat f(n)e^{inx}.$$ The functions $e_n(x)=e^{inx}$ form an orthonormal system in $L^2(\mathbb T)$ with the normalized inner product $$\langle f,g\rangle=\frac1{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^{\pi}f(x)\overline{g(x)}\,dx.$$ Hence $p_F$ is the orthogonal projection of $f$ onto the finite-dimensional span of \(\{e_n:n\in F\}\).

For every $m\in F$, $$\langle f-p_F,e_m\rangle=\widehat f(m)-\sum_{n\in F}\widehat f(n)\langle e_n,e_m\rangle=\widehat f(m)-\widehat f(m)=0.$$ Thus $f-p_F$ is orthogonal to $p_F$. By the Pythagorean identity, $$\|f\|_2^2=\|p_F\|_2^2+\|f-p_F\|_2^2\ge \|p_F\|_2^2.$$ Since the $e_n$ are orthonormal, $$\|p_F\|_2^2=\sum_{n\in F}|\widehat f(n)|^2.$$ Therefore $$\sum_{n\in F}|\widehat f(n)|^2\le \|f\|_2^2.$$

Apply the finite estimate to $F_N=\{-N,-N+1,\dots,N\}$. Then $$\sum_{|n|\le N}|\widehat f(n)|^2\le \|f\|_2^2\qquad(N\ge0).$$ The left-hand side is increasing in $N$, so taking the limit gives $$\sum_{n\in\mathbb Z}|\widehat f(n)|^2\le \|f\|_2^2.$$ This is Bessel's inequality for Fourier coefficients.

source.citation: Stein–Shakarchi: Fourier Analysis, Ch.3; UCI lecture notes on Bessel inequality and Parseval identity
source.ref: Stein–Shakarchi: Fourier Analysis, Ch.3; UCI lecture notes on Bessel inequality and Parseval identity

concepts.label: Best Approximation Property

content.statement: The Fourier partial sum is the orthogonal projection onto the low-frequency trigonometric polynomials.
content.formal: For $f\in L^2(\mathbb T)$, $S_Nf$ is the unique element of $\operatorname{span}\{e^{inx}:|n|\le N\}$ minimizing $\|f-p\|_2$. Equivalently, $f-S_Nf$ is orthogonal to every trigonometric polynomial of degree at most $N$.
content.intuition: Truncating a Fourier series is not a random cutoff. It is the closest low-frequency shadow of $f$ in the geometry of $L^2$.

assumptions: The minimization is in the $L^2$-norm on $\mathbb T$.
The minimization is over the finite-dimensional space of trigonometric polynomials with frequencies $|n|\le N$.
The proof uses only finite-dimensional orthogonal projection in $L^2(\mathbb T)$.

steps.content: Let $$V_N=\operatorname{span}\{e^{inx}:|n|\le N\}\subset L^2(\mathbb T).$$ The Fourier partial sum $$S_Nf=\sum_{|n|\le N}\widehat f(n)e^{inx}$$ belongs to $V_N$. The goal is to prove that it is the closest element of $V_N$ to $f$ in the $L^2$ norm.

For every basis vector $e_m$ with $|m|\le N$, $$\langle f-S_Nf,e_m\rangle=\widehat f(m)-\sum_{|n|\le N}\widehat f(n)\langle e_n,e_m\rangle=0.$$ Since the $e_m$ span $V_N$, it follows that $$f-S_Nf\perp V_N.$$ Thus $S_Nf$ is the orthogonal projection of $f$ onto $V_N$.

Let $p\in V_N$. Then $$f-p=(f-S_Nf)+(S_Nf-p),$$ where $f-S_Nf\perp V_N$ and $S_Nf-p\in V_N$. Therefore the two summands are orthogonal. Hence $$\|f-p\|_2^2=\|f-S_Nf\|_2^2+\|S_Nf-p\|_2^2\ge \|f-S_Nf\|_2^2.$$ Equality holds exactly when $p=S_Nf$. Thus $$S_Nf=\operatorname*{arg\,min}_{p\in V_N}\|f-p\|_2.$$

source.citation: Stein–Shakarchi: Fourier Analysis, Ch.3 Lemma 1.2; University of Oslo Fourier analysis notes
source.ref: Stein–Shakarchi: Fourier Analysis, Ch.3 Lemma 1.2; University of Oslo Fourier analysis notes

concepts.label: Completeness of the Trigonometric System

content.statement: The exponentials $e^{inx}$ form a complete orthonormal system in $L^2(\mathbb T)$.
content.formal: If $f\in L^2(\mathbb T)$ and $\langle f,e^{inx}\rangle=0$ for every $n\in\mathbb Z$, then $f=0$ in $L^2(\mathbb T)$. Equivalently, the closed linear span of $\{e^{inx}:n\in\mathbb Z\}$ is all of $L^2(\mathbb T)$.
content.intuition: Orthogonality alone only says Fourier coefficients are coordinates in some directions. Completeness says there are no hidden $L^2$ directions left over after all trigonometric frequencies are included.

assumptions: Completeness is an $L^2$ norm statement; it does not assert pointwise convergence of ordinary partial sums.

concepts.label: Decay–Smoothness Duality

content.statement: Smoothness of a periodic function forces decay of its Fourier coefficients; sufficiently fast coefficient decay forces smoothness.
content.definition: $$\begin{aligned} f\in C^k(\mathbb T)&\Longrightarrow \widehat f(n)=o(|n|^{-k})\quad(|n|\to\infty),\\ \sum_{n\in\mathbb Z}|n|^k|\widehat f(n)|<\infty&\Longrightarrow \sum_{n\in\mathbb Z}\widehat f(n)e^{inx}\in C^k(\mathbb T). \end{aligned}$$
content.formal: If $f$ is $2\pi$-periodic and $C^k$ with matching periodic derivatives up to order $k$, then repeated integration by parts gives $\hat f(n)=o(|n|^{-k})$ under standard endpoint regularity. Conversely, if $\sum_{n\in\mathbb Z}|n|^k|\hat f(n)|<\infty$, then the Fourier series defines a $C^k$ function.
content.intuition: Differentiation multiplies the $n$-th Fourier coefficient by $in$. Smooth functions therefore have coefficients that die quickly, while sufficiently fast decay lets derivatives be reconstructed term by term.

assumptions: Periodic endpoint matching matters: derivatives are taken on the circle, not merely on an interval with unrelated endpoints.

content.statement: Compute the Fourier coefficients of the triangular wave $f(x)=1-|x|/\pi$ on $[-\pi,\pi]$.
content.formal: Let $f$ be $2\pi$-periodic with $f(x)=1-\frac{|x|}{\pi}$ for $x\in[-\pi,\pi]$. Show that for $n\neq0$, $\ \hat f(n)=\frac{2}{\pi^2 n^2}$ if $n$ is odd and $\hat f(n)=0$ if $n$ is even.
content.intuition: Even + continuous + a single corner per half-period gives real coefficients decaying like $1/n^2$, with even harmonics annihilated by the symmetry $f(x+\pi)$.

steps.content:
$f$ is the \textbf{triangular wave}: even, continuous (note $f(\pm\pi)=0$, so the periodic extension has no jumps), piecewise linear with corners at integer multiples of $\pi$. Because $f$ is even and real, $\hat f(n)=\hat f(-n)\in\mathbb R$ and the sine part of $e^{-inx}$ drops out:
$$\hat f(n)=\frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^{\pi}\Big(1-\tfrac{|x|}{\pi}\Big)\cos(nx)\,dx=\frac1\pi\int_0^\pi\Big(1-\tfrac{x}{\pi}\Big)\cos(nx)\,dx,$$
where evenness folds $[-\pi,\pi]$ onto $[0,\pi]$. For $n\neq0$, $\int_0^\pi\cos(nx)\,dx=\frac{\sin(n\pi)}{n}=0$, and integration by parts gives
$$\int_0^\pi x\cos(nx)\,dx=\Big[\tfrac{x\sin(nx)}{n}\Big]_0^\pi-\frac1n\int_0^\pi\sin(nx)\,dx=\frac{1}{n^2}\big[\cos(nx)\big]_0^\pi=\frac{(-1)^n-1}{n^2}.$$
Hence
$$\hat f(n)=\frac1\pi\Big(0-\frac1\pi\cdot\frac{(-1)^n-1}{n^2}\Big)=\frac{1}{\pi^2}\cdot\frac{1-(-1)^n}{n^2}.$$
For $n$ even, $1-(-1)^n=0\Rightarrow\hat f(n)=0$; for $n$ odd, $1-(-1)^n=2\Rightarrow\hat f(n)=\dfrac{2}{\pi^2 n^2}$.
The mean value is $\hat f(0)=\frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^\pi(1-|x|/\pi)\,dx=\tfrac12$ — the average height of the triangle (used in 1c).

content.statement: Prove the Fourier series of the triangular wave converges uniformly to $f$.
content.formal: With $f$ and $\hat f$ as in 1a, prove that the Fourier series of $f$ converges uniformly to $f$.
content.intuition: Continuity + a single corner ⟹ $1/n^2$ decay ⟹ absolutely summable coefficients ⟹ uniform convergence; uniqueness fixes the limit at $f$.

steps.content:
From 1a, $|\hat f(n)|\le \dfrac{2}{\pi^2 n^2}$ for $n\neq0$, so the coefficients are \textbf{absolutely summable}:
$$\sum_{n\in\mathbb Z}|\hat f(n)|=\tfrac12+\frac{4}{\pi^2}\sum_{\substack{n\ge1\\ n\ \mathrm{odd}}}\frac1{n^2}<\infty$$
by comparison with $\sum 1/n^2$. Since $|\hat f(n)e^{inx}|=|\hat f(n)|$, the \textbf{Weierstrass M-test} makes
$$g(x):=\sum_{n\in\mathbb Z}\hat f(n)e^{inx}$$
converge absolutely and uniformly on $\mathbb R$; a uniform limit of continuous trigonometric polynomials is continuous, so $g\in C(\mathbb T)$.

To identify $g=f$: uniform convergence lets us integrate term by term, so for every $m$,
$$\hat g(m)=\frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^\pi g(x)e^{-imx}\,dx=\sum_n\hat f(n)\cdot\frac1{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^\pi e^{i(n-m)x}\,dx=\hat f(m).$$
Thus $f$ and $g$ are both continuous with the same Fourier coefficients; by the uniqueness theorem (applied to $f-g$), $f\equiv g$. Therefore $S_N[f]\to f$ uniformly.

content.statement: Use Parseval to prove $\sum_{n\ge0}1/(2n+1)^4=\pi^4/96$.
content.formal: With $f$ as in 1a, prove $\displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^\infty \frac{1}{(2n+1)^4}=\frac{\pi^4}{96}$.
content.intuition: Parseval converts an integral of $f^2$ (easy: $1/3$) into a sum over coefficients; only odd harmonics survive, isolating the odd-index zeta value.

steps.content: Apply \textbf{Parseval's identity} $\sum_{n}|\hat f(n)|^2=\frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^\pi|f|^2$.

Using evenness and $u=1-x/\pi$,
$$\frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^\pi\Big(1-\tfrac{|x|}\pi\Big)^2dx=\frac1\pi\int_0^\pi\Big(1-\tfrac{x}\pi\Big)^2dx=\frac1\pi\cdot\pi\int_0^1u^2\,du=\frac13.$$

With $\hat f(0)=\tfrac12$ and $\hat f(\pm n)=\tfrac{2}{\pi^2n^2}$ for odd $n$ (zero for even $n\neq0$),
$$\sum_n|\hat f(n)|^2=\frac14+2\sum_{\substack{n\ge1\\ n\ \mathrm{odd}}}\Big(\frac{2}{\pi^2n^2}\Big)^2=\frac14+\frac{8}{\pi^4}\sum_{k=0}^\infty\frac{1}{(2k+1)^4}.$$

Equating, $\frac14+\frac{8}{\pi^4}S=\frac13$ with $S=\sum_{k\ge0}(2k+1)^{-4}$, so $\frac{8}{\pi^4}S=\frac1{12}$ and
$$\boxed{\ \sum_{n=0}^\infty\frac{1}{(2n+1)^4}=\frac{\pi^4}{96}.\ }$$
$\zeta(4)=\frac{\pi^4}{90}$; odd/even split gives $S=\zeta(4)-\frac{1}{16}\zeta(4)=\frac{15}{16}\cdot\frac{\pi^4}{90}=\frac{\pi^4}{96}$.

concepts.label: Fourier Coefficient

content.statement: The $n$-th coordinate of a periodic function against the harmonic $e^{inx}$.
content.formal: For $f\in L^1(\mathbb T)$ and $n\in\mathbb Z$, $$\widehat f(n)=\int_{\mathbb T}f(x)e^{-inx}\,dm(x)=\frac1{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^{\pi}f(x)e^{-inx}\,dx.$$ If $f\in L^2(\mathbb T)$, then $\widehat f(n)=\langle f,e_n\rangle$ with $e_n(x)=e^{inx}$.
content.intuition: The coefficient $\hat f(n)$ measures the average alignment of $f$ with the $n$-th rotating wave. In $L^2$ this is literally an orthogonal coordinate; in $L^1$ the same integral still makes sense and ignores changes on null sets.

assumptions: For $L^1$ functions the integral definition is primary; the projection interpretation is geometrically literal in $L^2$.

concepts.label: Fourier Series
