content.statement: The formal reconstruction of a periodic function from all its Fourier coefficients.
content.formal: For $f\in L^1(\mathbb T)$, its Fourier series is written $$f(x)\sim\sum_{n\in\mathbb Z}\widehat f(n)e^{inx}.$$ The symbol $\sim$ means "has Fourier series," not automatic pointwise equality or convergence.
content.intuition: The series turns the spectrum back into a signal by putting each coefficient on its harmonic. Much of Fourier analysis asks when this formal reconstruction converges back to $f$, and in which sense.

assumptions: The notation $f\sim\sum\hat f(n)e^{inx}$ does not by itself assert convergence.

concepts.label: Fourier Spectrum

content.statement: The full indexed sequence of Fourier coefficients of a periodic function.
content.formal: For $f\in L^1(\mathbb T)$, its Fourier spectrum is the two-sided sequence $\{\widehat f(n)\}_{n\in\mathbb Z}$. The coefficient at $n=0$ is the normalized average of $f$: $$\widehat f(0)=\frac1{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^{\pi}f(x)\,dx.$$
content.intuition: The spectrum is the frequency-side fingerprint of $f$: low indices record coarse oscillation and large $|n|$ records fine oscillation.

assumptions: The spectrum is a sequence indexed by integer frequencies for functions on $\mathbb T$.

concepts.label: Half-Range Expansions

content.statement: A sine or cosine expansion on an interval obtained by odd or even periodic extension.
content.formal: A function on $[0,\pi]$ can be extended oddly to obtain a sine series or evenly to obtain a cosine series on $[-\pi,\pi]$. The chosen extension determines the boundary behavior.
content.intuition: Boundary conditions choose the symmetry. Fixed endpoints naturally lead to sines; Neumann-type symmetry naturally leads to cosines.

assumptions: Endpoint behavior depends on the chosen extension and the regularity of the original function.

concepts.label: Parseval's Identity

content.statement: Fourier coefficients preserve the full $L^2$ energy of a function.
content.formal: For $f,g\in L^2(\mathbb T)$, $\sum_{n\in\mathbb Z}|\hat f(n)|^2=\|f\|_2^2$ and $\langle f,g\rangle=\sum_{n\in\mathbb Z}\hat f(n)\overline{\hat g(n)}$.
content.formula: $$\begin{aligned} f,g\in L^2(\mathbb T)&\Longrightarrow \sum_{n\in\mathbb Z}|\widehat f(n)|^2=\|f\|_2^2,\\ \langle f,g\rangle&=\sum_{n\in\mathbb Z}\widehat f(n)\overline{\widehat g(n)}. \end{aligned}$$
content.intuition: Fourier analysis is an isometry in $L^2$: functions and their coefficient sequences carry the same geometric information.

assumptions: Equality uses completeness of the trigonometric system in $L^2(\mathbb T)$.
Completeness, not merely orthogonality, is the input that upgrades Bessel's inequality to equality.
The mixed inner-product identity follows from the norm identity by polarization.

steps.content: Let $$S_Nf=\sum_{|n|\le N}\widehat f(n)e^{inx}.$$ By orthogonality, $$\|f\|_2^2=\|S_Nf\|_2^2+\|f-S_Nf\|_2^2.$$ Since the exponentials are orthonormal, $$\|S_Nf\|_2^2=\sum_{|n|\le N}|\widehat f(n)|^2.$$ Therefore $$\|f\|_2^2=\sum_{|n|\le N}|\widehat f(n)|^2+\|f-S_Nf\|_2^2.$$

The missing input is that the trigonometric system is complete in $L^2(\mathbb T)$, equivalently that $$\|S_Nf-f\|_2\xrightarrow[N\to\infty]{}0.$$ This is the mean-square convergence theorem for Fourier series. Letting $N\to\infty$ in the projection identity gives $$\|f\|_2^2=\sum_{n\in\mathbb Z}|\widehat f(n)|^2.$$

For the mixed identity, apply the norm identity to $f+g$, $f-g$, $f+ig$, and $f-ig$, or equivalently use polarization in the Hilbert space $L^2(\mathbb T)$. This yields $$\langle f,g\rangle=\sum_{n\in\mathbb Z}\widehat f(n)\overline{\widehat g(n)}.$$ Thus Fourier coefficients preserve both norm and inner product in $L^2(\mathbb T)$.

source.citation: Stein–Shakarchi: Fourier Analysis, Ch.3 Lemma 1.5 and Theorem 1.3; UCI lecture notes on Bessel inequality and Parseval identity
source.ref: Stein–Shakarchi: Fourier Analysis, Ch.3 Lemma 1.5 and Theorem 1.3; UCI lecture notes on Bessel inequality and Parseval identity

content.statement: The finite Fourier approximation obtained by keeping only frequencies $|n|\le N$.
content.formal: For $f\in L^1(\mathbb T)$, $S_Nf(x)=\sum_{|n|\le N}\hat f(n)e^{inx}$. Equivalently, $S_Nf=D_N*f$, where $D_N$ is the Dirichlet kernel.
content.intuition: A partial sum is the low-frequency shadow of $f$. Whether these shadows converge to the original function is subtle.

concepts.label: Real Sine-Cosine Fourier Series

content.statement: The classical real form of a Fourier series using cosines and sines instead of complex exponentials.
content.formal: For a real-valued integrable $2\pi$-periodic function, the Fourier series may be written $$\frac{a_0}{2}+\sum_{n\ge1}\bigl(a_n\cos nx+b_n\sin nx\bigr),$$ where $$a_n=\frac1\pi\int_{-\pi}^{\pi}f(x)\cos nx\,dx,\qquad b_n=\frac1\pi\int_{-\pi}^{\pi}f(x)\sin nx\,dx.$$ This is equivalent to the complex form by Euler's formula.
content.intuition: The sine-cosine form is the same decomposition written in real waves. It is often the first form seen in applications, while the complex form keeps algebra and convolution cleaner.

assumptions: The displayed sine-cosine coefficients use the standard unnormalized $a_n,b_n$ convention on $[-\pi,\pi].

concepts.label: Riemann Localization Principle

content.statement: Pointwise convergence of a Fourier series at a point depends only on the function near that point.
content.formal: If $f,g\in L^1(\mathbb T)$ agree on an open neighborhood of $x_0$, then $S_Nf(x_0)-S_Ng(x_0)\to0$. Hence convergence and the limiting value at $x_0$, when they exist, are local.
content.intuition: Fourier coefficients are global, but the oscillation of the Dirichlet kernel cancels distant contributions at a fixed point.

assumptions: Usually stated for $L^1$ functions on the circle.

concepts.label: Riemann–Lebesgue Lemma

content.statement: Fourier coefficients of an integrable function vanish at high frequency.
content.formal: If $f\in L^1(\mathbb T)$, then $\hat f(n)\to0$ as $|n|\to\infty$. Equivalently, high-frequency oscillations average out against every $L^1$ function on the circle.
content.intuition: The waves $e^{-inx}$ oscillate faster and faster. Against an integrable function, their positive and negative swings cancel in the limit.

assumptions: The function is understood as an $L^1(\mathbb T)$ equivalence class; the conclusion concerns its Fourier coefficients.
The proof uses density of step functions in $L^1(\mathbb T)$.
The conclusion is decay of the coefficient sequence as $|n|\to\infty$.

steps.content: First consider the indicator function $\mathbf 1_{[a,b]}$ on $[-\pi,\pi]$. For $n\ne0$, its Fourier coefficient is $$\widehat{\mathbf 1_{[a,b]}}(n)=\frac1{2\pi}\int_a^b e^{-inx}\,dx=\frac{e^{-ina}-e^{-inb}}{2\pi in}.$$ Hence $$\widehat{\mathbf 1_{[a,b]}}(n)\to0\qquad(|n|\to\infty).$$ By linearity, the same holds for every step function.

Let $f\in L^1(\mathbb T)$ and let $\varepsilon>0$. Choose a step function $s$ such that $$\|f-s\|_1<\varepsilon.$$ For every $n\in\mathbb Z$, $$|\widehat f(n)-\widehat s(n)|=|\widehat{f-s}(n)|\le \frac1{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^{\pi}|f(x)-s(x)|\,dx=\|f-s\|_1<\varepsilon.$$

Since $\widehat s(n)\to0$, there exists $N$ such that $|\widehat s(n)|<\varepsilon$ for $|n|\ge N$. Therefore, for $|n|\ge N$, $$|\widehat f(n)|\le |\widehat f(n)-\widehat s(n)|+|\widehat s(n)|<2\varepsilon.$$ Because $\varepsilon>0$ was arbitrary, $$\widehat f(n)\xrightarrow[|n|\to\infty]{}0.$$

source.citation: Stein–Shakarchi: Fourier Analysis, Ch.3 Theorem 1.4; NTNU note on the Riemann–Lebesgue lemma; M.T. Nair Fourier analysis notes
source.ref: Stein–Shakarchi: Fourier Analysis, Ch.3 Theorem 1.4; NTNU note on the Riemann–Lebesgue lemma; M.T. Nair Fourier analysis notes

content.statement: Smoothness of $f$ controls the decay rate of $\hat f(n)$.
content.formal: Suppose $f$ is a $2\pi$-periodic function of class $C^k$. Show that $$\widehat f(n)=O(1/|n|^k)\qquad(|n|\to\infty),$$ meaning that there is a constant $C$ such that $|\widehat f(n)|\le C/|n|^k$. Equivalently, $|n|^k\widehat f(n)=O(1)$. Hint: integrate by parts.
content.intuition: Differentiation multiplies the $n$-th coefficient by $in$; running it backwards, $k$ orders of smoothness force $k$ powers of decay.

steps.content:
Integrate by parts; periodicity kills every boundary term:
$$\hat f(n)=\frac1{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^\pi f(\theta)e^{-in\theta}\,d\theta=\frac1{2\pi}\,\frac1{in}\int_{-\pi}^\pi f'(\theta)e^{-in\theta}\,d\theta=\frac1{in}\,\widehat{f'}(n).$$
Iterating $k$ times (valid since $f\in C^k$),
$$\hat f(n)=\frac{1}{(in)^k}\,\widehat{f^{(k)}}(n).$$
Because $f^{(k)}$ is continuous on the circle it is bounded, so $|\widehat{f^{(k)}}(n)|\le\frac1{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^\pi|f^{(k)}|=:C$ for all $n$. Therefore
$$|\hat f(n)|\le\frac{C}{|n|^k},\qquad\text{i.e. }\hat f(n)=O(|n|^{-k}).$$
This is the quantitative half of the decay–smoothness duality: each derivative buys one power of $1/|n|$.

concepts.label: SS 2.6.11 — L¹ convergence ⟹ uniform coefficient convergence

content.statement: $L^1$ convergence of functions gives uniform convergence of coefficients.
content.formal: Let $\{f_k\}_{k=1}^{\infty}$ be Riemann integrable functions on $[0,1]$ such that $$\int_0^1|f_k(x)-f(x)|\,dx\to0\qquad(k\to\infty).$$ Show that $\widehat f_k(n)\to\widehat f(n)$ uniformly in $n$ as $k\to\infty$.
content.intuition: The Fourier coefficient is an $L^1$-bounded functional ($|\hat g(n)|\le\|g\|_1$ uniformly in $n$), so $L^1$ closeness forces uniform spectral closeness.

steps.content:
For every $n$,
$$|\hat f_k(n)-\hat f(n)|=\Big|\int_0^1\big(f_k(x)-f(x)\big)e^{-2\pi inx}\,dx\Big|\le\int_0^1|f_k(x)-f(x)|\,|e^{-2\pi inx}|\,dx=\int_0^1|f_k-f|\,dx.$$
The right-hand side is independent of $n$ and tends to $0$. Hence
$$\sup_{n\in\mathbb Z}|\hat f_k(n)-\hat f(n)|\le\int_0^1|f_k-f|\,dx\xrightarrow[k\to\infty]{}0,$$
which is exactly uniform convergence of the coefficients.

content.statement: Translate evenness/oddness/half-period/reality of $f$ into symmetries of $\hat f(n)$.
content.formal: Let $f$ be a $2\pi$-periodic Riemann integrable function on $\mathbb R$. (a) Show that its Fourier series can be rewritten as $$f(\theta)\sim \widehat f(0)+\sum_{n\ge1}"][\widehat f(n)+\widehat f(-n)]\cos n\theta+i[\widehat f(n)-\widehat f(-n)]\sin n\theta.$$ (b) Prove that if $f$ is even, then $\widehat f(n)=\widehat f(-n)$ and the expansion is a cosine series. (c) Prove that if $f$ is odd, then $\widehat f(n)=-\widehat f(-n)$ and the expansion is a sine series. (d) If $f(\theta+\pi)=f(\theta)$ for all $\theta\in\mathbb R$, show that $\widehat f(n)=0$ for every odd integer $n$. (e) Show that $f$ is real-valued if and only if $\widehat f(-n)=\overline{\widehat f(n)}$ for every $n\in\mathbb Z$.
content.intuition: Each symmetry of $f$ becomes a linear constraint on its spectrum: even↔real cosine, odd↔imaginary sine, half-period↔kills odd harmonics, real↔Hermitian coefficients.

steps.content:
Group the symmetric tails of $\sum_n\hat f(n)e^{in\theta}$ and use $e^{\pm in\theta}=\cos n\theta\pm i\sin n\theta$:
$$\hat f(n)e^{in\theta}+\hat f(-n)e^{-in\theta}=[\hat f(n)+\hat f(-n)]\cos n\theta+i[\hat f(n)-\hat f(-n)]\sin n\theta,$$
and summing over $n\ge1$ with the $n=0$ term $\hat f(0)$ gives the stated form.

If $f$ is even, substitute $\theta\mapsto-\theta$ in $\hat f(-n)=\frac1{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^\pi f(\theta)e^{in\theta}d\theta$:
$$\hat f(-n)=\frac1{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^\pi f(-\theta)e^{-in\theta}d\theta=\frac1{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^\pi f(\theta)e^{-in\theta}d\theta=\hat f(n).$$
Then the $\sin$ coefficients $i[\hat f(n)-\hat f(-n)]$ vanish: a pure cosine series.
If $f$ is odd the same substitution gives $\hat f(-n)=-\hat f(n)$, so the $\cos$ coefficients vanish: a pure sine series.

Substituting $\theta\mapsto\theta+\pi$ and using $f(\theta+\pi)=f(\theta)$,
$$\hat f(n)=\frac1{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^\pi f(\theta+\pi)e^{-in(\theta+\pi)}d\theta=e^{-in\pi}\hat f(n)=(-1)^n\hat f(n),$$
so $(1-(-1)^n)\hat f(n)=0$; for odd $n$ this forces $\hat f(n)=0$.

$f$ is real $\iff f=\overline f$. Now $\widehat{\overline f}(n)=\frac1{2\pi}\int\overline{f}e^{-in\theta}=\overline{\frac1{2\pi}\int f e^{in\theta}}=\overline{\hat f(-n)}$. Matching coefficients, $f=\overline f\iff \hat f(n)=\overline{\hat f(-n)}$, i.e. $\hat f(-n)=\overline{\hat f(n)}$.

content.statement: Compute the sine series of the plucked-string tent profile.
content.formal: Return to the plucked string from Chapter 1. Let $f$ be the initial triangular profile on $[0,\pi]$, with height $h$ at $p$, where $0<p<\pi$. Show that $f$ is equal to its Fourier sine series $$f(x)=\sum_{m=1}^{\infty}A_m\sin mx,\qquad A_m=\frac{2h\sin(mp)}{m^2p(\pi-p)}.$$ Hint: use the estimate $|A_m|\le C/m^2$.
content.intuition: A piecewise-linear pluck has a single corner, so coefficients decay like $1/m^2$ — fast enough for the sine series to reproduce $f$ exactly and uniformly.

steps.content: The plucked profile is $f(x)=\frac{h}{p}x$ on $[0,p]$ and $f(x)=\frac{h(\pi-x)}{\pi-p}$ on $[p,\pi]$, extended as an odd $2\pi$-periodic function. Its sine coefficients are $A_m=\frac2\pi\int_0^\pi f(x)\sin mx\,dx$.

Two integrals (by parts):
$$\int_0^p x\sin mx\,dx=-\frac{p\cos mp}{m}+\frac{\sin mp}{m^2},\qquad \int_p^\pi(\pi-x)\sin mx\,dx=\frac{(\pi-p)\cos mp}{m}+\frac{\sin mp}{m^2},$$
(the second via $x\mapsto\pi-u$ and $\sin(m\pi-mu)=-(-1)^m\sin mu$, with the powers of $(-1)^m$ cancelling). Therefore
$$A_m=\frac{2h}{\pi}\Big[\frac1p\Big(-\frac{p\cos mp}{m}+\frac{\sin mp}{m^2}\Big)+\frac1{\pi-p}\Big(\frac{(\pi-p)\cos mp}{m}+\frac{\sin mp}{m^2}\Big)\Big].$$
The $\cos mp/m$ terms cancel, leaving
$$A_m=\frac{2h}{\pi}\cdot\frac{\sin mp}{m^2}\Big(\frac1p+\frac1{\pi-p}\Big)=\frac{2h}{\pi}\cdot\frac{\sin mp}{m^2}\cdot\frac{\pi}{p(\pi-p)}=\frac{2h\sin mp}{m^2\,p(\pi-p)}.$$
Finally $|A_m|\le \frac{2h}{p(\pi-p)}\,\frac1{m^2}$, so $\sum_m A_m\sin mx$ converges absolutely and uniformly; $f$ is continuous (and odd), so it equals its sine series.

content.statement: Find the odd Fourier series of $\theta(\pi-\theta)$.
content.formal: Consider the $2\pi$-periodic odd function whose restriction to $[0,\pi]$ is $f(\theta)=\theta(\pi-\theta)$. (a) Draw the graph of $f$. (b) Compute the Fourier coefficients of $f$ and show that $$f(\theta)=\frac8\pi\sum_{k\ge1,\ k\ \mathrm{odd}}\frac{\sin k\theta}{k^3}.$$
content.intuition: A smooth-looking parabola arch, odd-extended, is very smooth except at the joins, giving rapid $1/k^3$ decay and only odd harmonics by the half-wave symmetry.

steps.content:
Since $f$ is odd, $f(\theta)=\sum_{k\ge1}b_k\sin k\theta$ with $b_k=\frac2\pi\int_0^\pi(\pi\theta-\theta^2)\sin k\theta\,d\theta$. Compute the two pieces:
$$\int_0^\pi\theta\sin k\theta\,d\theta=-\frac{\pi(-1)^k}{k},\qquad \int_0^\pi\theta^2\sin k\theta\,d\theta=-\frac{\pi^2(-1)^k}{k}-\frac{2\big(1-(-1)^k\big)}{k^3}.$$
Hence
$$\int_0^\pi(\pi\theta-\theta^2)\sin k\theta\,d\theta=\pi\Big(-\frac{\pi(-1)^k}{k}\Big)-\Big(-\frac{\pi^2(-1)^k}{k}-\frac{2(1-(-1)^k)}{k^3}\Big)=\frac{2(1-(-1)^k)}{k^3}.$$
So $b_k=\frac2\pi\cdot\frac{2(1-(-1)^k)}{k^3}=\frac{4(1-(-1)^k)}{\pi k^3}$, which is $0$ for even $k$ and $\frac{8}{\pi k^3}$ for odd $k$. Thus
$$f(\theta)=\frac8\pi\sum_{k\ \mathrm{odd}\ge1}\frac{\sin k\theta}{k^3}.$$
The $1/k^3$ decay gives absolute, uniform convergence; $f$ is continuous and odd, so equality holds pointwise.

content.statement: Cosine series of a triangular tent of half-width $\delta$.
content.formal: On $[-\pi,\pi]$, define the triangular tent function $$f(\theta)=\begin{cases}0,& |\theta|>\delta,\\ 1-|\theta|/\delta,& |\theta|\le\delta.\end{cases}$$ Show that $$f(\theta)=\frac{\delta}{2\pi}+2\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\frac{1-\cos(n\delta)}{n^2\pi\delta}\cos n\theta.$$
content.intuition: A narrow tent of width $\delta$: width controls the spectral envelope through the factor $1-\cos n\delta$, while the corner fixes the $1/n^2$ decay.

steps.content:
$f$ is even and supported on $[-\delta,\delta]$. The mean is $\hat f(0)=\frac1{2\pi}\int_{-\delta}^\delta(1-|\theta|/\delta)\,d\theta=\frac{1}{2\pi}\cdot\delta=\frac{\delta}{2\pi}$ (the tent has area $\delta$). For $n\neq0$, using evenness,
$$\hat f(n)=\frac1\pi\int_0^\delta\Big(1-\frac{\theta}{\delta}\Big)\cos n\theta\,d\theta.$$
Now $\int_0^\delta\cos n\theta\,d\theta=\frac{\sin n\delta}{n}$ and, by parts,
$$\int_0^\delta\frac\theta\delta\cos n\theta\,d\theta=\frac{\sin n\delta}{n}-\frac{1-\cos n\delta}{\delta n^2}.$$
Subtracting, $\int_0^\delta(1-\theta/\delta)\cos n\theta\,d\theta=\frac{1-\cos n\delta}{\delta n^2}$, so $\hat f(n)=\frac{1-\cos n\delta}{\pi\delta n^2}$. Since $f$ is even, $\hat f(n)=\hat f(-n)$ and the real series is
$$f(\theta)=\hat f(0)+2\sum_{n\ge1}\hat f(n)\cos n\theta=\frac{\delta}{2\pi}+2\sum_{n\ge1}\frac{1-\cos n\delta}{n^2\pi\delta}\cos n\theta.$$
Coefficients are $O(1/n^2)$, so convergence is uniform to the continuous $f$.

content.statement: Hölder continuity of order $\alpha$ gives sharp $O(|n|^{-\alpha})$ coefficient decay.
content.formal:
Let $f$ be $2\pi$-periodic and Riemann integrable on $[-\pi,\pi]$.

\textbf{(a)} For $n\in\mathbb Z\setminus\{0\}$, show that
$$\widehat f(n)=-\frac1{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^{\pi} f\!\left(x+\frac{\pi}{n}\right)e^{-inx}\,dx,$$
hence
$$\widehat f(n)=\frac1{4\pi}\int_{-\pi}^{\pi}\left[f(x)-f\!\left(x+\frac{\pi}{n}\right)\right]e^{-inx}\,dx.$$

\textbf{(b)} Now assume that $f$ satisfies a Hölder condition of order $\alpha$, namely
$$|f(x+h)-f(x)|\le C|h|^\alpha$$
for some $0<\alpha\le1$, some $C>0$, and all $x,h$. Use part (a) to show that
$$\widehat f(n)=O\!\left(\frac1{|n|^\alpha}\right).$$

\textbf{(c)} Prove that the above result cannot be improved by showing that
$$f(x)=\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}2^{-k\alpha}e^{i2^kx},\qquad 0<\alpha<1,$$
satisfies
$$|f(x+h)-f(x)|\le C|h|^\alpha,$$
and
$$\widehat f(N)=\frac1{N^\alpha}\qquad\text{whenever }N=2^k.$$

\textbf{Hint.} For (c), break up the sum $f(x+h)-f(x)$ into the parts $2^k\le1/|h|$ and $2^k>1/|h|$. To estimate the first sum, use $|1-e^{i\theta}|\le|\theta|$ whenever $\theta$ is small. To estimate the second sum, use $|e^{ix}-e^{iy}|\le2$.
content.intuition: The shift-by-half-period trick converts a modulus-of-continuity bound directly into coefficient decay; lacunary series saturate it, proving the exponent is exact.

steps.content:
Substituting $x\mapsto x+\pi/n$ and using $e^{-i\pi}=-1$,
$$\hat f(n)=\frac1{2\pi}\int f(x+\tfrac\pi n)e^{-in(x+\pi/n)}dx=-\frac1{2\pi}\int f(x+\tfrac\pi n)e^{-inx}dx.$$
Averaging this with the defining formula $\hat f(n)=\frac1{2\pi}\int f(x)e^{-inx}dx$,
$$\hat f(n)=\frac1{4\pi}\int_{-\pi}^\pi\big[f(x)-f(x+\tfrac\pi n)\big]e^{-inx}\,dx.$$

Hence
$$|\hat f(n)|\le\frac1{4\pi}\int_{-\pi}^\pi|f(x)-f(x+\tfrac\pi n)|\,dx\le\frac1{4\pi}\cdot2\pi\cdot C\Big(\frac\pi{|n|}\Big)^\alpha=\frac{C\pi^\alpha}{2}\,|n|^{-\alpha}=O(|n|^{-\alpha}).$$

For the lacunary series, only the term $k$ with $2^k=N$ contributes to $\hat f(N)$, giving $\hat f(2^k)=2^{-k\alpha}=N^{-\alpha}$ exactly. And $f$ is Hölder-$\alpha$: splitting $f(x+h)-f(x)=\sum_{2^k\le1/|h|}+\sum_{2^k>1/|h|}$, the first sum is $\le\sum_{2^k\le1/|h|}2^{-k\alpha}2^k|h|\le C|h|^\alpha$ (using $|1-e^{i\theta}|\le|\theta|$), the second $\le\sum_{2^k>1/|h|}2\cdot2^{-k\alpha}\le C|h|^\alpha$ (geometric). So the decay rate $|n|^{-\alpha}$ cannot be improved.

content.statement: Bounded monotone functions have $O(1/|n|)$ Fourier coefficients.
content.formal:
If $f$ is a bounded monotonic function on $[-\pi,\pi]$, then
$$\widehat f(n)=O(1/|n|).$$
Hint: One may assume that $f$ is increasing and that $|f|\le M$. First check that the Fourier coefficients of the characteristic function of $[a,b]$ satisfy $O(1/|n|)$. Then show that a step function of the form
$$\sum_{k=1}^{N}\alpha_k\chi_{[a_k,a_{k+1}]}(x),$$
where
$$-\pi=a_1<a_2<\cdots<a_N<a_{N+1}=\pi,\qquad -M\le \alpha_1\le\cdots\le\alpha_N\le M,$$
has Fourier coefficients that are $O(1/|n|)$ uniformly in $N$. Summing by parts gives a telescopic sum $\sum(\alpha_{k+1}-\alpha_k)$, which can be bounded by $2M$. Finally, approximate $f$ by functions of the above type.
content.intuition: Monotonicity is bounded variation: total variation $\le2M$ caps the telescoped step-coefficients, and a single jump's $1/|n|$ decay sets the rate.

steps.content:
Assume $f$ increasing, $|f|\le M$. First, the characteristic function of $[a,b]$ has
$$\widehat{\chi_{[a,b]}}(n)=\frac{e^{-ina}-e^{-inb}}{2\pi in},\qquad|\widehat{\chi_{[a,b]}}(n)|\le\frac1{\pi|n|},$$
so $O(1/|n|)$ with a constant independent of $a,b$. Next, a step function $s=\sum_{k=1}^N\alpha_k\chi_{[a_k,a_{k+1}]}$ with $-\pi=a_1<\dots<a_{N+1}=\pi$ and $-M\le\alpha_1\le\dots\le\alpha_N\le M$: writing $\chi_{[a_k,a_{k+1}]}=\chi_{[a_k,\pi]}-\chi_{[a_{k+1},\pi]}$ and summing by parts,
$$\hat s(n)=\sum_{k}(\alpha_k-\alpha_{k-1})\widehat{\chi_{[a_k,\pi]}}(n)+\text{(endpoint)},$$
so $|\hat s(n)|\le\frac1{\pi|n|}\sum_k(\alpha_k-\alpha_{k-1})\le\frac{2M}{\pi|n|}$ — \emph{uniformly in $N$} (the increments telescope to $\le2M$). Finally, an increasing bounded $f$ is the uniform/$L^1$ limit of such increasing step functions $s_N$; their coefficients converge to $\hat f(n)$ while the bound $|\hat s_N(n)|\le\frac{2M}{\pi|n|}$ passes to the limit. Hence $\hat f(n)=O(1/|n|)$.

content.statement: Evaluate $\zeta(4)$ and $\zeta(6)$ (and odd-index sums) via Parseval.
content.formal: Exercise 6 in Chapter 2 evaluated $\sum_{n\ge1,\ n\ \mathrm{odd}}1/n^2$ and $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}1/n^2$. Use the methods of this chapter to obtain similar sums. (a) For $f(\theta)=|\theta|$ on $[-\pi,\pi]$, use Parseval's identity to find $$\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\frac1{(2n+1)^4}=\frac{\pi^4}{96},\qquad \sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\frac1{n^4}=\frac{\pi^4}{90}.$$ (b) For the $2\pi$-periodic odd function with $f(\theta)=\theta(\pi-\theta)$ on $[0,\pi]$, show that $$\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\frac1{(2n+1)^6}=\frac{\pi^6}{960},\qquad \sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\frac1{n^6}=\frac{\pi^6}{945}.$$ Remark: even zeta values can be expressed in terms of powers of $\pi$, whereas formulas for odd zeta values such as $\sum n^{-3}$ are much subtler.
content.intuition: Parseval converts the easy integral of $f^2$ into an exact value of a zeta sum; the smoother $f$ (faster decay) reaches the higher even zeta value.

steps.content:
For $f(\theta)=|\theta|$: $\hat f(0)=\frac\pi2$ and (Ch.2 Ex.6) $\hat f(n)=\frac{(-1)^n-1}{\pi n^2}$, i.e. $-\frac{2}{\pi n^2}$ for odd $n$, $0$ for even $n\neq0$. Parseval gives
$$\frac14\pi^2+2\sum_{n\ \mathrm{odd}\ge1}\frac{4}{\pi^2n^4}=\frac1{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^\pi\theta^2\,d\theta=\frac{\pi^2}3.$$
So $\frac{8}{\pi^2}\sum_{n\ \mathrm{odd}}n^{-4}=\frac{\pi^2}3-\frac{\pi^2}4=\frac{\pi^2}{12}$, giving $\sum_{n\ \mathrm{odd}}n^{-4}=\frac{\pi^4}{96}$. Splitting even/odd, $\zeta(4)=\sum_{\mathrm{odd}}+\frac1{16}\zeta(4)$, so $\zeta(4)=\frac{16}{15}\cdot\frac{\pi^4}{96}=\frac{\pi^4}{90}$.

For the odd $f(\theta)=\theta(\pi-\theta)$, the sine coefficients are $b_k=\frac{8}{\pi k^3}$ ($k$ odd), $0$ otherwise (Ch.2 Ex.4). Parseval for a sine series, $\|f\|^2=\frac12\sum_k b_k^2$, and $\|f\|^2=\frac1{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^\pi f^2=\frac1{2\pi}\cdot\frac{\pi^5}{15}=\frac{\pi^4}{30}$ (since $\int_0^\pi\theta^2(\pi-\theta)^2d\theta=\frac{\pi^5}{30}$). Hence
$$\frac12\sum_{k\ \mathrm{odd}}\frac{64}{\pi^2k^6}=\frac{\pi^4}{30}\ \Rightarrow\ \sum_{k\ \mathrm{odd}}k^{-6}=\frac{\pi^6}{960}.$$
Splitting, $\zeta(6)=\sum_{\mathrm{odd}}+\frac1{64}\zeta(6)$, so $\zeta(6)=\frac{64}{63}\cdot\frac{\pi^6}{960}=\frac{\pi^6}{945}$.

content.statement: Derive $\sum 1/(n+\alpha)^2=\pi^2/\sin^2\pi\alpha$ from Parseval.
content.formal: For $\alpha\notin\mathbb Z$, show that the Fourier series of $$\frac{\pi}{\sin\pi\alpha}e^{i(\pi-x)\alpha}$$ on $[0,2\pi]$ is $$\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty}\frac{e^{inx}}{n+\alpha}.$$ Apply Parseval's formula to show that $$\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty}\frac1{(n+\alpha)^2}=\frac{\pi^2}{(\sin\pi\alpha)^2}.$$
content.intuition: A single complex exponential profile has a tidy spectrum $1/(n+\alpha)$; Parseval then sums its squares to the classic cosecant-squared identity.

steps.content:
With $g(x)=\frac{\pi}{\sin\pi\alpha}e^{i(\pi-x)\alpha}$,
$$c_n=\frac1{2\pi}\int_0^{2\pi}g(x)e^{-inx}dx=\frac{\pi}{\sin\pi\alpha}\cdot\frac{e^{i\pi\alpha}}{2\pi}\int_0^{2\pi}e^{-i(n+\alpha)x}dx.$$
Since $\int_0^{2\pi}e^{-i(n+\alpha)x}dx=\frac{e^{-2\pi i\alpha}-1}{-i(n+\alpha)}$ and $e^{i\pi\alpha}(e^{-2\pi i\alpha}-1)=e^{-i\pi\alpha}-e^{i\pi\alpha}=-2i\sin\pi\alpha$,
$$c_n=\frac{\pi}{\sin\pi\alpha}\cdot\frac1{2\pi}\cdot\frac{-2i\sin\pi\alpha}{-i(n+\alpha)}=\frac1{n+\alpha}.$$
So $g(x)\sim\sum_n\frac{e^{inx}}{n+\alpha}$.

$|g(x)|^2=\frac{\pi^2}{(\sin\pi\alpha)^2}$ (the exponential has modulus $1$), so
$$\sum_{n}\frac1{(n+\alpha)^2}=\sum_n|c_n|^2=\frac1{2\pi}\int_0^{2\pi}|g|^2\,dx=\frac{\pi^2}{(\sin\pi\alpha)^2}.$$
This is the partial-fractions identity for $\pi^2/\sin^2(\pi\alpha)$, recovered purely from orthonormality.

concepts.label: Term-by-Term Differentiation

content.statement: A Fourier series may be differentiated term by term when the differentiated series is sufficiently controlled.
content.definition: $$\sum_{n\in\mathbb Z}|n\widehat f(n)|<\infty\Longrightarrow \frac{d}{dx}\sum_{n\in\mathbb Z}\widehat f(n)e^{inx}=\sum_{n\in\mathbb Z}in\widehat f(n)e^{inx}.$$
content.formal: If $\sum_{n\in\mathbb Z}|n\hat f(n)|<\infty$, then $\sum_n\hat f(n)e^{inx}$ defines a $C^1$ function and may be differentiated term by term: $f'(x)=\sum_{n\in\mathbb Z}in\hat f(n)e^{inx}$, uniformly in $x$.
content.intuition: Differentiating a frequency $e^{inx}$ multiplies it by $in$. Absolute summability after that multiplication is the safety check that prevents termwise differentiation from lying to you.

assumptions: The displayed sufficient condition is stronger than necessary but clean and safe.

content.statement: A finite linear combination of Fourier harmonics.
content.formal: A trigonometric polynomial has the form $p(x)=\sum_{|n|\le N}c_ne^{inx}$ for some $N\ge0$ and complex coefficients $c_n$.
content.intuition: These are finite Fourier series: simple, smooth, periodic building blocks used to approximate more complicated functions.

concepts.label: Uniqueness Theorem

content.statement: An integrable function is determined, up to equality almost everywhere, by all of its Fourier coefficients.
content.formal: If $f\in L^1(\mathbb T)$ and $\hat f(n)=0$ for every $n\in\mathbb Z$, then $f=0$ a.e. In particular, if $f\in C(\mathbb T)$, then $f\equiv0$.
content.formula: $$f\in L^1(\mathbb T),\quad \widehat f(n)=0\ (\forall n\in\mathbb Z)\Longrightarrow f=0\ \text{a.e.}$$
content.intuition: The full frequency spectrum contains all information about the function. For continuous functions this recovers pointwise equality; for $L^1$ functions equality means equality almost everywhere.

assumptions: For $L^1$ functions, equality is interpreted almost everywhere. For continuous representatives, it becomes pointwise equality.
The $L^1$ version uses convergence of Fejér means to $f$ in $L^1(\mathbb T)$.
For continuous representatives, equality almost everywhere upgrades to pointwise equality.

steps.content: Assume $f\in L^1(\mathbb T)$ and $$\widehat f(n)=0\qquad(\forall n\in\mathbb Z).$$ Every Fourier partial sum is therefore zero: $$S_Nf(x)=\sum_{|n|\le N}\widehat f(n)e^{inx}=0.$$ Hence every Cesàro mean is also zero: $$\sigma_Nf=\frac1{N+1}\sum_{k=0}^{N}S_kf=0.$$

Fejér's theorem gives $$\sigma_Nf\xrightarrow[N\to\infty]{}f\qquad\text{in }L^1(\mathbb T).$$ But $\sigma_Nf=0$ for every $N$, so $$\|f\|_1=\|f-\sigma_Nf\|_1\xrightarrow[N\to\infty]{}0.$$ Therefore $$f=0\quad\text{a.e.}$$

If in addition $f\in C(\mathbb T)$, then $f=0$ a.e. implies $f\equiv0$. Indeed, if $f(x_0)\ne0$, continuity gives an open arc around $x_0$ on which $|f|$ is bounded away from $0$, contradicting equality to zero almost everywhere. Thus continuous functions are determined pointwise by their Fourier coefficients.

source.citation: Stein–Shakarchi: Fourier Analysis, Ch.2 Theorem 5.2 and Ch.2 uniqueness corollaries
source.ref: Stein–Shakarchi: Fourier Analysis, Ch.2 Theorem 5.2 and Ch.2 uniqueness corollaries

concepts.label: Weierstrass Function (Nowhere Differentiable)

content.statement: A continuous nowhere-differentiable periodic function can be built from a lacunary trigonometric series.
content.formal: A classical example is $W(x)=\sum_{n=0}^\infty a^n\cos(b^n x)$, where $0<a<1$, $b$ is an odd integer, and suitable conditions such as $ab>1+3\pi/2$ guarantee that $W$ is continuous and nowhere differentiable.
content.intuition: The coefficients decay enough to give continuity, but the rapidly separated frequencies keep injecting small sharp oscillations at every scale.

assumptions: The displayed condition is a classical sufficient condition, not the sharpest possible theorem.

source.citation: Stein–Shakarchi: Ch.4 Lemma 3.2; Ch.4 Lemma 3.3; Ch.4 Theorem 3.1
source.ref: Stein–Shakarchi: Ch.4 Lemma 3.2; Ch.4 Lemma 3.3; Ch.4 Theorem 3.1

concepts.label: Øving 3 · P1 — A continuous trigonometric sum

content.statement: An absolutely summable trigonometric series sums to a continuous function.
content.formal: Show that $\sum_{n\neq0}\frac1n\sin\!\big(\tfrac5n\big)e^{i2nx}$ converges and that its sum is continuous on $\mathbb S$.
content.intuition: The inner $\sin(5/n)\approx5/n$ gives the coefficients an extra power of decay ($1/n^2$), so the Weierstrass $M$-test forces absolute, uniform, continuous convergence.

steps.content:
The coefficient of $e^{i2nx}$ is $c_n=\frac1n\sin(5/n)$. Using $|\sin t|\le|t|$,
$$|c_n|=\frac1{|n|}\Big|\sin\frac5n\Big|\le\frac1{|n|}\cdot\frac{5}{|n|}=\frac{5}{n^2}.$$
Hence $\sum_{n\neq0}|c_n|\le 10\sum_{n\ge1}\frac5{n^2}<\infty$: the coefficients are absolutely summable. Each term satisfies $|c_n e^{i2nx}|=|c_n|$, so by the Weierstrass $M$-test the series converges absolutely and uniformly on $\mathbb S$. A uniform limit of continuous trigonometric terms is continuous, so the sum is continuous. $\blacksquare$

(With the literal coefficient $\frac1n\sin(5n)$ one only has $|c_n|\le1/|n|$, which gives conditional convergence to a step function via Dirichlet's test — not continuity; the $\sin(5/n)$ reading is the one consistent with the claim.)

concepts.label: Øving 5 · P1 — Fourier series of x²

content.statement: Full analysis of the Fourier series of $x^2$ (regularity, convergence, $\zeta(2)$).
content.formal: Let $f(x)=x^2$ on $[-\pi,\pi]$. (a) Show $f\sim\frac{\pi^2}3+2\sum_{n\neq0}\frac{(-1)^n}{n^2}e^{inx}$. (b) It converges to $f$ in $L^2$. (c) Show $f\in C^\infty(-\pi,\pi)$, $f$ Lipschitz on $\mathbb S$, one-sided derivatives exist everywhere, but $f\notin C^1(\mathbb S)$. (d) Pointwise convergence on $\mathbb S$. (e) Absolute and uniform convergence on $\mathbb S$.
content.intuition: A parabola, periodized, is continuous with only a corner in its derivative — exactly the regularity ($1/n^2$ decay) that makes the series converge uniformly and yields $\zeta(2)$.

steps.content:
\textbf{(a)} $\hat f(0)=\frac1{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^\pi x^2dx=\frac{\pi^2}3$, and for $n\neq0$, two integrations by parts give $\hat f(n)=\frac1{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^\pi x^2e^{-inx}dx=\frac{2(-1)^n}{n^2}$. Hence $f\sim\frac{\pi^2}3+\sum_{n\neq0}\frac{2(-1)^n}{n^2}e^{inx}$.

\textbf{(b)} $f$ is continuous (hence Riemann integrable) on the circle, so its Fourier series converges to $f$ in $L^2$ (mean-square convergence).

\textbf{(c)} On the open interval $f=x^2\in C^\infty(-\pi,\pi)$. The periodic extension is continuous ($f(-\pi)=f(\pi)=\pi^2$) and piecewise $C^1$ with $|f'(x)|=|2x|\le2\pi$, so $f$ is Lipschitz on $\mathbb S$. At the join $x=\pi$ the one-sided derivatives are $f'(\pi^-)=2\pi$ and $f'(\pi^+)=-2\pi$ (from the next period) — both exist but differ, so $f\notin C^1(\mathbb S)$.

\textbf{(d)} $f$ is continuous and at every point either differentiable or possessing one-sided derivatives (a Dini/Dirichlet condition), so $S_N f\to f$ pointwise everywhere.

\textbf{(e)} $|\hat f(n)|=2/n^2$ is summable, so by the Weierstrass $M$-test the series converges absolutely and uniformly; the limit is the continuous $f$. $\blacksquare$

\emph{Byproduct.} Evaluating at $x=\pi$: $\pi^2=\frac{\pi^2}3+2\sum_{n\ge1}\frac1{n^2}$, giving $\sum 1/n^2=\pi^2/6$.

content.statement: Coefficient decay rates control the smoothness class of $f$.
content.formal: Let $f$ be Riemann integrable on $\mathbb S$. (a) Show $|\hat f(n)|\le C/n^2$ implies $f\in C(\mathbb S)$. (b) Show $|\hat f(n)|\le e^{-|n|}$ implies $f\in C^\infty(\mathbb S)$.
content.intuition: Decay of $\hat f$ is smoothness of $f$: polynomial decay $1/n^2$ buys continuity, exponential decay buys infinite differentiability — each derivative needs one more power summable.

steps.content:
\textbf{(a)} If $|\hat f(n)|\le C/n^2$ then $\sum_n|\hat f(n)|\le|\hat f(0)|+2C\sum_{n\ge1}n^{-2}<\infty$. By the Weierstrass $M$-test $g(x)=\sum_n\hat f(n)e^{inx}$ converges uniformly to a continuous function $g$, and $\hat g(n)=\hat f(n)$ for all $n$. A Riemann-integrable function and a continuous function with the same Fourier coefficients agree (the continuous representative), so $f\in C(\mathbb S)$.

\textbf{(b)} If $|\hat f(n)|\le e^{-|n|}$ then for every $k\ge0$, $\sum_n |n|^k|\hat f(n)|\le\sum_n|n|^k e^{-|n|}<\infty$. Thus the series differentiated term by term $k$ times, $\sum_n(in)^k\hat f(n)e^{inx}$, converges uniformly; so $f\in C^k$ for every $k$, i.e. $f\in C^\infty(\mathbb S)$. $\blacksquare$

concepts.label: Abel Summation

content.statement: A summability process that damps high frequencies by $r^{|n|}$, then lets $r\to1^-$.
content.formal: For a Fourier series $\sum_{n\in\mathbb Z}\widehat f(n)e^{inx}$, its Abel means are $A_rf(x)=\sum_{n\in\mathbb Z}r^{|n|}\widehat f(n)e^{inx}$, where $0\le r<1$. Equivalently, $A_rf=P_r*f$, where $P_r$ is the Poisson kernel. Abel summability asks whether $A_rf(x)\to L$ as $r\to1^-$.
content.formula: $$\begin{aligned} A_r f(x)&=\sum_{n\in\mathbb Z}r^{|n|}\widehat f(n)e^{inx},\qquad 0\le r<1,\\ P_r(t)&=\sum_{n\in\mathbb Z}r^{|n|}e^{int}=\frac{1-r^2}{1-2r\cos t+r^2},\\ A_r f&=P_r*f. \end{aligned}$$
content.intuition: Abel summation approaches the Fourier series from inside the unit disk. The factor $r^{|n|}$ suppresses high frequencies first, then gradually releases them as $r\to1^-$.

assumptions: The circle is normalized with measure $\frac1{2\pi}dx$.
The Abel parameter satisfies $0\le r<1$ and the limiting process is $r\to1^-$.
The identity $A_rf=P_r*f$ uses the Fourier coefficients $\widehat{P_r}(n)=r^{|n|}$.
Uniform convergence of $A_rf\to f$ requires $f\in C(\mathbb T)$.
Abel summability agrees with ordinary convergence whenever the original series converges.

steps.content: Let $$f(x)\sim\sum_{n\in\mathbb Z}\widehat f(n)e^{inx}$$ be the Fourier series of $f$. For $0\le r<1$, define $$A_r f(x)=\sum_{n\in\mathbb Z}r^{|n|}\widehat f(n)e^{inx}.$$ The factor $r^{|n|}$ decays geometrically, so the Abel mean is usually better behaved than the raw Fourier series. Abel summability asks whether $$\lim_{r\to1^-}A_r f(x)$$ exists.

For $0\le r<1$, define $$P_r(t)=\sum_{n\in\mathbb Z}r^{|n|}e^{int}.$$ Splitting positive and negative frequencies gives $$P_r(t)=1+\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}r^n e^{int}+\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}r^n e^{-int}=1+2\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}r^n\cos(nt).$$ Summing the geometric series yields $$P_r(t)=\frac{1-r^2}{1-2r\cos t+r^2}.$$ Hence $P_r(t)>0$, and its normalized integral is $$\frac1{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^{\pi}P_r(t)\,dt=1.$$

Using normalized convolution, $$(P_r*f)(x)=\frac1{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^{\pi}P_r(t)f(x-t)\,dt.$$ The Fourier coefficients of the Poisson kernel are $$\widehat{P_r}(n)=r^{|n|}.$$ Since convolution multiplies Fourier coefficients, $$\widehat{P_r*f}(n)=\widehat{P_r}(n)\widehat f(n)=r^{|n|}\widehat f(n).$$ Therefore the Fourier series of $P_r*f$ is $$\sum_{n\in\mathbb Z}r^{|n|}\widehat f(n)e^{inx},$$ so $$A_r f=P_r*f.$$

The Poisson kernels form a positive approximate identity as $r\to1^-$. First, $$P_r(t)\ge0.$$ Second, $$\frac1{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^{\pi}P_r(t)\,dt=1.$$ Third, for every fixed $0<\delta\le\pi$, the mass away from $0$ tends to zero. Indeed, on $\delta\le |t|\le\pi$, the denominator $$1-2r\cos t+r^2$$ stays bounded below by a positive constant as $r\to1^-$, while the numerator $1-r^2$ tends to $0$. Hence $$\frac1{2\pi}\int_{\delta\le |t|\le\pi}P_r(t)\,dt\xrightarrow[r\to1^-]{}0.$$ Thus $P_r$ is a good kernel.

Assume $f\in C(\mathbb T)$. Since $\mathbb T$ is compact, $f$ is uniformly continuous and bounded. By normalization of $P_r$, $$A_r f(x)-f(x)=\frac1{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^{\pi}\bigl(f(x-t)-f(x)\bigr)P_r(t)\,dt.$$ Let $\varepsilon>0$. Choose $0<\delta\le\pi$ such that $$|t|<\delta\Longrightarrow |f(x-t)-f(x)|<\frac{\varepsilon}{2}\quad\text{for all }x\in\mathbb T.$$ Let $M=\|f\|_\infty$. Since $P_r\ge0$, split the integral into $|t|<\delta$ and $\delta\le |t|\le\pi$. The near part is at most $\varepsilon/2$. The far part is bounded by $$2M\frac1{2\pi}\int_{\delta\le |t|\le\pi}P_r(t)\,dt,$$ which tends to $0$ as $r\to1^-$. Therefore $$\|A_r f-f\|_\infty\xrightarrow[r\to1^-]{}0.$$

Let $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}a_n$ converge to $S$, and let $$s_N=\sum_{n=0}^{N}a_n.$$ For $0\le r<1$, Abel's summation identity gives $$\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}a_n r^n=(1-r)\sum_{N=0}^{\infty}s_N r^N.$$ Since $s_N\to S$, write $$s_N=S+(s_N-S).$$ Then $$(1-r)\sum_{N=0}^{\infty}s_N r^N=S+(1-r)\sum_{N=0}^{\infty}(s_N-S)r^N.$$ The second term tends to $0$ as $r\to1^-$ by a finite-tail argument: first choose $K$ so that $|s_N-S|$ is small for $N\ge K$, then absorb the finite part $N<K$ using the factor $1-r$. Hence $$\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}a_n r^n\xrightarrow[r\to1^-]{}S.$$ Therefore Abel summability agrees with ordinary convergence whenever the original series converges.

concepts.label: Abel's Theorem

content.statement: Ordinary convergence implies Abel summability to the same value.
content.formal: If $\sum_{n=0}^\infty a_n=s$ converges, then $\lim_{r\to1^-}\sum_{n=0}^\infty a_nr^n=s$. For Fourier series, Abel means agree with the ordinary limit wherever ordinary convergence is already valid.
content.intuition: Abel summation is consistent: the gentler radial limiting process does not change answers that already converge honestly.

assumptions: The displayed scalar form is one-sided in $r$, with $0\le r<1$.

content.statement: If the Fourier coefficients are absolutely summable, the Fourier series converges absolutely and uniformly; for continuous $f$ the limit is $f$.
content.formal: If $\sum_{n\in\mathbb Z}|\hat f(n)|<\infty$ then $\sum_n \hat f(n)e^{inx}$ converges absolutely and uniformly to a continuous limit $g$. If moreover $f\in C(\mathbb T)$ (or $f$ is continuous), then $g=f$, so $S_N[f]\to f$ uniformly.
content.formula: $$\sum_{n\in\mathbb Z}|\hat f(n)|<\infty\ \Longrightarrow\ S_N[f]\rightrightarrows g\in C(\mathbb T),\quad\text{and}\quad f\in C(\mathbb T)\Rightarrow g=f.$$
content.intuition: The Weierstrass M-test: bound each harmonic by $|\hat f(n)|$; a summable bound forces uniform convergence. Uniqueness pins the limit to $f$.

source.citation: Stein–Shakarchi: Ch.2 §1, Ch.2 Cor. 2.3–2.4 (Weierstrass M-test + uniqueness)
source.ref: Stein–Shakarchi: Ch.2 §1, Ch.2 Cor. 2.3–2.4 (Weierstrass M-test + uniqueness)

concepts.label: Carleson's Theorem

content.statement: Fourier series of $L^2$ functions converge almost everywhere.
content.formal: If $f\in L^2(\mathbb T)$, then $S_Nf(x)\to f(x)$ for almost every $x\in\mathbb T$. Hunt extended this to $L^p(\mathbb T)$ for $1<p<\infty$.
content.intuition: Despite bad kernels and possible pointwise chaos, square-integrability is still strong enough to force convergence at almost every point. The proof is deep; the statement is not a routine kernel argument.

assumptions: Almost everywhere is with respect to Lebesgue measure on $\mathbb T$.

concepts.label: Cesàro Summation

content.statement: A summability process that replaces partial sums by their arithmetic averages.
content.formal: For a Fourier series with partial sums $S_N f$, the Cesàro means are $\sigma_N f(x)=\frac1{N+1}\sum_{n=0}^{N}S_n f(x)$. Equivalently, $\sigma_N f=F_N*f$, where $F_N$ is the Fejér kernel.
content.formula: $$\begin{aligned} \sigma_N f(x)&=\frac1{N+1}\sum_{n=0}^{N}S_n f(x),\\ S_n f&=D_n*f,\\ \sigma_N f&=F_N*f,\\ F_N&=\frac1{N+1}\sum_{n=0}^{N}D_n. \end{aligned}$$
content.intuition: Instead of trusting the last partial sum, Cesàro summation averages all earlier partial sums. This damps oscillation and replaces the unstable Dirichlet kernel by the positive Fejér kernel.

assumptions: The circle is normalized with measure $\frac1{2\pi}dx$.
The convolution identity uses $S_Nf=D_N*f$.
Cesàro summability is weaker than ordinary convergence: ordinary convergence implies Cesàro convergence, but not conversely.
Fejér's theorem gives the main Fourier convergence result for Cesàro means.

steps.content: Let $S_N f$ denote the $N$-th Fourier partial sum of $f$. The Cesàro mean is the arithmetic average of the first $N+1$ partial sums: $$\sigma_N f(x)=\frac1{N+1}\sum_{n=0}^{N}S_n f(x).$$ Thus Cesàro summation does not inspect only the final partial sum $S_N f$; it averages the whole initial sequence $S_0 f,S_1 f,\dots,S_N f$.

Work on $\mathbb T=\mathbb R/2\pi\mathbb Z$ with normalized convolution $$(f*g)(x)=\frac1{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^{\pi}f(x-t)g(t)\,dt.$$ Since Fourier partial sums satisfy $$S_n f=D_n*f,$$ where $D_n$ is the Dirichlet kernel, linearity gives $$\sigma_N f=\frac1{N+1}\sum_{n=0}^{N}(D_n*f)=\left(\frac1{N+1}\sum_{n=0}^{N}D_n\right)*f.$$ Define the Fejér kernel by $$F_N=\frac1{N+1}\sum_{n=0}^{N}D_n.$$ Therefore $$\sigma_N f=F_N*f.$$

Let $(s_N)$ be a sequence with $s_N\to L$, and define $$a_N=\frac1{N+1}\sum_{n=0}^{N}s_n.$$ Then $$a_N-L=\frac1{N+1}\sum_{n=0}^{N}(s_n-L).$$ Given $\varepsilon>0$, choose $K$ such that $|s_n-L|<\varepsilon/2$ for all $n\ge K$. Then $$|a_N-L|\le \frac1{N+1}\sum_{n=0}^{K-1}|s_n-L|+\frac1{N+1}\sum_{n=K}^{N}|s_n-L|.$$ The first term tends to $0$ because it is a fixed finite sum divided by $N+1$, while the second term is at most $\varepsilon/2$ for all large $N$. Hence $a_N\to L$. Therefore ordinary convergence implies Cesàro convergence.

For Fourier series, ordinary partial sums use the Dirichlet kernel $D_N$, which oscillates and does not form a positive approximate identity. Cesàro summation replaces $D_N$ by the Fejér kernel $$F_N(t)=\frac1{N+1}\left(\frac{\sin((N+1)t/2)}{\sin(t/2)}\right)^2.$$ This kernel satisfies $$F_N(t)\ge0,\qquad \frac1{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^{\pi}F_N(t)\,dt=1,$$ and its mass concentrates near $0$ as $N\to\infty$. Thus $F_N*f$ behaves like a stable local average of $f$, which is the mechanism behind Fejér's theorem.

concepts.label: Dini's Criterion

content.statement: A local Dini condition guarantees pointwise convergence at a chosen point.
content.formal: If $f\in L^1(\mathbb T)$ and $\int_{-\pi}^{\pi}\left|\frac{f(x_0+t)-f(x_0)}{t}\right|\,dt<\infty$, then $S_Nf(x_0)\to f(x_0)$.
content.intuition: The condition measures whether the local error $f(x_0+t)-f(x_0)$ is tame enough to survive division by $t$. It is a pointwise regularity test, not a global smoothness assumption.

assumptions: The value $f(x_0)$ must be specified; the condition is local at $x_0$.

content.statement: The convolution kernel for ordinary Fourier partial sums.
content.definition: $$\begin{aligned} D_N(x)&=\sum_{|n|\le N}e^{inx},\\ D_N(x)&=\frac{\sin((N+\frac12)x)}{\sin(x/2)},\qquad x\notin2\pi\mathbb Z,\\ D_N(2\pi k)&=2N+1,\qquad k\in\mathbb Z. \end{aligned}$$
content.formal: $D_N(x)=\sum_{|n|\le N}e^{inx}=\frac{\sin((N+\frac12)x)}{\sin(x/2)}$, with removable value $D_N(0)=2N+1$. For $f\in L^1(\mathbb T)$, $S_Nf=D_N*f$.
content.intuition: $D_N$ reconstructs by sharp frequency cutoff. Its oscillations and growing $L^1$-norm make ordinary partial sums unstable compared with Fejér averages.

concepts.label: Dirichlet's Convergence Theorem

content.statement: Piecewise smooth functions have Fourier partial sums converging to the midpoint of each jump.
content.formal: If $f$ is $2\pi$-periodic and piecewise $C^1$, then $S_Nf(x)\to\frac12(f(x^+)+f(x^-))$ at every $x$. In particular, at points of continuity, $S_Nf(x)\to f(x)$.
content.intuition: Fourier series cannot choose one side of a jump, so the symmetric oscillations settle on the average of the two one-sided limits.

assumptions: Uses a periodic piecewise $C^1$ convention with one-sided limits at jumps.

concepts.label: Du Bois-Reymond Counterexample

content.statement: There exists a continuous periodic function whose Fourier partial sums diverge at a point.
content.formal: There is $f\in C(\mathbb T)$ such that the sequence $S_Nf(x_0)$ diverges for some $x_0\in\mathbb T$; in stronger versions, $\sup_N|S_Nf(x_0)|=\infty$.
content.intuition: Continuity alone does not control the Dirichlet kernel strongly enough. This counterexample explains why Fejer averaging is not just a cosmetic improvement.

assumptions: The counterexample concerns ordinary Fourier partial sums, not Cesaro or Abel means.

content.statement: For four functions on the circle, decide which of: uniform / $L^2$ / pointwise-at-0 / Cesàro convergence hold.
content.intuition: $L^2$ convergence is free for integrable $f$; pointwise/uniform need smoothness or absolute summability; at a jump everything converges to the midpoint, not the assigned value.

steps.content: (i) \emph{Mean-square convergence} holds for every Riemann-integrable $f$ — so \textbf{B is true in all four rows}. (ii) \emph{Absolutely summable coefficients} on a continuous $f$ ⟹ uniform (hence pointwise) convergence to $f$. (iii) \emph{Pointwise/Fejér:} if $f$ is integrable and differentiable at $x_0$ then $S_N[f](x_0)\to f(x_0)$; and (Fejér) if the one-sided limits $f(x_0^\pm)$ exist then the Cesàro means converge to $\tfrac12(f(x_0^+)+f(x_0^-))$. Crucially, \emph{mere continuity at a point — or even everywhere — does not give pointwise or uniform convergence} (du Bois-Reymond).

(RI, continuous only at $0$): A ✗ ($f$ may be discontinuous elsewhere, and a uniform limit of continuous functions is continuous), B ✓, C ✗ (continuity at a point is not enough), D ✓ (Fejér: $f(0^\pm)=1$). $\Rightarrow$ \textbf{B, D}.
(also $f'(0)$ exists): adds C ✓ (differentiability at $0$ forces $S_N(0)\to f(0)=1$); A still ✗. $\Rightarrow$ \textbf{B, C, D}.
($f$ continuous, $\sum|\hat f(n)|<\infty$): uniform convergence to $f$, so A ✓, and then B, C ✓; a convergent series is Cesàro summable to the same value, D ✓. $\Rightarrow$ \textbf{A, B, C, D}.

($f=\mathbf 1_{[-\pi,0]}$, a jump at $0$): $f(0^-)=1,\ f(0^+)=0$. Both the partial sums (Dirichlet) and the Cesàro means (Fejér) converge to the average $\tfrac12\neq1$, so C ✗ and D ✗; A ✗ (discontinuous), B ✓. $\Rightarrow$ \textbf{B}.

content.statement: The nonnegative Cesàro average of Dirichlet kernels.
content.definition: $$\begin{aligned} F_N(x)&=\frac1{N+1}\sum_{n=0}^{N}D_n(x),\\ F_N(x)&=\frac1{N+1}\left(\frac{\sin((N+1)x/2)}{\sin(x/2)}\right)^2,\qquad x\notin2\pi\mathbb Z,\\ F_N(2\pi k)&=N+1,\qquad k\in\mathbb Z. \end{aligned}$$
content.formal: $F_N(x)=\frac1{N+1}\sum_{n=0}^{N}D_n(x)=\frac1{N+1}\left(\frac{\sin((N+1)x/2)}{\sin(x/2)}\right)^2\ge0$, with $F_N(0)=N+1$, and $\sigma_Nf=F_N*f$.
content.intuition: Averaging the unstable Dirichlet kernels cancels their worst oscillations. Positivity and mass concentration make $F_N$ a genuine approximate identity.

concepts.label: Fejér's Theorem

content.statement: The Cesàro means of the Fourier series of a continuous periodic function converge uniformly to the function.
content.formal: If $f\in C(\mathbb T)$, then $\sigma_N f=F_N*f\to f$ uniformly on $\mathbb T$. Equivalently, $\|\sigma_N f-f\|_\infty\to0$. More generally, if $f\in L^1(\mathbb T)$, then $\sigma_N f\to f$ in $L^1$.
content.intuition: Ordinary Fourier partial sums use the oscillatory Dirichlet kernel, which can behave badly. Fejér averaging replaces it by the positive Fejér kernel, so reconstruction becomes a stable local average that sharpens into the original function.

assumptions: The circle is normalized with measure $\frac1{2\pi}dx$.
Uniform convergence requires $f\in C(\mathbb T)$.
The $L^1$ variant uses translation continuity in $L^1(\mathbb T)$.
The conclusion concerns Cesàro means, not ordinary Fourier partial sums.

steps.content: Work on $\mathbb T=\mathbb R/2\pi\mathbb Z$, with normalized convolution $(f*g)(x)=\frac1{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^{\pi}f(x-t)g(t)\,dt$. For $f\in L^1(\mathbb T)$, the $N$-th Fourier partial sum satisfies $S_N f=D_N*f$, where $D_N$ is the Dirichlet kernel. The Fejér mean is the arithmetic average of the first partial sums: $$\sigma_N f=\frac1{N+1}\sum_{k=0}^{N}S_k f.$$ By linearity of convolution, $$\sigma_N f=\frac1{N+1}\sum_{k=0}^{N}(D_k*f)=\left(\frac1{N+1}\sum_{k=0}^{N}D_k\right)*f=F_N*f.$$ Thus Fejér means are convolution averages against the Fejér kernel.

The Fejér kernel is $$F_N(t)=\frac1{N+1}\sum_{k=0}^{N}D_k(t)=\frac1{N+1}\left(\frac{\sin((N+1)t/2)}{\sin(t/2)}\right)^2,$$ with removable value $F_N(0)=N+1$. Hence $F_N(t)\ge0$. Also, since $D_k$ has normalized integral $1$, averaging gives $$\frac1{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^{\pi}F_N(t)\,dt=1.$$ Finally, for each fixed $0<\delta\le\pi$, the mass of $F_N$ away from $0$ tends to zero. Indeed, on $\delta\le |t|\le\pi$, we have $\sin^2(t/2)\ge m_\delta>0$, so $$0\le \frac1{2\pi}\int_{\delta\le |t|\le\pi}F_N(t)\,dt\le \frac1{2\pi}\int_{\delta\le |t|\le\pi}\frac1{(N+1)m_\delta}\,dt\xrightarrow[N\to\infty]{}0.$$ Therefore $\{F_N\}$ is a positive approximate identity.

Now assume $f\in C(\mathbb T)$. Since $\mathbb T$ is compact, $f$ is uniformly continuous and bounded. Let $\varepsilon>0$. Choose $0<\delta\le\pi$ such that $$|t|<\delta\Longrightarrow |f(x-t)-f(x)|<\frac{\varepsilon}{2}\quad\text{for all }x\in\mathbb T.$$ Let $M=\|f\|_\infty$. Using $\frac1{2\pi}\int F_N=1$, we write $$\sigma_N f(x)-f(x)=\frac1{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^{\pi}\bigl(f(x-t)-f(x)\bigr)F_N(t)\,dt.$$ Since $F_N\ge0$, $$|\sigma_N f(x)-f(x)|\le \frac1{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^{\pi}|f(x-t)-f(x)|F_N(t)\,dt.$$ Split the integral into $|t|<\delta$ and $\delta\le |t|\le\pi$. On the near region, uniform continuity gives $$\frac1{2\pi}\int_{|t|<\delta}|f(x-t)-f(x)|F_N(t)\,dt\le \frac{\varepsilon}{2}\frac1{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^{\pi}F_N(t)\,dt=\frac{\varepsilon}{2}.$$ On the far region, boundedness gives $|f(x-t)-f(x)|\le2M$, hence $$\frac1{2\pi}\int_{\delta\le |t|\le\pi}|f(x-t)-f(x)|F_N(t)\,dt\le 2M\frac1{2\pi}\int_{\delta\le |t|\le\pi}F_N(t)\,dt.$$ By the tail property of $F_N$, choose $N_0$ such that for all $N\ge N_0$, this far-region term is less than $\varepsilon/2$. Therefore, for all $x\in\mathbb T$ and all $N\ge N_0$, $$|\sigma_N f(x)-f(x)|<\varepsilon.$$ Hence $\|\sigma_N f-f\|_\infty\to0$, proving the uniform convergence statement.

For the $L^1$ version, let $f\in L^1(\mathbb T)$, with $\|h\|_1=\frac1{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^{\pi}|h(x)|\,dx$. Since translations are continuous in $L^1$, for every $\varepsilon>0$ there exists $\delta>0$ such that $|t|<\delta$ implies $\|f(\cdot-t)-f\|_1<\varepsilon/2$. Using positivity and normalization of $F_N$, $$\|F_N*f-f\|_1\le \frac1{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^{\pi}\|f(\cdot-t)-f\|_1F_N(t)\,dt.$$ Split again into $|t|<\delta$ and $\delta\le |t|\le\pi$. The near part is at most $\varepsilon/2$. The far part is bounded by $$2\|f\|_1\frac1{2\pi}\int_{\delta\le |t|\le\pi}F_N(t)\,dt,$$ which tends to $0$. Hence $\|F_N*f-f\|_1\to0$. Since $\sigma_N f=F_N*f$, this proves $\sigma_N f\to f$ in $L^1(\mathbb T)$.

concepts.label: Gibbs Phenomenon

content.statement: Fourier partial sums have a persistent overshoot near jump discontinuities.
content.formal: For a piecewise smooth function with a jump of size $J$, the overshoot of $S_Nf$ near the jump approaches $GJ$, where $G=\frac1\pi\int_0^\pi\frac{\sin t}{t}\,dt-\frac12\approx0.08949$. The overshoot region narrows as $N\to\infty$, but its height does not vanish.
content.intuition: Truncating frequencies cannot draw a sharp jump cleanly. The ringing gets squeezed toward the discontinuity, but the first overshoot keeps roughly nine percent of the jump.

assumptions: The standard statement concerns Fourier partial sums near a jump of a piecewise smooth function.

concepts.label: Good Kernel (Approximate Identity)

content.statement: A normalized family of kernels whose mass concentrates near $0$, so convolution approximates the identity.
content.formal: A family $\{K_n\}$ on $\mathbb T$ is a good kernel if $\frac1{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^{\pi}K_n(x)\,dx=1$, $\frac1{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^{\pi}|K_n(x)|\,dx\le M$, and for every $\delta>0$, $\frac1{2\pi}\int_{\delta\le |x|\le\pi}|K_n(x)|\,dx\to0$.
content.intuition: A good kernel is a controlled bump whose mass collapses to the origin. Convolution replaces $f(x)$ by a weighted local average near $x$, and the average becomes exact in the limit.

assumptions: Kernels are normalized using the circle measure $\frac1{2\pi}dx$.

source.citation: Stein–Shakarchi: Ch.2 Theorem 4.1; Ch.5 Corollary 1.7; Ch.5 Theorem 1.6
source.ref: Stein–Shakarchi: Ch.2 Theorem 4.1; Ch.5 Corollary 1.7; Ch.5 Theorem 1.6

concepts.label: Lacunary Series

content.statement: A power series or Fourier series whose nonzero terms occur only at a sparse sequence of exponents or frequencies.
content.formal: A lacunary series is a series whose active exponents or frequencies form a strictly increasing sequence with large gaps. In the standard Hadamard-gap case, the frequencies satisfy $$\lambda_1<\lambda_2<\cdots,\qquad \frac{\lambda_{k+1}}{\lambda_k}\ge q>1.$$ For Fourier analysis, the typical form is $$\sum_{k=1}^{\infty} a_k e^{i\lambda_k x}$$ or its real trigonometric version.
content.intuition: A lacunary series is a series with most of its possible terms missing. Instead of using every frequency, it jumps through a sparse list such as $$1,2,4,8,16,\ldots$$. These gaps make the series behave less like an ordinary Fourier series and more like a sum of weakly independent oscillations. In complex analysis, the gaps can force a natural boundary; in Fourier analysis, they strongly affect almost everywhere convergence and probabilistic behavior.
notation: $$\sum_{k=1}^{\infty} a_k z^{\lambda_k},\qquad \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} a_k e^{i\lambda_k x},\qquad \lambda_{k+1}/\lambda_k\ge q>1.$$

assumptions: $$\lambda_k\in\mathbb N,\qquad \lambda_1<\lambda_2<\cdots.$$
$$\exists q>1\ \text{ such that }\ \lambda_{k+1}/\lambda_k\ge q\ \text{ for all }k.$$

concepts.label: Mean-Square Convergence

content.statement: Fourier partial sums converge to every $L^2$ function in mean square.
content.formal: For every $f\in L^2(\mathbb T)$, $\|f-S_Nf\|_2\to0$ as $N\to\infty$. Equivalently, the trigonometric system $\{e^{inx}\}_{n\in\mathbb Z}$ is complete in $L^2(\mathbb T)$.
content.intuition: In $L^2$, Fourier series are orthogonal projections onto larger and larger frequency subspaces. Completeness says those shadows converge back to the original object in energy.

assumptions: Convergence is in the $L^2$-norm, not necessarily pointwise.

source.citation: Stein–Shakarchi: Ch.3 Lemma 2.4; Ch.3 Lemma 3.2; Ch.3 Theorem 1.1
source.ref: Stein–Shakarchi: Ch.3 Lemma 2.4; Ch.3 Lemma 3.2; Ch.3 Theorem 1.1

concepts.label: Pointwise Convergence Problem

content.statement: The question whether Fourier partial sums converge at a specific point.
content.formal: Given $f\in L^1(\mathbb T)$ and $x_0\in\mathbb T$, ask whether $S_Nf(x_0)=(D_N*f)(x_0)$ has a limit, and whether that limit equals $f(x_0)$ or the jump average $\frac12(f(x_0^+)+f(x_0^-))$.
content.intuition: Pointwise convergence is delicate because the Dirichlet kernel is oscillatory and not a good approximate identity. Local regularity can tame it; mere continuity cannot.

assumptions: The value $f(x_0)$ must be meaningful; for discontinuities one usually compares with the average of one-sided limits.

content.statement: The positive approximate identity behind Abel summation and harmonic extension to the disk.
content.formal: For $0\le r<1$, $P_r(\theta)=\sum_{n\in\mathbb Z}r^{|n|}e^{in\theta}=\frac{1-r^2}{1-2r\cos\theta+r^2}$, with $P_r\ge0$ and $\frac1{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^{\pi}P_r(\theta)\,d\theta=1$. As $r\to1^-$, $P_r*f\to f$ for $f\in C(\mathbb T)$.
content.intuition: $P_r*f$ is the harmonic extension of boundary data into the disk. Letting $r\to1^-$ approaches the boundary from inside, smoothing first and recovering later.

content.statement: A Tauberian converse: Cesàro + $o(1/n)$ implies convergence.
content.formal: Tauber's theorem, Cesàro version. If $\sum c_n$ is Cesàro summable to $\sigma$ and $c_n=o(1/n)$, equivalently $nc_n\to0$, prove that $\sum c_n$ converges to $\sigma$. Hint: use $$s_n-\sigma_n=\frac{(n-1)c_n+(n-2)c_{n-1}+\cdots+c_2}{n},$$ with the appropriate convention for partial sums and Cesàro means.
content.intuition: Cesàro averaging can only smear, not invent, a limit: a mild decay condition on the terms forces the averaged limit back onto the genuine partial sums.

steps.content:
Let $s_n=\sum_{k=1}^n c_k$ and $\sigma_n=\frac1n\sum_{k=1}^n s_k\to\sigma$. A direct computation rearranges the Cesàro mean:
$$\sigma_n=\frac1n\sum_{k=1}^n s_k=\frac1n\sum_{j=1}^n(n-j+1)c_j\quad\Longrightarrow\quad s_n-\sigma_n=\frac1n\sum_{j=1}^n(j-1)c_j.$$
Set $a_j=(j-1)c_j$. By hypothesis $jc_j\to0$, hence $a_j\to0$. The Cesàro mean of a null sequence is null:
$$\frac1n\sum_{j=1}^n a_j\to0,$$
so $s_n-\sigma_n\to0$. Since $\sigma_n\to\sigma$, we conclude $s_n\to\sigma$, i.e. $\sum c_n=\sigma$.
The Tauberian side condition $nc_n\to0$ is what blocks pathologies (e.g. $\sum(-1)^n$ is Cesàro summable but divergent — there $nc_n\not\to0$).

concepts.label: SS 2.6.15 — Closed form of the Fejér kernel

content.statement: Derive the squared-sine closed form of the Fejér kernel.
content.formal: Prove that the Fejér kernel is given by $$F_N(x)=\frac1N\frac{\sin^2(Nx/2)}{\sin^2(x/2)}.$$ Hint: use $NF_N(x)=D_0(x)+\cdots+D_{N-1}(x)$, where $D_n$ is the Dirichlet kernel; if $\omega=e^{ix}$, then $$NF_N(x)=\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}\frac{\omega^{-n}-\omega^{n+1}}{1-\omega}.$$
content.intuition: Averaging the oscillating Dirichlet kernels produces a perfect square — positivity is exactly why Cesàro means behave where raw partial sums fail.

steps.content:
By definition $N F_N(x)=\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}D_n(x)$ where $D_n(x)=\sum_{k=-n}^n e^{ikx}=\dfrac{\sin((n+\tfrac12)x)}{\sin(x/2)}$. Hence
$$N F_N(x)=\frac1{\sin(x/2)}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}\sin\big((n+\tfrac12)x\big).$$
Evaluate the sum as an imaginary part of a geometric series ($\omega=e^{ix}$):
$$\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}\sin\big((n+\tfrac12)x\big)=\operatorname{Im}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}\omega^{\,n+1/2}=\operatorname{Im}\frac{\omega^{1/2}(\omega^N-1)}{\omega-1}=\operatorname{Im}\frac{e^{iNx}-1}{e^{ix/2}-e^{-ix/2}}=\frac{1-\cos Nx}{2\sin(x/2)}=\frac{\sin^2(Nx/2)}{\sin(x/2)},$$
using $1-\cos Nx=2\sin^2(Nx/2)$. Therefore
$$N F_N(x)=\frac{\sin^2(Nx/2)}{\sin^2(x/2)},\qquad F_N(x)=\frac1N\frac{\sin^2(Nx/2)}{\sin^2(x/2)}.$$
In particular $F_N\ge0$, $\frac1{2\pi}\int F_N=1$, and the mass concentrates at $0$ — the three properties making $\{F_N\}$ a good kernel.

content.statement: Prove Weierstrass approximation from Fejér's theorem.
content.formal: Prove the Weierstrass approximation theorem: if $f$ is continuous on a closed bounded interval $[a,b]\subset\mathbb R$, then for every $\varepsilon>0$ there is a polynomial $P$ such that $$\sup_{x\in[a,b]}|f(x)-P(x)|<\varepsilon.$$ Prove this by applying Corollary 5.4 of Fejér's theorem and using that $e^{ix}$ can be approximated uniformly by polynomials on any interval.
content.intuition: Two approximations in series: Fejér makes trig polynomials dense in continuous functions, and Taylor makes ordinary polynomials dense in trig polynomials.

steps.content: Rescale so that $[a,b]\subset(-\pi,\pi)$, say into $[-\pi+\eta,\pi-\eta]$, and extend $f$ to a continuous $2\pi$-periodic function $g$ (taper to $0$ near $\pm\pi$). Let $\varepsilon>0$.

By Fejér's theorem (Cor. 5.4), the Cesàro means $\sigma_N(g)$ converge uniformly to $g$ on the circle; so there is a trigonometric polynomial $T(x)=\sum_{|k|\le M}c_ke^{ikx}$ with $\sup_x|g(x)-T(x)|<\varepsilon/2$.

Each $e^{ikx}=\sum_{j\ge0}\frac{(ikx)^j}{j!}$ is entire; its Taylor partial sums converge uniformly on the bounded interval $[a,b]$. Choose a polynomial $P$ with $\sup_{[a,b]}|T(x)-P(x)|<\varepsilon/2$ (approximate each of the finitely many exponentials).
On $[a,b]$, $|f-P|\le|g-T|+|T-P|<\varepsilon$.
This deduces the polynomial (real-analysis) density theorem from the trigonometric one, with Fejér's positive kernel doing the analytic work.

concepts.label: SS 2.6.17b — Cesàro sum at a jump

content.statement: Fejér means converge to the midpoint at a jump discontinuity.
content.formal: Assume $f$ is integrable and has a jump discontinuity at $\theta$, meaning the one-sided limits $$f(\theta^+)=\lim_{h\to0^+}f(\theta+h),\qquad f(\theta^-)=\lim_{h\to0^+}f(\theta-h)$$ exist. Using the same type of argument as for Abel means at a jump, show that the Fourier series of $f$ at $\theta$ is Cesàro summable to $$\frac{f(\theta^+)+f(\theta^-)}2.$$
content.intuition: Fejér's positive, concentrating kernel reads off the symmetric average of the two one-sided values — so Cesàro summation tames jumps where ordinary partial sums (Gibbs) overshoot.

steps.content:
The Cesàro mean is $\sigma_N(f)(\theta)=(f*F_N)(\theta)=\frac1{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^\pi f(\theta-t)F_N(t)\,dt$. Since $F_N$ is even and $\frac1{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^\pi F_N=1$, we have $\frac1{2\pi}\int_0^\pi F_N=\frac1{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^0F_N=\tfrac12$. Split the convolution into $t>0$ and $t<0$ (substituting $t\mapsto-t$ in the latter):
$$\sigma_N(f)(\theta)=\frac1{2\pi}\int_0^\pi F_N(t)\big[f(\theta-t)+f(\theta+t)\big]\,dt.$$
Subtract the target using the half-masses:
$$\sigma_N(f)(\theta)-\frac{f(\theta^+)+f(\theta^-)}2=\frac1{2\pi}\int_0^\pi F_N(t)\Big[\big(f(\theta-t)-f(\theta^-)\big)+\big(f(\theta+t)-f(\theta^+)\big)\Big]dt.$$
Fix $\varepsilon>0$. By the one-sided limits choose $\delta$ so the bracket is $<\varepsilon$ for $0<t<\delta$; that part contributes $\le\varepsilon\cdot\frac1{2\pi}\int_0^\pi F_N\le\varepsilon$. On $\delta\le t\le\pi$, $F_N(t)=\frac1N\frac{\sin^2(Nt/2)}{\sin^2(t/2)}\le\frac1{N\sin^2(\delta/2)}\to0$ uniformly, so (with $f$ integrable, hence the bracket integrable) that part $\to0$. Thus $\limsup_N|\sigma_N(f)(\theta)-\tfrac12(f(\theta^+)+f(\theta^-))|\le\varepsilon$ for all $\varepsilon$, giving the claim.

concepts.label: SS 2.6.7 — Summation by parts & Dirichlet's test

content.statement: Summation by parts and the resulting Dirichlet convergence test.
content.formal: Let $\{a_n\}_{n=1}^{N}$ and $\{b_n\}_{n=1}^{N}$ be finite complex sequences, and let $B_k=\sum_{n=1}^{k}b_n$ with $B_0=0$. (a) Prove the summation-by-parts formula $$\sum_{n=M}^{N}a_nb_n=a_NB_N-a_MB_{M-1}-\sum_{n=M}^{N-1}(a_{n+1}-a_n)B_n.$$ (b) Deduce Dirichlet's test: if the partial sums of $\sum b_n$ are bounded and $\{a_n\}$ is a real sequence decreasing monotonically to $0$, then $\sum a_nb_n$ converges.
content.intuition: The discrete integration-by-parts trades oscillation (bounded $B_k$) against monotone decay ($a_n\downarrow0$) — the discrete analogue of $\int u\,dv$ and the engine behind conditional convergence.

steps.content:
Write $b_n=B_n-B_{n-1}$. Then
$$\sum_{n=M}^N a_nb_n=\sum_{n=M}^N a_n(B_n-B_{n-1})=\sum_{n=M}^N a_nB_n-\sum_{n=M-1}^{N-1}a_{n+1}B_n.$$
Peel the top term $a_NB_N$ from the first sum and the bottom term $a_MB_{M-1}$ from the second; the overlapping range $M\le n\le N-1$ gives $\sum_{n=M}^{N-1}(a_n-a_{n+1})B_n$. Hence
$$\sum_{n=M}^N a_nb_n=a_NB_N-a_MB_{M-1}-\sum_{n=M}^{N-1}(a_{n+1}-a_n)B_n.$$

Suppose $|B_k|\le L$ for all $k$ and $a_n\downarrow0$. In (a) with $M$ fixed and $N\to\infty$: the term $a_NB_N\to0$ (since $a_N\to0$, $|B_N|\le L$). The series $\sum_{n\ge M}(a_{n+1}-a_n)B_n$ converges absolutely because
$$\sum_{n\ge M}|a_{n+1}-a_n|\,|B_n|\le L\sum_{n\ge M}(a_n-a_{n+1})=L\,a_M<\infty$$
(telescoping, using monotonicity $a_n\ge a_{n+1}$). Therefore the partial sums $\sum_{n=M}^N a_nb_n$ converge as $N\to\infty$, i.e. $\sum a_nb_n$ converges.

content.statement: Identify and prove everywhere-convergence of the sawtooth series.
content.formal: Verify that $$\frac1{2i}\sum_{n\ne0}\frac{e^{inx}}{n}$$ is the Fourier series of the $2\pi$-periodic sawtooth function defined by $f(0)=0$ and $$f(x)=\begin{cases}-\frac\pi2-\frac x2,& -\pi<x<0,\\ \frac\pi2-\frac x2,& 0<x<\pi.\end{cases}$$ Although $f$ is not continuous, show that the symmetric partial sums converge for every $x$. In particular, show that the value at $0$ is $0$, the average of the left and right limiting values. Hint: use Dirichlet's test.
content.intuition: A jump discontinuity drops decay to $1/n$ — too slow for absolute convergence, but Dirichlet's test still gives conditional convergence everywhere, landing on the jump's midpoint.

steps.content:
For $n
eq0$, integrating each linear piece and the $-x/2$ term by parts,
$$\hat f(n)= rac1{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^\pi f(x)e^{-inx}\,dx= rac1{2in}.$$
(The two constant pieces contribute $\pi rac{(-1)^n-1}{-in}$ and $-	frac12\int_{-\pi}^\pi xe^{-inx}dx=- rac{\pi(-1)^n}{-in}$; summing gives $ rac{\pi}{in}\cdot rac1{2\pi}= rac1{2in}$.) Also $\hat f(0)=0$. Hence the Fourier series is $\sum_{n
eq0} rac1{2in}e^{inx}= rac1{2i}\sum_{n
eq0} rac{e^{inx}}n$.

Pairing $\pm n$, $\frac1{2i}\big(\frac{e^{inx}}n+\frac{e^{-inx}}{-n}\big)=\frac{\sin nx}{n}$, so the symmetric partial sums equal $\sum_{n=1}^N\frac{\sin nx}n$. Apply Dirichlet's test (Exercise 7) with $a_n=1/n\downarrow0$ and $b_n=\sin nx$: for $x\notin2\pi\mathbb Z$ the partial sums $\sum_{n=1}^N\sin nx=\frac{\cos(x/2)-\cos((N+1/2)x)}{2\sin(x/2)}$ are bounded by $1/|\sin(x/2)|$, so the series converges. For $x\in2\pi\mathbb Z$ every term is $0$. Thus the series converges for all $x$.
At $x=0$ the sum is $0$, which equals $\tfrac12\big(f(0^+)+f(0^-)\big)=\tfrac12(\tfrac\pi2-\tfrac\pi2)=0$ — the midpoint of the jump, as expected for a piecewise-$C^1$ function.

concepts.label: SS 2.7.2 — Lebesgue constants and divergence at a point

content.statement: Lebesgue constants grow like $\log N$, forcing divergence at a point.
content.formal: Let $$D_N(\theta)=\sum_{k=-N}^{N}e^{ik\theta}=\frac{\sin((N+1/2)\theta)}{\sin(\theta/2)},\qquad L_N=\frac1{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^{\pi}|D_N(\theta)|\,d\theta.$$ (a) Prove that $L_N\ge c\log N$ for some constant $c>0$. Hint: show that $|D_N(\theta)|\ge c|\sin((N+1/2)\theta)|/|\theta|$, change variables, and prove $$L_N\ge c\int_{\pi}^{N\pi}\frac{|\sin\theta|}{|\theta|}\,d\theta+O(1),$$ then split the integral into intervals $[k\pi,(k+1)\pi]$ and use $\sum_{k=1}^{n}1/k\ge c\log n$. A sharper estimate is $L_N=\frac4{\pi^2}\log N+O(1)$. (b) Deduce that for each $n\ge1$ there exists a continuous function $f_n$ with $|f_n|\le1$ and $|S_n(f_n)(0)|\ge c'\log n$. Hint: first use the sign function of $D_n$, then approximate it in integral norm by continuous functions bounded by $1$.
content.intuition: The $\log N$ growth of the Lebesgue constants is the precise obstruction to pointwise convergence: the Dirichlet kernel is not a good kernel, and its $L^1$ norm blows up.

steps.content:
Since $|\sin(\theta/2)|\le|\theta|/2$, $|D_N(\theta)|\ge\frac{2|\sin((N+\frac12)\theta)|}{|\theta|}$. Thus, using evenness,
$$L_N=\frac1{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^\pi|D_N|\ge\frac{c}{\,}\int_0^\pi\frac{|\sin((N+\tfrac12)\theta)|}{\theta}\,d\theta\overset{\phi=(N+\frac12)\theta}{=}c\int_0^{(N+\frac12)\pi}\frac{|\sin\phi|}{\phi}\,d\phi.$$
Bound the integral below by summing over the humps $[k\pi,(k+1)\pi]$:
$$\int_\pi^{N\pi}\frac{|\sin\phi|}{\phi}\,d\phi\ge\sum_{k=1}^{N-1}\frac1{(k+1)\pi}\int_{k\pi}^{(k+1)\pi}|\sin\phi|\,d\phi=\frac2\pi\sum_{k=1}^{N-1}\frac1{(k+1)\pi}\ge c\log N.$$
Hence $L_N\ge c\log N$ (a sharper analysis gives $L_N=\frac4{\pi^2}\log N+O(1)$).

Note $S_n(f)(0)=(f*D_n)(0)=\frac1{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^\pi f(-\theta)D_n(\theta)\,d\theta$. The choice $g_n=\operatorname{sgn}(D_n)$ gives $\frac1{2\pi}\int g_nD_n=L_n\ge c\log n$, but $g_n$ is only piecewise constant. Approximate $g_n$ in the $L^1$ norm by continuous $h_k$ with $|h_k|\le1$ (Lemma 3.2); since $|\frac1{2\pi}\int(g_n-h_k)D_n|\le\|D_n\|_\infty\|g_n-h_k\|_1\to0$, some $f_n:=h_k$ satisfies $|S_n(f_n)(0)|\ge \tfrac12 c\log n=:c'\log n$ with $|f_n|\le1$.

The partial-sum operators $f\mapsto S_n(f)(0)$ are unbounded on $C(\mathbb T)$ ($\|S_n\|=L_n\to\infty$); by uniform boundedness this is the seed of du Bois-Reymond's continuous function with a divergent Fourier series.

concepts.label: SS 3.3.12 — The Dirichlet integral $\int \sin x / x = \pi/2$

content.statement: Evaluate the Dirichlet integral via the Dirichlet kernel and Riemann–Lebesgue.
content.formal: Prove the Dirichlet integral identity $$\int_0^{\infty}\frac{\sin x}{x}\,dx=\frac\pi2.$$ Hint: start from the fact that the integral of the Dirichlet kernel $D_N$ is $2\pi$, observe that $$\frac1{\sin(\theta/2)}-\frac2\theta$$ is continuous on $[-\pi,\pi]$, and apply the Riemann-Lebesgue lemma.
content.intuition: The Dirichlet kernel has total mass $2\pi$; peeling off its $2/\theta$ singular part (the rest is killed by Riemann–Lebesgue) leaves exactly the sine integral.

steps.content:
Start from $\frac1{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^\pi D_N(\theta)\,d\theta=1$, i.e. $\int_{-\pi}^\pi\frac{\sin((N+\frac12)\theta)}{\sin(\theta/2)}\,d\theta=2\pi$. Write
$$\frac{1}{\sin(\theta/2)}=\frac{2}{\theta}+\Big(\frac1{\sin(\theta/2)}-\frac2\theta\Big),$$
where the bracket extends to a continuous (hence integrable) function on $[-\pi,\pi]$. By the Riemann–Lebesgue lemma, $\int_{-\pi}^\pi\sin((N+\tfrac12)\theta)\big(\tfrac1{\sin(\theta/2)}-\tfrac2\theta\big)d\theta\to0$. Therefore
$$\int_{-\pi}^\pi\sin\big((N+\tfrac12)\theta\big)\frac2\theta\,d\theta\to2\pi\ \Longrightarrow\ \int_{-\pi}^\pi\frac{\sin((N+\frac12)\theta)}{\theta}\,d\theta\to\pi.$$
By evenness this is $2\int_0^\pi\frac{\sin((N+\frac12)\theta)}\theta d\theta\to\pi$, so $\int_0^\pi\frac{\sin((N+\frac12)\theta)}\theta d\theta\to\frac\pi2$. Substituting $x=(N+\tfrac12)\theta$ gives $\int_0^{(N+\frac12)\pi}\frac{\sin x}{x}\,dx\to\frac\pi2$, i.e.
$$\int_0^\infty\frac{\sin x}{x}\,dx=\frac\pi2.$$

content.statement: Lipschitz functions have absolutely (hence uniformly) convergent Fourier series.
content.formal:
Let $f$ be a $2\pi$-periodic function satisfying a Lipschitz condition with constant $K$, that is,
$$|f(x)-f(y)|\le K|x-y|\qquad\text{for all }x,y.$$
This is the Hölder condition with $\alpha=1$, so Exercise 15 gives $\widehat f(n)=O(1/|n|)$. Since the harmonic series $\sum 1/n$ diverges, this alone does not yet imply absolute convergence. The following outline proves that the Fourier series of $f$ converges absolutely and uniformly.

\textbf{(a)} For every positive $h$, define
$$g_h(x)=f(x+h)-f(x-h).$$
Prove that
$$\frac1{2\pi}\int_0^{2\pi}|g_h(x)|^2\,dx=\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty}4|\sin(nh)|^2|\widehat f(n)|^2,$$
and show that
$$\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty}|\sin(nh)|^2|\widehat f(n)|^2\le K^2h^2.$$

\textbf{(b)} Let $p$ be a positive integer. By choosing $h=\pi/2^{p+1}$, show that
$$\sum_{2^{p-1}<|n|\le 2^p}|\widehat f(n)|^2\le \frac{K^2\pi^2}{2^{2p+1}}.$$

\textbf{(c)} Estimate
$$\sum_{2^{p-1}<|n|\le 2^p}|\widehat f(n)|,$$
and conclude that the Fourier series of $f$ converges absolutely, hence uniformly. Hint: use the Cauchy--Schwarz inequality to estimate the sum.

\textbf{(d)} Modify the argument slightly to prove Bernstein's theorem: if $f$ satisfies a Hölder condition of order $\alpha>1/2$, then the Fourier series of $f$ converges absolutely.
content.intuition: Dyadic frequency blocks + Parseval turn a Lipschitz modulus into square-summable blocks; Cauchy–Schwarz then upgrades $\ell^2$ control to $\ell^1$ — the threshold being $\alpha>1/2$.

steps.content:
Since $\widehat{g_h}(n)=(e^{inh}-e^{-inh})\hat f(n)=2i\sin(nh)\hat f(n)$, Parseval gives
$$\frac1{2\pi}\int_0^{2\pi}|g_h|^2=\sum_n|\widehat{g_h}(n)|^2=\sum_n4\sin^2(nh)|\hat f(n)|^2.$$
Lipschitz: $|g_h(x)|=|f(x+h)-f(x-h)|\le K|2h|=2Kh$, so $\frac1{2\pi}\int|g_h|^2\le4K^2h^2$, whence $\sum_n\sin^2(nh)|\hat f(n)|^2\le K^2h^2$.

Take $h=\pi/2^{p+1}$. For $2^{p-1}<|n|\le2^p$ we have $nh\in(\pi/4,\pi/2]$, so $\sin^2(nh)\ge\tfrac12$. Therefore
$$\frac12\sum_{2^{p-1}<|n|\le2^p}|\hat f(n)|^2\le\sum_n\sin^2(nh)|\hat f(n)|^2\le K^2h^2=\frac{K^2\pi^2}{2^{2p+2}},$$
i.e. $\sum_{2^{p-1}<|n|\le2^p}|\hat f(n)|^2\le K^2\pi^2/2^{2p+1}$.

By Cauchy–Schwarz over the dyadic block (which has $\le2^p$ terms),
$$\sum_{2^{p-1}<|n|\le2^p}|\hat f(n)|\le(2^p)^{1/2}\Big(\frac{K^2\pi^2}{2^{2p+1}}\Big)^{1/2}=\frac{K\pi}{2^{(p+1)/2}}.$$
Summing the geometric series over $p\ge1$ (plus the $n=0$ term) gives $\sum_n|\hat f(n)|<\infty$. By the Weierstrass $M$-test the Fourier series converges absolutely and uniformly (to $f$, by continuity).
The same dyadic argument with the exponent bookkeeping shows: Hölder-$\alpha$ with $\alpha>1/2$ already forces absolute convergence.

content.statement: An $L^2$-null sequence that diverges at every single point.
content.formal: Construct a sequence of integrable functions $\{f_k\}$ on $[0,2\pi]$ such that $$\lim_{k\to\infty}\frac1{2\pi}\int_0^{2\pi}|f_k(\theta)|^2\,d\theta=0,$$ but $\lim_{k\to\infty}f_k(\theta)$ fails to exist for every $\theta$. Hint: choose intervals $I_k\subset[0,2\pi]$ whose lengths tend to $0$ and such that every point belongs to infinitely many of them; then take $f_k=\chi_{I_k}$.
content.intuition: Mean-square smallness controls average size, not values: a sweeping indicator of shrinking intervals vanishes in $L^2$ while pinging $1$ at every point forever.

steps.content: Enumerate dyadic blocks: for $m\ge0$ and $0\le j<2^m$ take the interval $I_{m,j}=[\,2\pi j/2^m,\ 2\pi(j+1)/2^m\,]$, and list them as $I_1,I_2,\dots$ in order of increasing $m$. Let $f_k=\chi_{I_k}$.

$|I_k|\to0$ as $k\to\infty$, so
$$\frac1{2\pi}\int_0^{2\pi}|f_k|^2\,d\theta=\frac{|I_k|}{2\pi}\to0.$$

Every $\theta$ lies in exactly one block of each level $m$, hence in infinitely many $I_k$ (so $f_k(\theta)=1$ infinitely often) and outside infinitely many (so $f_k(\theta)=0$ infinitely often). Thus $\limsup_k f_k(\theta)=1\neq0=\liminf_k f_k(\theta)$, and $\lim_k f_k(\theta)$ exists for no $\theta$.
This shows mean-square convergence does \emph{not} imply pointwise convergence (only a subsequence converges a.e.).

concepts.label: Summation by Parts

content.statement: The discrete integration-by-parts identity used to control oscillatory sums.
content.formal: Let $B_k=\sum_{n=M}^{k}b_n$. Then $$\sum_{n=M}^{N}a_nb_n=a_NB_N-a_MB_{M-1}-\sum_{n=M}^{N-1}(a_{n+1}-a_n)B_n,$$ with the usual convention for the lower partial sum. In particular, bounded partial sums of $\sum b_n$ combined with monotone $a_n\to0$ give Dirichlet's test.
content.intuition: Summation by parts transfers difficulty from an oscillatory factor to its partial sums. If those partial sums stay bounded and the other factor decreases, the oscillations force convergence.

assumptions: The displayed identity is finite; convergence conclusions require separate boundedness and monotonicity hypotheses.

concepts.label: Weierstrass Approximation Theorem

content.statement: Continuous periodic functions can be uniformly approximated by trigonometric polynomials.
content.formal: For every $f\in C(\mathbb T)$ and every $\varepsilon>0$, there exists a trigonometric polynomial $p$ such that $\|f-p\|_\infty<\varepsilon$. Equivalently, trigonometric polynomials are dense in $C(\mathbb T)$ in the uniform norm.
content.intuition: Fejer means are themselves trigonometric polynomials and converge uniformly to $f$, so they provide explicit approximants rather than just existence magic.

assumptions: This is the trigonometric version on the circle. Algebraic polynomial approximation on intervals is a related but different statement.

concepts.label: Band-Limited Function

content.statement: A function whose Fourier transform vanishes outside a bounded frequency band.
content.formal: A function $f$ is band-limited to $[-W,W]$ if $\operatorname{supp}\hat f\subset[-W,W]$. Equivalently, no frequencies with $|\xi|>W$ are present.
content.intuition: Band-limited signals have finite frequency range. That frequency restriction makes exact sampling and analytic continuation possible.

assumptions: Support of $\hat f$ is understood in the appropriate sense: ordinary support for continuous transforms, essential support for $L^2$ transforms.

concepts.label: Convolution Theorem

content.statement: The Fourier transform turns convolution into multiplication, and multiplication into convolution under suitable hypotheses.
content.formal: If $f,g\in L^1(\mathbb R)$, then $\widehat{f*g}=\hat f\,\hat g$. If $f,g\in\mathcal S(\mathbb R)$, then $\widehat{fg}=\hat f*\hat g$, using the same Fourier convention.
content.intuition: Fourier waves diagonalize translation-invariant averaging. That is why convolution, which mixes nearby values, becomes pointwise multiplication in frequency.

assumptions: Assumptions depend on the identity: $L^1$ is enough for convolution-to-product; Schwartz functions safely justify both identities.
The identity $\widehat{f*g}=\widehat f\widehat g$ holds for $f,g\in L^1(\mathbb R)$.
The identity $\widehat{fg}=\widehat f*\widehat g$ is stated here under Schwartz hypotheses to justify inversion and Fubini cleanly.

steps.content: Use the graph convention $$\widehat f(\xi)=\int_{\mathbb R}f(x)e^{-2\pi i x\xi}\,dx.$$ For the convolution-to-product identity, assume $f,g\in L^1(\mathbb R)$. Then $f*g\in L^1(\mathbb R)$, where $$(f*g)(x)=\int_{\mathbb R}f(y)g(x-y)\,dy.$$ The absolute integrability needed below follows from Fubini--Tonelli: $$\int_{\mathbb R}\int_{\mathbb R}|f(y)|\,|g(x-y)|\,dy\,dx=\|f\|_1\|g\|_1<\infty.$$

Compute directly and use Fubini to change the order of integration: $$\begin{aligned} \widehat{f*g}(\xi)&=\int_{\mathbb R}\int_{\mathbb R}f(y)g(x-y)e^{-2\pi i x\xi}\,dy\,dx. \end{aligned}$$ Put $u=x-y$, so $x=u+y$. Then $$\begin{aligned} \widehat{f*g}(\xi)&=\int_{\mathbb R}f(y)e^{-2\pi i y\xi}\left(\int_{\mathbb R}g(u)e^{-2\pi i u\xi}\,du\right)dy\\ &=\widehat f(\xi)\widehat g(\xi). \end{aligned}$$ Thus $$\widehat{f*g}=\widehat f\,\widehat g.$$

For $f,g\in\mathcal S(\mathbb R)$, the functions and their Fourier transforms are rapidly decreasing, so all integrals below are absolutely justified. By Fourier inversion, $$g(x)=\int_{\mathbb R}\widehat g(\eta)e^{2\pi i x\eta}\,d\eta.$$ Hence $$\begin{aligned} \widehat{fg}(\xi)&=\int_{\mathbb R}f(x)g(x)e^{-2\pi i x\xi}\,dx\\ &=\int_{\mathbb R}\widehat g(\eta)\left(\int_{\mathbb R}f(x)e^{-2\pi i x(\xi-\eta)}\,dx\right)d\eta\\ &=\int_{\mathbb R}\widehat f(\xi-\eta)\widehat g(\eta)\,d\eta=(\widehat f*\widehat g)(\xi). \end{aligned}$$ Therefore multiplication in physical space becomes convolution in frequency space under Schwartz hypotheses.

source.citation: Stein–Shakarchi: Fourier Analysis, Ch.5 Proposition 1.8 and Proposition 1.11; UC Davis notes on the Fourier transform and Schwartz functions
source.ref: Stein–Shakarchi: Fourier Analysis, Ch.5 Proposition 1.8 and Proposition 1.11; UC Davis notes on the Fourier transform and Schwartz functions

concepts.label: Differentiation Rule

content.statement: Differentiation becomes multiplication by frequency under the Fourier transform.
content.formal: For $f\in\mathcal S(\mathbb R)$, $\widehat{f'}(\xi)=2\pi i\xi\,\hat f(\xi)$ and $\widehat{xf}(\xi)=-\frac1{2\pi i}\frac{d}{d\xi}\hat f(\xi)$.
content.intuition: The Fourier transform turns differential operators into algebraic multipliers, which is why linear constant-coefficient PDEs become solvable in frequency space.

assumptions: Schwartz hypotheses avoid boundary terms and justify integration by parts.
Schwartz hypotheses justify integration by parts and differentiation under the integral sign.

steps.content: Let $f\in\mathcal S(\mathbb R)$. The rapid decay of $f$ and its derivatives implies that integration by parts has no boundary contribution: $$\lim_{R\to\infty}f(R)e^{-2\pi iR\xi}=\lim_{R\to\infty}f(-R)e^{2\pi iR\xi}=0.$$
