Using integration by parts, $$\begin{aligned} \widehat{f'}(\xi)&=\int_{\mathbb R}f'(x)e^{-2\pi i x\xi}\,dx\\ &=\left[f(x)e^{-2\pi i x\xi}\right]_{-\infty}^{\infty}+2\pi i\xi\int_{\mathbb R}f(x)e^{-2\pi i x\xi}\,dx\\ &=2\pi i\xi\widehat f(\xi). \end{aligned}$$ Thus differentiation in $x$ becomes multiplication by $2\pi i\xi$ in frequency.

Differentiate under the integral sign, justified by the rapid decay of $xf(x)$: $$\frac{d}{d\xi}\widehat f(\xi)=\int_{\mathbb R}f(x)(-2\pi i x)e^{-2\pi i x\xi}\,dx=-2\pi i\widehat{xf}(\xi).$$ Therefore $$\widehat{xf}(\xi)=-\frac1{2\pi i}\frac{d}{d\xi}\widehat f(\xi).$$

source.citation: Stein–Shakarchi: Fourier Analysis, Ch.5 Proposition 1.1 and Proposition 1.2; UC Davis notes on the Fourier transform and Schwartz functions
source.ref: Stein–Shakarchi: Fourier Analysis, Ch.5 Proposition 1.1 and Proposition 1.2; UC Davis notes on the Fourier transform and Schwartz functions

concepts.label: Entire Function

content.statement: A complex function holomorphic on the whole complex plane.
content.formal: A function $F:\mathbb C\to\mathbb C$ is entire if it is complex-differentiable at every point of $\mathbb C$.
content.intuition: Entire functions are globally analytic. Paley-Wiener connects compact frequency support with this strong complex-analytic regularity.

assumptions: This is a complex-analysis prerequisite used only where analytic continuation matters.

content.statement: Show the sinc function lies in $L^2(\mathbb R)$ and has Fourier transform $\chi_{[-1/2,1/2]}$.
content.formal: For $\psi(x)=\frac{\sin(\pi x)}{\pi x}$ ($\psi(0)=1$), show $\psi\in L^2(\mathbb R)$ and $\hat\psi(\xi)=\chi_{[-1/2,1/2]}(\xi)$ (transform convention $\hat f(\xi)=\int f(x)e^{-2\pi i x\xi}dx$).
content.intuition: Box and sinc are a Fourier pair; the box is the easy ($L^1$) side, and unitarity of $\mathcal F$ on $L^2$ transports the identity back to the sinc.

steps.content: $\psi$ is continuous and $|\psi(x)|^2=\frac{\sin^2(\pi x)}{\pi^2x^2}$ is bounded near $0$ (limit $1$); for $|x|\ge1$, $|\psi(x)|^2\le\frac{1}{\pi^2x^2}$, which is integrable. Hence $\int_{\mathbb R}|\psi|^2<\infty$. (Note $\psi\notin L^1$: it decays only like $1/|x|$, so the transform must be taken in the $L^2$/Plancherel sense.)

Let $b:=\chi_{[-1/2,1/2]}\in L^1\cap L^2$, even. Compute its transform directly:
$$\hat b(x)=\int_{-1/2}^{1/2}e^{-2\pi i x\xi}\,d\xi=\frac{e^{-\pi ix}-e^{\pi ix}}{-2\pi ix}=\frac{\sin(\pi x)}{\pi x}=\psi(x).$$
So $\hat b=\psi$. The Fourier transform extends to a \textbf{unitary} map on $L^2$ with $\mathcal F\mathcal F\,b=b(-\,\cdot)$ (double transform = reflection). Since $b$ is even, $b(-\cdot)=b$, hence
$$\hat\psi=\mathcal F[\hat b]=\mathcal F\mathcal F\,b=b(-\cdot)=b=\chi_{[-1/2,1/2]}.$$
Equivalently: $\psi$ is the inverse transform of the unit box, so by inversion the forward transform of $\psi$ is that box; membership $\psi\in L^2$ is exactly what makes Plancherel applicable despite $\psi\notin L^1$.

content.statement: Show the integer translates of sinc, and their transforms, are orthonormal in $L^2(\mathbb R)$.
content.formal: With $\psi_k(x)=\psi(x+k)$ and $\hat\psi_k=\mathcal F[\psi_k]$, show $\{\hat\psi_k\}_{k\in\mathbb Z}$ and $\{\psi_k\}_{k\in\mathbb Z}$ are orthonormal sets in $L^2(\mathbb R)$.
content.intuition: On the frequency side the translates become pure exponentials on $[-1/2,1/2]$, whose orthonormality is classical; unitarity carries it back to the sincs.

steps.content:
Translation by $+k$ multiplies the transform by a phase:
$$\hat\psi_k(\xi)=\mathcal F[\psi(\cdot+k)](\xi)=e^{2\pi i k\xi}\,\hat\psi(\xi)=e^{2\pi i k\xi}\,\chi_{[-1/2,1/2]}(\xi).$$

For $k,\ell\in\mathbb Z$,
$$\langle\hat\psi_k,\hat\psi_\ell\rangle=\int_{-1/2}^{1/2}e^{2\pi i(k-\ell)\xi}\,d\xi=\frac{\sin(\pi(k-\ell))}{\pi(k-\ell)}=\delta_{k\ell},$$
the classical orthonormality of $\{e^{2\pi i k\xi}\}$ on an interval of length $1$.

The Fourier transform is \textbf{unitary} on $L^2$ (Plancherel), so it preserves inner products:
$$\langle\psi_k,\psi_\ell\rangle_{L^2}=\langle\hat\psi_k,\hat\psi_\ell\rangle_{L^2}=\delta_{k\ell}.$$

The $\psi_k$ form an orthonormal basis of the Paley–Wiener space $\mathrm{PW}_{1/2}=\{f\in L^2:\hat f\ \text{supported in}\ [-\tfrac12,\tfrac12]\}$ of band-limited functions; on the transform side this is just the Fourier basis $e^{2\pi i k\xi}$ on the band, and the reconstruction $f(x)=\sum_k f(-k)\psi(x+k)$ is the sampling theorem.

content.statement: Decide, for $f$ Schwartz / moderate-decrease / $L^2$ / a jump function, which target spaces $\hat f$ lands in.
content.formal:
Classify $\hat f$ for each $f$ against: A ($\hat f\in C_0$), B ($\hat f\in C_m$, moderate decrease), C ($\hat f\in\mathcal S$), D ($\hat f\in L^1$), E ($\hat f\in L^2$), where
(1) $f\in\mathcal S(\mathbb R)$; (2) $f\in C_m(\mathbb R)$; (3) $f\in L^2(\mathbb R)$; (4) $f(x)=x^{-2}\chi_{[1,\infty)}(x)$.
content.intuition: L¹ buys continuity-and-decay (A); $L^2$ buys $L^2$ (E); rapid decay of $\hat f$ (B, C, D) is bought only by smoothness of $f$ — a jump caps decay at $1/|\xi|$.

steps.content: Riemann–Lebesgue: $f\in L^1\Rightarrow\hat f\in C_0$ (gives A). Plancherel: $f\in L^2\Rightarrow\hat f\in L^2$ (gives E). $\mathcal F:\mathcal S\to\mathcal S$ bijectively, and $\mathcal S\subset C_m\subset L^1\cap L^2$, $\mathcal S\subset C_0$. \emph{Decay of $\hat f$ is governed by smoothness of $f$}: a jump in $f$ ⟹ $\hat f\sim 1/|\xi|$ only.
($f\in\mathcal S$): $\hat f\in\mathcal S$ (C ✓), and $\mathcal S$ sits inside all the others, so \textbf{A, B, C, D, E}.

($f\in C_m$): $C_m\subset L^1\Rightarrow$ A ✓ (R–L); $C_m\subset L^2\Rightarrow$ E ✓ (Plancherel). But moderate decrease says nothing about smoothness of $f$, so $\hat f$ need not decay faster than $C_0$: B ✗, C ✗, D ✗. $\Rightarrow$ \textbf{A, E}.
($f\in L^2$ only): Plancherel gives E ✓; without $f\in L^1$ there is no continuity/decay, so A, B, C, D ✗. $\Rightarrow$ \textbf{E}.

($f=x^{-2}\chi_{[1,\infty)}$): $\int_1^\infty x^{-2}=1$ and $\int_1^\infty x^{-4}<\infty$, so $f\in L^1\cap L^2$ ⟹ A ✓, E ✓. But $f$ has a jump at $x=1$; integrating by parts,
$$\hat f(\xi)=\frac{e^{-2\pi i\xi}}{2\pi i\xi}-\frac{1}{\pi i\xi}\int_1^\infty x^{-3}e^{-2\pi i x\xi}\,dx,$$
so $\hat f\sim \frac{1}{2\pi|\xi|}$. Since $1/|\xi|$ is neither integrable nor of moderate decrease, B ✗, D ✗, C ✗. $\Rightarrow$ \textbf{A, E}.

content.statement: For Schwartz $\phi$, prove $\|\phi\|_2^2\le 2\|x\phi\|_2\|\phi'\|_2$.
content.formal: For $\phi\in\mathcal S(\mathbb R)$ with $\|\phi\|_2\neq0$, show $\|\phi\|_2^2\le 2\,\|x\phi\|_2\,\|\phi'\|_2$.
content.intuition: Integration by parts trades $\|\phi\|_2^2$ for a product of position-spread $\|x\phi\|_2$ and gradient-size $\|\phi'\|_2$; Cauchy–Schwarz closes it — a function cannot be sharp in both position and frequency.

steps.content:
Differentiate $|\phi|^2$: $\ \frac{d}{dx}|\phi(x)|^2=\phi'\overline\phi+\phi\overline{\phi'}=2\,\mathrm{Re}\big(\overline{\phi}\,\phi'\big)$. Integrate $\|\phi\|_2^2=\int 1\cdot|\phi|^2$ by parts with $u=|\phi|^2$, $v=x$:
$$\int_{\mathbb R}|\phi|^2\,dx=\Big[x|\phi(x)|^2\Big]_{-\infty}^{\infty}-\int_{\mathbb R}x\,\frac{d}{dx}|\phi(x)|^2\,dx.$$
The boundary term vanishes because $\phi\in\mathcal S$ decays faster than any power, so $x|\phi(x)|^2\to0$. Therefore, using $-2\,\mathrm{Re}(z)\le2|z|$ and then Cauchy–Schwarz on $|x\phi|$ and $|\phi'|$,
$$\|\phi\|_2^2=-2\int_{\mathbb R}x\,\mathrm{Re}\big(\overline\phi\,\phi'\big)\,dx\le 2\int_{\mathbb R}|x\phi|\,|\phi'|\,dx\le 2\,\|x\phi\|_2\,\|\phi'\|_2.$$

Plancherel gives $\|\phi'\|_2=\|\widehat{\phi'}\|_2=\|2\pi i\xi\,\hat\phi\|_2=2\pi\|\xi\hat\phi\|_2$, so
$$\|x\phi\|_2^2\,\|\xi\hat\phi\|_2^2\ge\frac{\|\phi\|_2^4}{16\pi^2},$$
which, normalised to $\|\phi\|_2=1$, is the uncertainty principle in the problem's header.

content.statement: Show $(\mathcal H_t^{(2)}*f)(x)\to f(x)$ as $t\to0^+$ for $f\in\mathcal S(\mathbb R^2)$.
content.formal: For $\mathcal H_t^{(2)}(x)=\frac{1}{4\pi t}e^{-|x|^2/(4t)}$ and $f\in\mathcal S(\mathbb R^2)$, show $(\mathcal H_t^{(2)}*f)(x)\to f(x)$ as $t\to0^+$ for every $x\in\mathbb R^2$.
content.intuition: A Gaussian of shrinking width and unit mass becomes a delta; convolving against it returns the function. Split into a near part (continuity) and a far part (vanishing tail mass).

steps.content: We show $\{\mathcal H_t^{(2)}\}$ is an \textbf{approximate identity} as $t\to0^+$.

With $\int_{\mathbb R}e^{-s^2/4t}ds=\sqrt{4\pi t}$,
$$\int_{\mathbb R^2}\mathcal H_t^{(2)}=\frac{1}{4\pi t}\Big(\int_{\mathbb R}e^{-y_1^2/4t}dy_1\Big)\Big(\int_{\mathbb R}e^{-y_2^2/4t}dy_2\Big)=\frac{1}{4\pi t}(4\pi t)=1.$$
$\mathcal H_t^{(2)}\ge0$, so $\|\mathcal H_t^{(2)}\|_{L^1}=1$ uniformly.

For $\delta>0$, substitute $y=\sqrt t\,u$:
$$\int_{|y|\ge\delta}\mathcal H_t^{(2)}(y)\,dy=\frac{1}{4\pi}\int_{|u|\ge\delta/\sqrt t}e^{-|u|^2/4}\,du\xrightarrow[t\to0^+]{}0,$$
since $\delta/\sqrt t\to\infty$ and $e^{-|u|^2/4}$ is integrable.

Fix $x,\ \varepsilon>0$. Using (i),
$$\big(\mathcal H_t^{(2)}*f\big)(x)-f(x)=\int_{\mathbb R^2}\mathcal H_t^{(2)}(y)\big[f(x-y)-f(x)\big]\,dy.$$
$f\in\mathcal S$ is bounded and uniformly continuous; choose $\delta$ with $|f(x-y)-f(x)|<\varepsilon$ for $|y|<\delta$. Splitting and using $\mathcal H_t^{(2)}\ge0$,
$$\big|\mathcal H_t^{(2)}*f(x)-f(x)\big|\le \varepsilon\cdot1+2\|f\|_\infty\int_{|y|\ge\delta}\mathcal H_t^{(2)}(y)\,dy.$$
By (iii) the second term $\to0$, so $\limsup_{t\to0^+}|\cdots|\le\varepsilon$ for all $\varepsilon>0$; the limit is $0$.

The bound used only uniform continuity and boundedness, so convergence is in fact uniform in $x$. On the transform side $\widehat{\mathcal H_t^{(2)}}(\xi)=e^{-4\pi^2t|\xi|^2}\to1$, i.e. $u(x,t)=\mathcal H_t^{(2)}*f$ solves the heat equation with initial data $f$.

concepts.label: Fourier Inversion Theorem

content.statement: The Fourier transform is inverted by the opposite-sign integral when the hypotheses justify reconstruction.
content.formal: For $f\in\mathcal S(\mathbb R)$, $f(x)=\int_{\mathbb R}\hat f(\xi)e^{2\pi i x\xi}\,d\xi$. More generally, inversion holds in other spaces with the appropriate convergence interpretation.
content.formula: $$f\in\mathcal S(\mathbb R)\Longrightarrow f(x)=\int_{\mathbb R}\widehat f(\xi)e^{2\pi i x\xi}\,d\xi.$$
content.intuition: Analysis and synthesis are symmetric: the inverse transform recombines continuous waves with the sign reversed.

assumptions: Uses the graph's $2\pi$-frequency convention.
This proof uses Gaussian regularization and dominated convergence.
For Schwartz functions, inversion is pointwise and the inverse integral is absolutely convergent.

steps.content: Let $f\in\mathcal S(\mathbb R)$ and use $$\widehat f(\xi)=\int_{\mathbb R}f(x)e^{-2\pi i x\xi}\,dx.$$ Since the Fourier transform maps Schwartz functions to Schwartz functions, $\widehat f\in\mathcal S(\mathbb R)\subset L^1(\mathbb R)$. For $\varepsilon>0$, define the Gaussian multiplier $$m_\varepsilon(\xi)=e^{-\pi\varepsilon^2\xi^2}.$$ Then $$I_\varepsilon(x)=\int_{\mathbb R}\widehat f(\xi)m_\varepsilon(\xi)e^{2\pi i x\xi}\,d\xi$$ is absolutely convergent.

Let $$\phi_\varepsilon(x)=\varepsilon^{-1}e^{-\pi(x/\varepsilon)^2}.$$ The Gaussian transform identity gives $$\widehat{\phi_\varepsilon}(\xi)=e^{-\pi\varepsilon^2\xi^2}=m_\varepsilon(\xi).$$ By the convolution theorem, $$\widehat{f*\phi_\varepsilon}(\xi)=\widehat f(\xi)m_\varepsilon(\xi).$$ Hence the regularized inverse integral equals the Gaussian convolution: $$I_\varepsilon(x)=(f*\phi_\varepsilon)(x).$$

The kernels $\phi_\varepsilon$ have unit mass and concentrate at $0$: $$\int_{\mathbb R}\phi_\varepsilon(t)\,dt=1,$$ and for every $\delta>0$, $$\int_{|t|\ge\delta}\phi_\varepsilon(t)\,dt\xrightarrow[\varepsilon\to0^+]{}0.$$ Since $f\in\mathcal S(\mathbb R)$ is uniformly continuous and bounded, the standard approximate-identity estimate gives $$\|f*\phi_\varepsilon-f\|_\infty\xrightarrow[\varepsilon\to0^+]{}0.$$ Therefore $$I_\varepsilon(x)\to f(x)$$ uniformly in $x$.

For each fixed $x$, the functions $$\widehat f(\xi)e^{-\pi\varepsilon^2\xi^2}e^{2\pi i x\xi}$$ converge pointwise to $$\widehat f(\xi)e^{2\pi i x\xi}$$ as $\varepsilon\to0^+$. Also, $$\left|\widehat f(\xi)e^{-\pi\varepsilon^2\xi^2}e^{2\pi i x\xi}\right|\le |\widehat f(\xi)|,$$ and $\widehat f\in L^1(\mathbb R)$. Dominated convergence gives $$\lim_{\varepsilon\to0^+}I_\varepsilon(x)=\int_{\mathbb R}\widehat f(\xi)e^{2\pi i x\xi}\,d\xi.$$ Combining this with $I_\varepsilon(x)	o f(x)$ yields $$f(x)=\int_{\mathbb R}\widehat f(\xi)e^{2\pi i x\xi}\,d\xi.$$

Thus, for every $f\in\mathcal S(\mathbb R)$, Fourier analysis followed by synthesis recovers the original function pointwise: $$\mathcal F^{-1}\mathcal F f=f.$$ Under the graph convention, the inverse transform uses the opposite sign and no extra constant.

source.citation: Stein–Shakarchi: Fourier Analysis, Ch.5 Theorem 1.9 and Corollary 1.10; UC Davis notes on the Fourier transform and Schwartz functions
source.ref: Stein–Shakarchi: Fourier Analysis, Ch.5 Theorem 1.9 and Corollary 1.10; UC Davis notes on the Fourier transform and Schwartz functions

concepts.label: Fourier Transform

content.statement: The transform that decomposes functions on $\mathbb R$ into continuous frequencies.
content.formal: For $f\in L^1(\mathbb R)$, $\hat f(\xi)=\int_{\mathbb R}f(x)e^{-2\pi i x\xi}\,dx$. This graph uses the $2\pi$-frequency convention.
content.intuition: Fourier coefficients measure alignment with discrete waves on the circle; the Fourier transform does the same with a continuum of waves on the line.

assumptions: Convention: $\hat f(\xi)=\int f(x)e^{-2\pi i x\xi}\,dx$.

content.statement: The sign and 2π-normalization used for the Fourier transform.
content.definition: $$\widehat f(\xi)=\int_{\mathbb R}f(x)e^{-2\pi ix\xi}\,dx,\qquad f(x)=\int_{\mathbb R}\widehat f(\xi)e^{2\pi ix\xi}\,d\xi.$$
content.formal: $\hat f(\xi)=\int_{\mathbb R}f(x)e^{-2\pi i x\xi}\,dx$, with inverse $f(x)=\int_{\mathbb R}\hat f(\xi)e^{2\pi i x\xi}\,d\xi$ when inversion is valid.
content.intuition: Fourier constants are bookkeeping with teeth. Fixing the convention prevents sign and 2π errors across inversion, Plancherel, PDEs, and uncertainty.

concepts.label: Fourier Transform on $\mathbb R^d$

content.statement: The Fourier transform on $\mathbb R^d$, decomposing functions into multidimensional plane waves.
content.definition: $$\widehat f(\xi)=\int_{\mathbb R^d}f(x)e^{-2\pi i x\cdot\xi}\,dx,\qquad x,\xi\in\mathbb R^d.$$
content.formal: For $f\in L^1(\mathbb R^d)$, $\hat f(\xi)=\int_{\mathbb R^d}f(x)e^{-2\pi i x\cdot\xi}\,dx$, where $x,\xi\in\mathbb R^d$. The inverse uses $e^{2\pi i x\cdot\xi}$ when inversion is valid.
content.intuition: In several variables, the pure frequencies are plane waves. The vector $\xi$ records both direction and rate of oscillation.

assumptions: Uses the same $2\pi$-frequency convention as the one-dimensional transform.
$L^1(\mathbb R^d)$ means Lebesgue integrability on $\mathbb R^d$.

source.citation: Stein–Shakarchi: Ch.6 Corollary 2.2; Ch.6 Corollary 2.3; Ch.6 Proposition 2.1
source.ref: Stein–Shakarchi: Ch.6 Corollary 2.2; Ch.6 Corollary 2.3; Ch.6 Proposition 2.1

concepts.label: Gaussian (Self-Dual)

content.statement: The normalized Gaussian is fixed by the Fourier transform.
content.formal: With the graph's convention, if $f(x)=e^{-\pi x^2}$, then $\hat f(\xi)=e^{-\pi \xi^2}$. More generally, Fourier transforms send Gaussians to Gaussians.
content.intuition: The Gaussian is the perfectly balanced function: stable under Fourier transform and extremal for uncertainty.

assumptions: Uses the $e^{-2\pi i x\xi}$ convention.

concepts.label: Hausdorff–Young Inequality

content.statement: The Fourier transform maps $L^p$ into $L^{p'}$ for $1\le p\le2$.
content.formal: With the graph's Fourier convention, if $1\le p\le2$ and $p'=p/(p-1)$, then $\|\hat f\|_{p'}\le\|f\|_p$ for $f\in L^p(\mathbb R)$, with the endpoint $p=1$ interpreted as $\|\hat f\|_\infty\le\|f\|_1$.
content.intuition: Hausdorff-Young interpolates between the obvious $L^1\to L^\infty$ bound and Plancherel's $L^2\to L^2$ isometry. It is the standard $L^p$ mapping theorem before the transform stops improving integrability past $p=2$.

assumptions: The constant is convention-dependent; for the normalized $2\pi$ convention the standard interpolation form has constant at most $1$.
The statement is for $1\le p\le2$; for $p>2$ the Fourier transform is not generally defined as an $L^{p'}$ function.

source.ref: Stein–Shakarchi: Ch.5 and standard Riesz-Thorin interpolation consequence

concepts.label: Heat Kernel on $\mathbb R$

content.statement: The Gaussian convolution kernel solving the heat equation on the real line.
content.formal: For $t>0$, $H_t(x)=\frac1{\sqrt{4\pi t}}e^{-x^2/(4t)}$, $\hat H_t(\xi)=e^{-4\pi^2t\xi^2}$, and $u(x,t)=(H_t*f)(x)$ solves $u_t=u_{xx}$ with initial data $f$ in suitable senses.
content.intuition: Heat flow is Gaussian averaging. As $t\downarrow0$, $H_t$ concentrates near $0$; as $t$ grows, it spreads and smooths.

assumptions: The initial-data interpretation depends on the function space, such as $L^p$, $C_0$, or distributions.

source.citation: Stein–Shakarchi: Ch.5 Corollary 1.5; Ch.5 Corollary 3.4; Ch.5 Theorem 3.3
source.ref: Stein–Shakarchi: Ch.5 Corollary 1.5; Ch.5 Corollary 3.4; Ch.5 Theorem 3.3

concepts.label: Heat Kernel on $\mathbb R^d$

content.statement: The Gaussian convolution kernel that solves the heat equation in $\mathbb R^d$; an approximate identity as $t\to0^+$.
content.formal: For $t>0$, $\mathcal H_t^{(d)}(x)=(4\pi t)^{-d/2}e^{-|x|^2/(4t)}$ with $\widehat{\mathcal H_t^{(d)}}(\xi)=e^{-4\pi^2 t|\xi|^2}$. Then $u(x,t)=(\mathcal H_t^{(d)}*f)(x)$ solves $\partial_t u=\Delta u$ with $u(\cdot,t)\to f$ as $t\to0^+$.
content.intuition: A product of one-dimensional Gaussians, one per coordinate; total mass $1$, concentrating at the origin as $t\downarrow0$.

assumptions: Separability: $\mathcal H_t^{(d)}(x)=\prod_{j=1}^d \mathcal H_t^{(1)}(x_j)$, so all one-dimensional identities tensor up.

source.citation: Stein–Shakarchi: Ch.6 §2 (the heat kernel in R^d)
source.ref: Stein–Shakarchi: Ch.6 §2 (the heat kernel in R^d)

concepts.label: Paley–Wiener Theorem

content.statement: Compact frequency support corresponds to entire extension with controlled exponential growth.
content.formal: For $f\in L^2(\mathbb R)$, $\operatorname{supp}\hat f\subset[-W,W]$ iff $f$ agrees a.e. on $\mathbb R$ with an entire function $F$ such that $F|_{\mathbb R}\in L^2(\mathbb R)$ and $|F(z)|\le C e^{2\pi W|\operatorname{Im}z|}$ in the appropriate $L^2$ Paley-Wiener sense.
content.formula: $$\begin{aligned} f\in L^2(\mathbb R),\ \operatorname{supp}\widehat f\subset[-W,W]&\Longleftrightarrow \exists F\in\mathcal O(\mathbb C):\ F|_{\mathbb R}=f\ \text{a.e.},\\ |F(z)|&\le Ce^{2\pi W|\operatorname{Im}z|},\\ F|_{\mathbb R}&\in L^2(\mathbb R). \end{aligned}$$
content.intuition: Cutting off frequency creates strong analytic structure in space. Band limitation is not just smoothness; it forces complex-analytic continuation.

assumptions: This is a schematic $L^2$ Paley-Wiener formulation; precise versions specify the exact growth and square-integrability condition.

concepts.label: Plancherel's Theorem

content.statement: The Fourier transform extends to a unitary operator on $L^2(\mathbb R)$.
content.formal: For $f\in\mathcal S(\mathbb R)$, $\|\hat f\|_{L^2(\mathbb R)}=\|f\|_{L^2(\mathbb R)}$. Hence $\mathcal F$ extends uniquely to a unitary map on $L^2(\mathbb R)$.
content.formula: $$\begin{aligned} \mathcal F:L^2(\mathbb R)&\to L^2(\mathbb R),\\ \|\widehat f\|_2&=\|f\|_2,\\ \langle \widehat f,\widehat g\rangle&=\langle f,g\rangle. \end{aligned}$$
content.intuition: The Fourier transform preserves energy. In $L^2$, it is not a lossy change of representation but a rotation of Hilbert space.

assumptions: Fourier convention $e^{-2\pi i x\xi}$; $L^2$ is on $\mathbb R$.
The proof first establishes Plancherel on $\mathcal S(\mathbb R)$ and then extends by density to $L^2(\mathbb R)$.
The graph convention $e^{-2\pi i x\xi}$ makes the Fourier transform unitary without extra constants.

steps.content: First prove the identity for $f,g\in\mathcal S(\mathbb R)$. With the graph convention, $$\widehat f(\xi)=\int_{\mathbb R}f(x)e^{-2\pi i x\xi}\,dx.$$ Since $f,g,\widehat f,\widehat g$ are Schwartz functions, the integrals below are absolutely justified.

Use Fubini and Fourier inversion for $g$: $$\begin{aligned} \langle \widehat f,\widehat g\rangle_{L^2}&=\int_{\mathbb R}\widehat f(\xi)\overline{\widehat g(\xi)}\,d\xi\\ &=\int_{\mathbb R}\left(\int_{\mathbb R}f(x)e^{-2\pi i x\xi}\,dx\right)\overline{\widehat g(\xi)}\,d\xi\\ &=\int_{\mathbb R}f(x)\left(\int_{\mathbb R}e^{-2\pi i x\xi}\overline{\widehat g(\xi)}\,d\xi\right)dx. \end{aligned}$$ Since $$\overline{g(x)}=\int_{\mathbb R}\overline{\widehat g(\xi)}e^{-2\pi i x\xi}\,d\xi,$$ the last expression becomes $$\int_{\mathbb R}f(x)\overline{g(x)}\,dx=\langle f,g\rangle_{L^2}.$$ Hence $$\langle \widehat f,\widehat g\rangle=\langle f,g\rangle$$ for Schwartz functions.

Taking $g=f$ in the inner-product identity gives $$\|\widehat f\|_2^2=\langle \widehat f,\widehat f\rangle=\langle f,f\rangle=\|f\|_2^2.$$ Thus the Fourier transform preserves the $L^2$ norm on $\mathcal S(\mathbb R)$.

The Schwartz space is dense in $L^2(\mathbb R)$. If $f\in L^2(\mathbb R)$ and $f_k\in\mathcal S(\mathbb R)$ with $f_k\to f$ in $L^2$, then $$\|\widehat{f_k}-\widehat{f_j}\|_2=\|f_k-f_j\|_2.$$ Hence $(\widehat{f_k})$ is Cauchy in $L^2(\mathbb R)$, so it has an $L^2$ limit. Define $\mathcal F f$ to be this limit. The definition is independent of the approximating Schwartz sequence by the same norm identity.

The extension satisfies $$\|\mathcal F f\|_2=\|f\|_2$$ for every $f\in L^2(\mathbb R)$. Applying the same construction to the inverse transform gives surjectivity and $$\langle \mathcal Ff,\mathcal Fg\rangle=\langle f,g\rangle.$$ Therefore the Fourier transform extends uniquely to a unitary operator on $L^2(\mathbb R)$.

source.citation: Stein–Shakarchi: Fourier Analysis, Ch.5 Theorem 1.12; UC Davis notes on Fourier transform on Schwartz functions and $L^2$
source.ref: Stein–Shakarchi: Fourier Analysis, Ch.5 Theorem 1.12; UC Davis notes on Fourier transform on Schwartz functions and $L^2$

concepts.label: Poisson Kernel on the Half-Plane

content.statement: The Poisson kernel solving the Dirichlet problem on the upper half-plane.
content.formal: For $y>0$, $P_y(x)=\frac1\pi\frac{y}{x^2+y^2}$, $\hat P_y(\xi)=e^{-2\pi y|\xi|}$, and $u(x,y)=(f*P_y)(x)$ is harmonic in $\mathbb R^2_+$ with boundary trace $f$ under suitable hypotheses.
content.intuition: It is the real-line version of harmonic extension: moving upward into the half-plane smooths boundary data by a scale $y$.

assumptions: Boundary convergence depends on the function space of $f$.

source.citation: Stein–Shakarchi: Ch.5 Lemma 2.4; Ch.5 Lemma 2.5; Ch.5 Lemma 2.8; Ch.5 Theorem 2.6; Ch.5 Theorem 2.7; Ch.5 Theorem 3.5
source.ref: Stein–Shakarchi: Ch.5 Lemma 2.4; Ch.5 Lemma 2.5; Ch.5 Lemma 2.8; Ch.5 Theorem 2.6; Ch.5 Theorem 2.7; Ch.5 Theorem 3.5

concepts.label: Poisson Summation Formula

content.statement: Periodizing a function in space corresponds to sampling its Fourier transform.
content.formal: For $f\in\mathcal S(\mathbb R)$, using $\hat f(\xi)=\int_{\mathbb R}f(x)e^{-2\pi i x\xi}\,dx$, one has $\sum_{n\in\mathbb Z}f(n)=\sum_{k\in\mathbb Z}\hat f(k)$.
content.intuition: The formula is the hinge between continuous and discrete Fourier analysis: adding copies of $f$ at integer shifts turns into reading $\hat f$ at integer frequencies.

assumptions: Stated here for Schwartz functions to avoid convergence side quests. Other versions require explicit decay and regularity hypotheses.

concepts.label: Scaling Rule

content.statement: Dilation in physical space produces inverse dilation in frequency.
content.formal: For $a\ne0$ and suitable $f$, if $g(x)=f(ax)$, then $\widehat g(\xi)=|a|^{-1}\hat f(\xi/a)$. More generally, if $g(x)=|a|^{-1}f(x/a)$, then $\widehat g(\xi)=\hat f(a\xi)$.
content.intuition: Squeezing a function in space spreads its transform in frequency, while spreading in space concentrates frequency. This is the calculation behind rescaled Gaussians and approximate identities.

assumptions: The formulas use the graph's $e^{-2\pi i x\xi}$ convention and hold first for $L^1$ or Schwartz functions.

concepts.label: SS 5.5.15 — Poisson summation gives the cosecant² identity

content.statement: Poisson summation yields $\sum(n+\alpha)^{-2}=\pi^2/\sin^2\pi\alpha$ and the cotangent sum.
content.formal: This exercise provides another example of periodization. (a) Apply the Poisson summation formula to the function $g$ in Exercise 2 to obtain $$\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty}\frac1{(n+\alpha)^2}=\frac{\pi^2}{(\sin \pi\alpha)^2}$$ whenever $\alpha\in\mathbb R\setminus\mathbb Z$. (b) Prove as a consequence that $$\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty}\frac1{n+\alpha}=\frac{\pi}{\tan\pi\alpha}$$ whenever $\alpha\in\mathbb R\setminus\mathbb Z$. Hint: First prove the identity when $0<\alpha<1$ by integrating the formula in part (a); specify the precise symmetric/principal-value meaning of the series on the left-hand side; then evaluate at $\alpha=1/2$ to determine the constant.
content.intuition: Poisson summation trades a one-term left side (the tent samples to a spike) for the full $1/(n+\alpha)^2$ series — recovering Euler's cosecant identity, whose integral is the cotangent.

steps.content:
Apply Poisson summation in the modulated form $\sum_n g(n)e^{-2\pi i\alpha n}=\sum_n\hat g(n+\alpha)$ (modulation shifts the transform). With $g(x)=(1-|x|)\chi_{[-1,1]}$ we have $\hat g(\xi)=\big(\frac{\sin\pi\xi}{\pi\xi}\big)^2$ (Ex. 2). The left side collapses since $g(n)=0$ for $|n|\ge1$ and $g(0)=1$: LHS $=1$. On the right, $\sin\pi(n+\alpha)=(-1)^n\sin\pi\alpha$, so
$$1=\sum_n\frac{\sin^2\pi(n+\alpha)}{\pi^2(n+\alpha)^2}=\frac{\sin^2\pi\alpha}{\pi^2}\sum_n\frac1{(n+\alpha)^2}\ \Longrightarrow\ \sum_{n}\frac1{(n+\alpha)^2}=\frac{\pi^2}{\sin^2\pi\alpha}.$$

The function $\alpha\mapsto\pi\cot\pi\alpha$ satisfies $\frac{d}{d\alpha}(\pi\cot\pi\alpha)=-\frac{\pi^2}{\sin^2\pi\alpha}=-\sum_n\frac1{(n+\alpha)^2}=\frac{d}{d\alpha}\sum_n\frac{-1}{n+\alpha}$ (differentiating the symmetric sum termwise). Both sides agree up to a constant; matching the odd symmetry about $\alpha=\tfrac12$ (or evaluating at $\alpha=\tfrac12$, where both vanish) fixes the constant to $0$:
$$\sum_{n=-\infty}^\infty\frac1{n+\alpha}=\pi\cot\pi\alpha=\frac{\pi}{\tan\pi\alpha}\qquad(\text{summed symmetrically}).$$

concepts.label: SS 5.5.16 — Periodizing the line Dirichlet kernel

content.statement: The periodization of the line Dirichlet kernel is the modified periodic kernel.
content.formal: The Dirichlet kernel on the real line is defined by $$\int_{-R}^{R}\widehat f(\xi)e^{2\pi ix\xi}\,d\xi=(f*D_R)(x),\qquad D_R(x)=\widehat{\chi}_{[-R,R]}(x)=\frac{\sin(2\pi Rx)}{\pi x}.$$ The modified Dirichlet kernel for periodic functions of period $1$ is $$D_N^*(x)=\sum_{|n|\le N-1}e^{2\pi inx}+\frac12\left(e^{-2\pi iNx}+e^{2\pi iNx}\right).$$ Show that Exercise 15 gives $$\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty}D_N(x+n)=D_N^*(x),$$ where $N\ge1$ is an integer and the infinite series is summed symmetrically. Equivalently, the periodization of $D_N$ is the modified Dirichlet kernel $D_N^*$.
content.intuition: Poisson summation samples a transform on the integers; periodizing the line Dirichlet kernel just reads off $\chi_{[-N,N]}$ at integers, with the half-weights at $\pm N$ producing the 'modified' kernel.

steps.content:
$D_R$ is the inverse transform of $\chi_{[-R,R]}$: indeed $\int_{-R}^R e^{2\pi ix\xi}d\xi=\frac{\sin2\pi Rx}{\pi x}=D_R(x)$, so $\widehat{D_R}=\chi_{[-R,R]}$. Poisson summation gives
$$\sum_{n}D_N(x+n)=\sum_{k}\widehat{D_N}(k)\,e^{2\pi ikx}=\sum_{k}\chi_{[-N,N]}(k)\,e^{2\pi ikx}.$$
For integer $N$ the indicator $\chi_{[-N,N]}$ equals $1$ at the interior integers $|k|\le N-1$ and takes the boundary value at $k=\pm N$. Summing the defining integral symmetrically assigns each endpoint weight $\tfrac12$ (the value of a jump indicator at its discontinuity), so
$$\sum_n D_N(x+n)=\sum_{|k|\le N-1}e^{2\pi ikx}+\tfrac12\big(e^{-2\pi iNx}+e^{2\pi iNx}\big)=D_N^*(x).$$

concepts.label: SS 5.5.2 — Transforms of the box and the triangle

content.statement: Fourier transforms of the indicator and the tent function.
content.formal: Let $$f(x)=\chi_{[-1,1]}(x)=\begin{cases}1,& |x|\le1,\\0,& |x|>1,\end{cases}\qquad g(x)=\begin{cases}1-|x|,& |x|\le1,\\0,& |x|>1.\end{cases}$$ Although $f$ is not continuous, the integral defining its Fourier transform still makes sense. Show that $$\widehat f(\xi)=\frac{\sin(2\pi\xi)}{\pi\xi},\qquad \widehat g(\xi)=\left(\frac{\sin(\pi\xi)}{\pi\xi}\right)^2,$$ with the removable values $\widehat f(0)=2$ and $\widehat g(0)=1$.
content.intuition: The box transforms to a sinc; convolving the box with itself makes the triangle, which squares the sinc — the cleanest illustration of the convolution theorem.

steps.content:
$$\widehat{\chi_{[-1,1]}}(\xi)=\int_{-1}^1 e^{-2\pi i x\xi}\,dx=\frac{e^{-2\pi i\xi}-e^{2\pi i\xi}}{-2\pi i\xi}=\frac{\sin2\pi\xi}{\pi\xi},$$
and $\hat f(0)=\int_{-1}^1 1\,dx=2$.

Since $g$ is even, $\hat g(\xi)=2\int_0^1(1-x)\cos(2\pi\xi x)\,dx$. Using $\int_0^1\cos=\frac{\sin2\pi\xi}{2\pi\xi}$ and $\int_0^1 x\cos(2\pi\xi x)dx=\frac{\sin2\pi\xi}{2\pi\xi}+\frac{\cos2\pi\xi-1}{(2\pi\xi)^2}$, the $\sin$ terms cancel and
$$\hat g(\xi)=2\cdot\frac{1-\cos2\pi\xi}{(2\pi\xi)^2}=\frac{2\cdot2\sin^2\pi\xi}{4\pi^2\xi^2}=\Big(\frac{\sin\pi\xi}{\pi\xi}\Big)^2,$$
with $\hat g(0)=1$. (Equivalently $g=\chi_{[-1/2,1/2]}*\chi_{[-1/2,1/2]}$, so $\hat g=(\widehat{\chi_{[-1/2,1/2]}})^2$.)

concepts.label: SS 5.5.23 — Heisenberg via the Hermite operator

content.statement: Operator (creation/annihilation) proof of the Heisenberg uncertainty principle.
content.formal: The Heisenberg uncertainty principle can be formulated in terms of the Hermite operator $$L=-\frac{d^2}{dx^2}+x^2,$$ acting on Schwartz functions by $$L(f)=-\frac{d^2f}{dx^2}+x^2f.$$ Use the usual inner product on $\mathcal S(\mathbb R)$, $$(f,g)=\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}f(x)\overline{g(x)}\,dx.$$ (a) Prove that the Heisenberg uncertainty principle implies $$(Lf,f)\ge(f,f)\qquad(f\in\mathcal S(\mathbb R)),$$ usually denoted $L\ge I$. Hint: Integrate by parts. (b) Define the annihilation and creation operators $$A(f)=\frac{df}{dx}+xf,\qquad A^*(f)=-\frac{df}{dx}+xf.$$ Prove that for all $f,g\in\mathcal S(\mathbb R)$, (i) $(Af,g)=(f,A^*g)$, (ii) $(Af,Af)=(A^*Af,f)\ge0$, and (iii) $A^*A=L-I$. In particular, this again shows $L\ge I$. (c) For $t\in\mathbb R$, define $$A_t(f)=\frac{df}{dx}+txf,\qquad A_t^*(f)=-\frac{df}{dx}+txf.$$ Use $(A_t^*A_tf,f)\ge0$ to give another proof of the Heisenberg uncertainty principle: if $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}|f(x)|^2\,dx=1$, then $$\left(\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}x^2|f(x)|^2\,dx\right)\left(\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\left|\frac{df}{dx}\right|^2\,dx\right)\ge\frac14.$$ Hint: Think of $(A_t^*A_tf,f)$ as a quadratic polynomial in $t$.
content.intuition: Factoring the Hermite operator as $A^*A+I$ makes positivity automatic; the $t$-family turns the uncertainty principle into 'a nonnegative quadratic has nonpositive discriminant'.

steps.content:
Integrating by parts, $(Lf,f)=\int(-f''+x^2f)\overline f=\int|f'|^2+\int x^2|f|^2=\|f'\|_2^2+\|xf\|_2^2$. By AM–GM and the core inequality $\|f\|_2^2\le2\|xf\|_2\|f'\|_2$ (the Heisenberg step),
$$(Lf,f)=\|f'\|_2^2+\|xf\|_2^2\ge2\|f'\|_2\|xf\|_2\ge\|f\|_2^2=(f,f).$$

\emph{(i)} $(Af,g)=\int(f'+xf)\overline g=\int f\,\overline{(-g'+xg)}=(f,A^*g)$ (boundary terms vanish for Schwartz $f,g$). \emph{(iii)} Compute $A^*A f=(-\frac{d}{dx}+x)(f'+xf)=-f''-(xf)'+xf'+x^2f=-f''+x^2f-f=(L-I)f$. \emph{(ii)} Hence $(A^*Af,f)=(Af,Af)=\|Af\|^2\ge0$, so $L-I=A^*A\ge0$, i.e. $L\ge I$ again.

For real $t$, $0\le\|A_tf\|_2^2=\int|f'+txf|^2=\|f'\|_2^2+t^2\|xf\|_2^2+t\int x(|f|^2)'$. Since $\int x(|f|^2)'=-\int|f|^2=-\|f\|_2^2$ (parts), this is the quadratic in $t$
$$q(t)=\|xf\|_2^2\,t^2-\|f\|_2^2\,t+\|f'\|_2^2\ge0\quad\forall t.$$
A nonnegative quadratic has discriminant $\le0$: $\|f\|_2^4\le4\|xf\|_2^2\|f'\|_2^2$. With $\|f\|_2=1$, $\|xf\|_2^2\|f'\|_2^2\ge\frac14$, and via $\|f'\|_2=2\pi\|\xi\hat f\|_2$ this is the Heisenberg uncertainty bound $\ge\frac1{16\pi^2}$.

content.statement: Spectral decay $O(|\xi|^{-1-\alpha})$ yields Hölder-$\alpha$ continuity of $f$.
content.formal: This exercise illustrates the principle that decay of $\widehat f$ is related to continuity properties of $f$. Suppose $f$ is a function of moderate decrease on $\mathbb R$, and suppose its Fourier transform $\widehat f$ is continuous and satisfies $$\widehat f(\xi)=O\!\left(\frac1{|\xi|^{1+\alpha}}\right)\qquad(|\xi|\to\infty)$$ for some $0<\alpha<1$. Prove that $f$ satisfies a Hölder condition of order $\alpha$: there exists $M>0$ such that $$|f(x+h)-f(x)|\le M|h|^\alpha\qquad(x,h\in\mathbb R).$$ Hint: use Fourier inversion to express $f(x+h)-f(x)$ as an integral involving $\widehat f$, then estimate separately on $|\xi|\le1/|h|$ and $|\xi|\ge1/|h|$.
content.intuition: Inversion writes a finite difference as a frequency integral against $e^{2\pi ih\xi}-1$; balancing the two regimes at $|\xi|\sim1/|h|$ converts spectral decay into a modulus of continuity.

steps.content:
By Fourier inversion, $f(x)=\int_{\mathbb R}\hat f(\xi)e^{2\pi ix\xi}\,d\xi$, so
$$f(x+h)-f(x)=\int_{\mathbb R}\hat f(\xi)e^{2\pi ix\xi}\big(e^{2\pi ih\xi}-1\big)\,d\xi,\qquad |f(x+h)-f(x)|\le\int_{\mathbb R}|\hat f(\xi)|\,|e^{2\pi ih\xi}-1|\,d\xi.$$
Split at $|\xi|=1/|h|$ and use $|\hat f(\xi)|\le C|\xi|^{-1-\alpha}$:

$|\xi|\le1/|h|$: with $|e^{2\pi ih\xi}-1|\le2\pi|h||\xi|$,
$$\int_{|\xi|\le1/|h|}\!\!\!|\hat f||e^{2\pi ih\xi}-1|\le2\pi|h|\,C\!\int_{|\xi|\le1/|h|}\!\!|\xi|^{-\alpha}d\xi=2\pi C|h|\cdot\frac{2}{1-\alpha}|h|^{-(1-\alpha)}=C_1|h|^\alpha.$$

$|\xi|\ge1/|h|$: with $|e^{2\pi ih\xi}-1|\le2$,
$$\int_{|\xi|\ge1/|h|}2\,|\hat f|\le 2C\int_{|\xi|\ge1/|h|}|\xi|^{-1-\alpha}d\xi=2C\cdot\frac2\alpha|h|^{\alpha}=C_2|h|^\alpha.$$
Adding, $|f(x+h)-f(x)|\le(C_1+C_2)|h|^\alpha$.

concepts.label: SS 5.5.5 — F̂ continuous, vanishing, and injectivity

content.statement: Continuity, decay, and injectivity of the Fourier transform on moderate-decrease functions.
content.formal: Suppose $f$ is continuous and of moderate decrease. (a) Prove that $\widehat f$ is continuous and that $\widehat f(\xi)\to0$ as $|\xi|\to\infty$. (b) Show that if $\widehat f(\xi)=0$ for every $\xi$, then $f$ is identically zero. Hint for (a): for $\xi\ne0$, use the half-period shift identity $$\widehat f(\xi)=\frac12\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\left(f(x)-f\!\left(x-\frac1{2\xi}\right)\right)e^{-2\pi ix\xi}\,dx.$$ Hint for (b): verify that the multiplication formula $$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x)\widehat g(x)\,dx=\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\widehat f(y)g(y)\,dy$$ still holds whenever $g\in\mathcal S(\mathbb R)$.
content.intuition: The half-shift trick is Riemann–Lebesgue in disguise; the multiplication formula then turns 'all moments against Schwartz functions vanish' into injectivity of the transform.

steps.content:
$|f(x)e^{-2\pi ix\xi}|\le|f(x)|\in L^1$ (moderate decrease), and $\xi\mapsto f(x)e^{-2\pi ix\xi}$ is continuous; dominated convergence gives $\hat f$ continuous. \textbf{Decay.} Using $e^{-2\pi ix\xi}=-e^{-2\pi i(x-1/(2\xi))\xi}$ (since $e^{\pi i}=-1$), substitute $x\mapsto x-1/(2\xi)$ in half of the integral:
$$\hat f(\xi)=\tfrac12\int_{\mathbb R}\big[f(x)-f\big(x-\tfrac1{2\xi}\big)\big]e^{-2\pi ix\xi}\,dx.$$
As $|\xi|\to\infty$, $f(x)-f(x-\frac1{2\xi})\to0$ pointwise and is dominated by an $L^1$ function (moderate decrease + continuity), so $\hat f(\xi)\to0$ — the Riemann–Lebesgue lemma.

The multiplication formula $\int_{\mathbb R}f(x)\hat g(x)\,dx=\int_{\mathbb R}\hat f(y)g(y)\,dy$ holds for $g\in\mathcal S$ (Fubini). If $\hat f\equiv0$ then $\int f\hat g=0$ for all $g\in\mathcal S$. But $\{\hat g:g\in\mathcal S\}=\mathcal S$, which is rich enough (e.g. approximate identities) to force $f=0$ at every point of continuity; since $f$ is continuous, $f\equiv0$.

concepts.label: SS 5.5.6 — Self-dual functions and F⁴ = I

content.statement: The Fourier transform satisfies $\mathcal F^4=I$; its eigenvalues are the 4th roots of unity.
content.formal: The Gaussian $e^{-\pi x^2}$ is its own Fourier transform. Generate other functions that, up to a constant multiple, are their own Fourier transforms. Determine what the possible constant multiples can be by proving $$\mathcal F^4=I,$$ where $\mathcal F(f)=\widehat f$, $\mathcal F^4=\mathcal F\circ\mathcal F\circ\mathcal F\circ\mathcal F$, and $I$ is the identity operator $If=f$.
content.intuition: Two transforms reflect, four restore — so the Fourier transform is an order-4 unitary, its spectrum confined to the fourth roots of unity, with Hermite functions as the eigenbasis.

steps.content: For Schwartz $f$, Fourier inversion says $\mathcal F^2 f(x)=f(-x)$ (transforming twice reflects). Applying it again, $\mathcal F^4 f(x)=\mathcal F^2 f(-x)=f(x)$, so $\mathcal F^4=I$.
If $\mathcal F f=\lambda f$ with $f\neq0$, then $\lambda^4 f=\mathcal F^4 f=f$, so $\lambda^4=1$: the only possible eigenvalues are $\{1,i,-1,-i\}$ — these are the constants that can occur.

The Gaussian $h_0=e^{-\pi x^2}$ has $\mathcal F h_0=h_0$ ($\lambda=1$). The raising operator $(x-\frac1{2\pi}\frac{d}{dx})$ generates the Hermite functions $h_n$, and one checks $\mathcal F h_n=(-i)^n h_n$. Thus eigenvalue $1$ is hit by $h_0,h_4,\dots$; eigenvalue $-1$ by $h_2,h_6,\dots$, etc. Concretely $\big(x^2-\frac1{4\pi^2}\big)e^{-\pi x^2}$-type combinations are self-dual up to sign.

content.statement: $\mathcal M(\mathbb R)$ is closed under convolution.
content.formal: Prove that the convolution of two functions of moderate decrease is again a function of moderate decrease. Hint: split $$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x-y)g(y)\,dy=\int_{|y|\le |x|/2}f(x-y)g(y)\,dy+\int_{|y|\ge |x|/2}f(x-y)g(y)\,dy.$$ In the first integral, use $f(x-y)=O((1+x^2)^{-1})$; in the second, use $g(y)=O((1+x^2)^{-1})$.
content.intuition: Whichever factor is far from the bulk supplies the $1/x^2$ tail; splitting the integral by which variable is large makes the decay transfer to the convolution.

steps.content:
Let $|f(x)|,|g(x)|\le\frac{A}{1+x^2}$. Split the convolution at $|y|=|x|/2$:
$$(f*g)(x)=\int_{|y|\le|x|/2}f(x-y)g(y)\,dy+\int_{|y|\ge|x|/2}f(x-y)g(y)\,dy.$$

$|y|\le|x|/2\Rightarrow|x-y|\ge|x|/2$, so $|f(x-y)|\le\frac{A}{1+(x/2)^2}=O\big(\frac1{1+x^2}\big)$. Pulling this bound out,
$$\Big|\int_{|y|\le|x|/2}\Big|\le O\Big(\frac1{1+x^2}\Big)\int_{\mathbb R}|g|=O\Big(\frac1{1+x^2}\Big).$$

$|y|\ge|x|/2\Rightarrow|g(y)|\le\frac{A}{1+(x/2)^2}=O\big(\frac1{1+x^2}\big)$, so likewise $\big|\int_{|y|\ge|x|/2}\big|\le O(\frac1{1+x^2})\int|f|$. Hence $(f*g)(x)=O(1/(1+x^2))$; continuity of $f*g$ is standard (dominated convergence). So $f*g$ has moderate decrease.

concepts.label: SS 6.6.1 — Planar rotations as complex multiplication

content.statement: Identify 2D orthogonal matrices with complex multiplication/conjugation.
content.formal: Let $R:\mathbb R^2\to\mathbb R^2$ be a rotation, and write its matrix in the standard basis as $$R=\begin{pmatrix}a&b\\ c&d\end{pmatrix}.$$ (a) Express the conditions $R^t=R^{-1}$ and $\det R=\pm1$ as equations involving $a,b,c,d$. (b) Show that there exists $\varphi\in\mathbb R$ such that $a+ib=e^{i\varphi}$. (c) Deduce the complex description of planar rotations: if $R$ is proper, then, under the identification $(x,y)\leftrightarrow z=x+iy$, $R$ has the form $z\mapsto ze^{i\varphi}$; if $R$ is improper, then it has the form $z\mapsto \overline z e^{i\varphi}$, where $\overline z=x-iy$.
content.intuition: In two dimensions the orthogonal group is just the unit complex numbers: proper rotations are multiplication by $e^{i\varphi}$, improper ones are that composed with conjugation.

steps.content: $R^tR=I$ says the columns are orthonormal: $a^2+c^2=1$, $b^2+d^2=1$, $ab+cd=0$; and $\det R=ad-bc=\pm1$.
From orthonormality, $(a,c)$ is a unit vector, so $a^2+c^2=1$ and we may write $a=\cos\varphi$, $c=\sin\varphi$; then $a+ic=e^{i\varphi}$ (relabelling, $a+ib=e^{i\varphi}$ in the proper case below).

\emph{Proper} ($\det=+1$): the conditions force $d=a$, $c=-b$ with $a^2+b^2=1$. Identifying $(x,y)\leftrightarrow z=x+iy$, $R$ acts by
$$(ax+by)+i(-bx+ay)=(a-ib)(x+iy)=e^{i\varphi}z,$$
a rotation by complex multiplication with $|e^{i\varphi}|=1$. \emph{Improper} ($\det=-1$): then $d=-a$, $c=b$, and $R$ acts as $z\mapsto e^{i\varphi}\bar z$ — a reflection.

concepts.label: SS 6.6.2 — Every 3D proper rotation has an axis

content.statement: Euler's theorem: every proper 3D rotation fixes an axis.
content.formal: Let $R:\mathbb R^3\to\mathbb R^3$ be a proper rotation. (a) Show that $$p(t)=\det(R-tI)$$ is a polynomial of degree $3$, and prove that there exists a unit vector $\gamma\in S^2$ such that $$R(\gamma)=\gamma.$$ Hint: since $p(0)>0$ and $p$ is cubic, find $\lambda>0$ with $p(\lambda)=0$; then $R-\lambda I$ has nontrivial kernel. (b) If $P=\gamma^\perp$ is the plane through the origin perpendicular to $\gamma$, prove that $R(P)\subseteq P$ and that the restricted map $R:P\to P$ is a planar rotation.
content.intuition: A real cubic has a real root and orthogonality pins it to $+1$ — so a 3D rotation always fixes an axis and spins the perpendicular plane (Euler's theorem).

steps.content: $p(t)=\det(R-tI)$ is a real cubic with leading term $-t^3$, so $p(t)\to-\infty$ as $t\to+\infty$, while $p(0)=\det R=1>0$. By the intermediate value theorem $p$ has a positive real root $\lambda$, and since $R$ is orthogonal all eigenvalues have modulus $1$, forcing $\lambda=1$. (Indeed the eigenvalues are $\{1,e^{\pm i\theta}\}$, their product being $\det R=1$.) Thus $R-I$ is singular: pick a unit vector $\gamma$ in its kernel, $R\gamma=\gamma$.

If $v\perp\gamma$ then $\langle Rv,\gamma\rangle=\langle Rv,R\gamma\rangle=\langle v,\gamma\rangle=0$ (orthogonality of $R$), so $Rv\in P$: $R$ maps $P=\gamma^\perp$ to itself. The restriction $R|_P$ is orthogonal with $\det(R|_P)=\det R/1=1$, hence a planar rotation (Exercise 1).

concepts.label: SS 6.6.3 — Rotation invariance of surface measure

content.statement: Spherical surface measure is invariant under rotations.
content.formal: Using the polar-coordinate formula $$\int_{\mathbb R^d}F(x)\,dx=\int_{S^{d-1}}\int_0^\infty F(r\gamma)r^{d-1}\,dr\,d\sigma(\gamma),$$ apply it to functions of the separated form $F(x)=g(r)f(\gamma)$, where $x=r\gamma$, to prove rotation invariance of surface measure: for every rotation $R$ and every continuous function $f$ on $S^{d-1}$, $$\int_{S^{d-1}} f(R\gamma)\,d\sigma(\gamma)=\int_{S^{d-1}}f(\gamma)\,d\sigma(\gamma).$$
content.intuition: Surface measure inherits rotation invariance from Lebesgue measure: separate radius from direction, and the common radial factor cancels.

steps.content:
Apply the polar formula to $F(x)=g(|x|)f(x/|x|)$ for a fixed radial profile $g\ge0$ with $0<\int_0^\infty g(r)r^{d-1}dr<\infty$:
$$\int_{\mathbb R^d}g(|x|)f(x/|x|)\,dx=\Big(\int_0^\infty g(r)r^{d-1}dr\Big)\Big(\int_{S^{d-1}}f(\gamma)\,d\sigma(\gamma)\Big).$$
Lebesgue measure on $\mathbb R^d$ is rotation invariant, and $|Rx|=|x|$, $(Rx)/|Rx|=R(x/|x|)$, so replacing $x\mapsto R^{-1}x$ shows the left side is unchanged if $f$ is replaced by $f\circ R$. The radial factor $\int_0^\infty g\,r^{d-1}dr$ is the same and nonzero, so cancelling it gives
$$\int_{S^{d-1}}f(R\gamma)\,d\sigma(\gamma)=\int_{S^{d-1}}f(\gamma)\,d\sigma(\gamma).$$

concepts.label: SS 6.6.5 — Gaussian integral for a positive-definite form

content.statement: Anisotropic Gaussian integral equals $(\det A)^{-1/2}$.
content.formal: Let $A$ be a real symmetric positive-definite $d\times d$ matrix. Prove the anisotropic Gaussian integral formula $$\int_{\mathbb R^d}e^{-\pi(x,A(x))}\,dx=(\det A)^{-1/2}.$$ This extends the standard identity $$\int_{\mathbb R^d}e^{-\pi|x|^2}\,dx=1,$$ corresponding to $A=I$. Hint: use the spectral theorem to write $A=RDR^{-1}$, where $R$ is a rotation and $D=\operatorname{diag}(\lambda_1,\dots,\lambda_d)$ has the eigenvalues of $A$ on the diagonal.
content.intuition: Diagonalising the quadratic form decouples the Gaussian into one-dimensional pieces; the eigenvalues multiply into the determinant.

steps.content:
By the spectral theorem write $A=RDR^t$ with $R$ a rotation and $D=\operatorname{diag}(\lambda_1,\dots,\lambda_d)$, $\lambda_i>0$ the eigenvalues. Substitute $y=R^tx$ (Jacobian $|\det R|=1$); since $(x,Ax)=(R^tx,DR^tx)=(y,Dy)=\sum_i\lambda_i y_i^2$, the integral factorises:
$$\int_{\mathbb R^d}e^{-\pi(x,Ax)}dx=\prod_{i=1}^d\int_{\mathbb R}e^{-\pi\lambda_i y_i^2}\,dy_i=\prod_{i=1}^d\lambda_i^{-1/2}=\Big(\prod_i\lambda_i\Big)^{-1/2}=(\det A)^{-1/2},$$
using $\int_{\mathbb R}e^{-\pi\lambda y^2}dy=\lambda^{-1/2}$ and $\det A=\prod_i\lambda_i$.

concepts.label: SS 6.6.6 — Heisenberg uncertainty in d dimensions

content.statement: The $d$-dimensional Heisenberg uncertainty inequality.
content.formal: Let $\psi\in\mathcal S(\mathbb R^d)$ satisfy the normalization $$\int_{\mathbb R^d}|\psi(x)|^2\,dx=1.$$ Prove the $d$-dimensional Heisenberg uncertainty inequality $$\left(\int_{\mathbb R^d}|x|^2|\psi(x)|^2\,dx\right)\left(\int_{\mathbb R^d}|\xi|^2|\widehat\psi(\xi)|^2\,d\xi\right)\ge \frac{d^2}{16\pi^2}.$$
content.intuition: The divergence identity for $x|\psi|^2$ gives a factor $d$ on the left; Cauchy–Schwarz plus Plancherel then spreads the uncertainty over all $d$ coordinates.

steps.content:
Since $\operatorname{div}(x|\psi|^2)=d|\psi|^2+x\cdot
abla|\psi|^2$ integrates to $0$ (Schwartz decay),
$$d=d\int|\psi|^2=-\int x\cdot
abla|\psi|^2\,dx=-2\operatorname{Re}\int(x\cdot
abla\psi)\,\overline\psi\,dx.$$
By Cauchy–Schwarz (and $|x\cdot
abla\psi|\le|x|\,|
abla\psi|$),
$$d\le2\Big(\int|x|^2|\psi|^2\Big)^{1/2}\Big(\int|
abla\psi|^2\Big)^{1/2}.$$
Plancherel coordinate-by-coordinate gives $\int|
abla\psi|^2=\sum_j\int|\partial_j\psi|^2=\sum_j4\pi^2\int\xi_j^2|\hat\psi|^2=4\pi^2\int|\xi|^2|\hat\psi|^2$. Substituting,
$$d\le2\Big(\int|x|^2|\psi|^2\Big)^{1/2}\cdot2\pi\Big(\int|\xi|^2|\hat\psi|^2\Big)^{1/2}\ \Longrightarrow\ \Big(\int|x|^2|\psi|^2\Big)\Big(\int|\xi|^2|\hat\psi|^2\Big)\ge rac{d^2}{16\pi^2}.$$

concepts.label: SS 6.6.7 — The d-dimensional heat kernel solves the heat equation

content.statement: The heat kernel on $\mathbb R^d$ smooths data and attains the initial condition.
content.formal: Consider the time-dependent heat equation on $\mathbb R^d$, $$\frac{\partial u}{\partial t}=\frac{\partial^2u}{\partial x_1^2}+\cdots+\frac{\partial^2u}{\partial x_d^2},\qquad t>0,$$ with boundary value $u(x,0)=f(x)$ for $f\in\mathcal S(\mathbb R^d)$. Define the $d$-dimensional heat kernel by $$H_t^{(d)}(x)=\frac{1}{(4\pi t)^{d/2}}e^{-|x|^2/(4t)}=\int_{\mathbb R^d}e^{-4\pi^2t|\xi|^2}e^{2\pi i x\cdot\xi}\,d\xi.$$ Show that $$u(x,t)=(f*H_t^{(d)})(x)$$ is $C^\infty$ in $x$ for $t>0$, solves the heat equation, and extends continuously to $t=0$ with $u(x,0)=f(x)$. Also formulate the $d$-dimensional analogues of the one-dimensional heat-kernel convergence theorems from Chapter 5.
content.intuition: Heat flow is the Fourier multiplier $e^{-4\pi^2t|\xi|^2}$: it smooths instantly (super-polynomial decay), realises $\partial_t=\Delta$, and relaxes to the identity as $t\to0$.

steps.content:
On the Fourier side, $\widehat{H_t^{(d)}}(\xi)=e^{-4\pi^2t|\xi|^2}$, so
$$\hat u(\xi,t)=\hat f(\xi)\,e^{-4\pi^2t|\xi|^2}.$$

For $t>0$ the factor $e^{-4\pi^2t|\xi|^2}$ decays faster than any polynomial, and $\hat f\in\mathcal S$; hence $\xi^\alpha\partial_t^m\hat u\in L^1$ for all $\alpha,m$, so differentiation under the inversion integral is justified and $u\in C^\infty$.
Differentiating $\hat u$: $\partial_t\hat u=-4\pi^2|\xi|^2\hat u$, while $\widehat{\Delta u}=-4\pi^2|\xi|^2\hat u$. Since the transform is injective, $\partial_t u=\Delta u$.

As $t\to0^+$, $\hat u(\xi,t)\to\hat f(\xi)$ pointwise with $|\hat u|\le|\hat f|\in L^1$; by dominated convergence in the inversion formula $u(x,t)\to f(x)$ uniformly. So $u$ is continuous up to $t=0$ with $u(\cdot,0)=f$.
(This is the $\mathbb R^d$ form of the approximate-identity statement; cf. the 2-D exam problem.)

concepts.label: Transform of $L^1$ is Continuous & Decays

content.statement: The Fourier transform of an integrable function is continuous and vanishes at infinity.
content.definition: $$f\in L^1(\mathbb R)\Longrightarrow \widehat f\in C_0(\mathbb R),\qquad \lim_{|\xi|\to\infty}\widehat f(\xi)=0.$$
content.formal: If $f\in L^1(\mathbb R)$, then $\hat f\in C_0(\mathbb R)$: it is continuous and $\hat f(\xi)\to0$ as $|\xi|\to\infty$.
content.intuition: Integrability makes high-frequency oscillations average out. This is the Riemann-Lebesgue lemma on the line.

assumptions: The proof uses dominated convergence for continuity and $L^1$ approximation by step functions for decay.

steps.content: Let $f\in L^1(\mathbb R)$ and define $$\widehat f(\xi)=\int_{\mathbb R}f(x)e^{-2\pi i x\xi}\,dx.$$ The integral is absolutely convergent for every $\xi$, and $$|\widehat f(\xi)|\le\|f\|_1.$$

If $\xi_k\to\xi$, then for every $x$, $$f(x)e^{-2\pi i x\xi_k}\to f(x)e^{-2\pi i x\xi}.$$ Also, $$|f(x)e^{-2\pi i x\xi_k}|=|f(x)|,$$ and $f\in L^1(\mathbb R)$. Dominated convergence gives $$\widehat f(\xi_k)\to\widehat f(\xi).$$ Hence $\widehat f$ is continuous.

For an interval indicator, direct integration gives $$\widehat{\mathbf 1_{[a,b]}}(\xi)=\int_a^b e^{-2\pi i x\xi}\,dx=\frac{e^{-2\pi ia\xi}-e^{-2\pi ib\xi}}{2\pi i\xi},\qquad \xi\ne0,$$ so $$\widehat{\mathbf 1_{[a,b]}}(\xi)\to0$$ as $|\xi|\to\infty$. By linearity, the same holds for finite linear combinations of interval indicators.

Choose a simple interval step function $s$ with $$\|f-s\|_1<\varepsilon.$$ Then $$|\widehat f(\xi)|\le |\widehat{s}(\xi)|+|\widehat{f-s}(\xi)|\le |\widehat{s}(\xi)|+\|f-s\|_1.$$ Since $\widehat{s}(\xi)	o0$, for large $|\xi|$ we get $$|\widehat f(\xi)|<2\varepsilon.$$ Therefore $$\widehat f(\xi)\xrightarrow[|\xi|\to\infty]{}0.$$

The Fourier transform maps $L^1(\mathbb R)$ into continuous functions vanishing at infinity: $$f\in L^1(\mathbb R)\Longrightarrow \widehat f\in C_0(\mathbb R).$$ This is the real-line Riemann--Lebesgue lemma.

source.citation: Stein–Shakarchi: Fourier Analysis, Ch.5 Proposition 1.4; UC Davis notes on Fourier transform on $L^1$
source.ref: Stein–Shakarchi: Fourier Analysis, Ch.5 Proposition 1.4; UC Davis notes on Fourier transform on $L^1$

concepts.label: Translation & Modulation

content.statement: Translation in space becomes modulation in frequency, and modulation in space becomes translation in frequency.
content.formal: For suitable $f$, $\widehat{f(\cdot-a)}(\xi)=e^{-2\pi ia\xi}\hat f(\xi)$ and $\widehat{e^{2\pi ibx}f(x)}(\xi)=\hat f(\xi-b)$.
content.intuition: Moving a signal changes phases but not frequency magnitudes; multiplying by a pure wave shifts the frequency content.

assumptions: Uses the graph's $2\pi$-frequency convention.
The formulas hold for $L^1$ functions; Schwartz functions give a safe default setting.

steps.content: Let $f$ be integrable, or take $f\in\mathcal S(\mathbb R)$ for a safe proof. Use $$\widehat f(\xi)=\int_{\mathbb R}f(x)e^{-2\pi i x\xi}\,dx.$$

For $a\in\mathbb R$, compute with $u=x-a$: $$\begin{aligned} \widehat{f(\cdot-a)}(\xi)&=\int_{\mathbb R}f(x-a)e^{-2\pi i x\xi}\,dx\\ &=\int_{\mathbb R}f(u)e^{-2\pi i(u+a)\xi}\,du\\ &=e^{-2\pi ia\xi}\widehat f(\xi). \end{aligned}$$ Thus spatial translation changes phase in frequency.

For $b\in\mathbb R$, compute directly: $$\begin{aligned} \widehat{e^{2\pi ibx}f(x)}(\xi)&=\int_{\mathbb R}e^{2\pi ibx}f(x)e^{-2\pi i x\xi}\,dx\\ &=\int_{\mathbb R}f(x)e^{-2\pi i x(\xi-b)}\,dx=\widehat f(\xi-b). \end{aligned}$$ Thus multiplying by a pure frequency shifts the Fourier transform.

source.citation: Stein–Shakarchi: Fourier Analysis, Ch.5 Proposition 1.2; UC Davis notes on Fourier transform properties
source.ref: Stein–Shakarchi: Fourier Analysis, Ch.5 Proposition 1.2; UC Davis notes on Fourier transform properties

concepts.label: Uncertainty Principle

content.statement: A nonzero function and its Fourier transform cannot both be arbitrarily concentrated.
content.formal: If $f\in L^2(\mathbb R)$, $xf\in L^2$, $\xi\hat f\in L^2$, and $\|f\|_2=1$, then $\Delta_x(f)\Delta_\xi(\hat f)\ge \frac1{4\pi}$, where $\Delta_x,\Delta_\xi$ are centered standard deviations in position and frequency.
content.intuition: Narrow localization in $x$ requires many frequencies; narrow localization in frequency forces spreading in $x$. Gaussians are the optimally balanced case.

assumptions: Uses centered variances; equality occurs for Gaussian wave packets under the $2\pi$-frequency convention.

concepts.label: Variance and Standard Deviation

content.statement: The centered second moment measuring spread.
content.formal: For a nonnegative normalized density $\rho$ on $\mathbb R$ with finite second moment, $\mu=\int_{\mathbb R} x\rho(x)\,dx$ and $\Delta_x^2=\int_{\mathbb R}(x-\mu)^2\rho(x)\,dx$. In uncertainty estimates for nonzero $f\in L^2(\mathbb R)$, $\rho(x)=|f(x)|^2/\|f\|_2^2$ when the relevant moments are finite.
content.intuition: The uncertainty principle is about spread around the center of mass, not just raw distance from zero.

assumptions: $\rho$ has finite first and second moments.
For uncertainty estimates, $f\ne0$ and the position and frequency variances are finite.

concepts.label: Øving 10 · P1 — Heisenberg for non-normalized f

content.statement: The scale-free form of the Heisenberg inequality.
content.formal: Show Heisenberg's uncertainty principle can be written, for arbitrary $f$ (not assuming $\|f\|_2=1$), as $\|xf\|_2\,\|2\pi\xi\hat f\|_2=\|xf\|_2\,\|f'\|_2\ge\tfrac12\|f\|_2^2$.
content.intuition: Stripping the normalization shows uncertainty is a homogeneous inequality: the product of spreads is bounded below by $\tfrac12\|f\|_2^2$, scaling correctly under $f\mapsto\lambda f$.

steps.content:
Plancherel applied to $f'$ gives $\|f'\|_2=\|\widehat{f'}\|_2=\|2\pi i\xi\hat f\|_2=\|2\pi\xi\hat f\|_2$, which is the first equality. For the inequality, integrate $\|f\|_2^2=\int|f|^2$ by parts (boundary term vanishes for Schwartz $f$, as $x|f|^2\to0$):
$$\|f\|_2^2=-\int_{\mathbb R}x\,\frac{d}{dx}|f|^2\,dx=-2\,\mathrm{Re}\int_{\mathbb R}x\,\overline f\,f'\,dx\le 2\int_{\mathbb R}|xf|\,|f'|\,dx\le 2\,\|xf\|_2\,\|f'\|_2$$
(Cauchy–Schwarz). Dividing by $2$,
$$\|xf\|_2\,\|f'\|_2\ge\tfrac12\|f\|_2^2,\qquad\text{equivalently}\qquad \|xf\|_2\,\|2\pi\xi\hat f\|_2\ge\tfrac12\|f\|_2^2.$$
$\blacksquare$ Normalizing $\|f\|_2=1$ recovers $\|xf\|_2^2\|\xi\hat f\|_2^2\ge\frac1{16\pi^2}$.

concepts.label: Øving 11 · P1 — Fourier transforms in ℝ³

content.statement: Compute 3-D Fourier transforms of a separable and a radial function.
content.intuition: Product functions transform coordinate-wise (Lorentzian → exponential per axis); radial functions reduce to a single sine integral, turning $e^{-|x|}$ into a squared-Lorentzian.

steps.content:
\textbf{(i) Separable (tensor) function.} Since $f(x)=\prod_{j=1}^3\frac1{1+x_j^2}$ and $e^{-2\pi ix\cdot\xi}=\prod_j e^{-2\pi ix_j\xi_j}$, the transform factorizes into one-dimensional transforms. By Øving 8 P3 (with $a=1$), $\mathcal F\big[\tfrac1{1+x^2}\big](\eta)=\pi e^{-2\pi|\eta|}$, so
$$\hat f(\xi)=\prod_{j=1}^3\pi e^{-2\pi|\xi_j|}=\pi^3\,e^{-2\pi(|\xi_1|+|\xi_2|+|\xi_3|)}.$$

\textbf{(ii) Radial function.} For radial $f$ in $\mathbb R^3$, the transform is radial with $\hat f(\xi)=\frac{2}{\rho}\int_0^\infty f(r)\,r\sin(2\pi r\rho)\,dr$, $\rho=|\xi|$ (from $\int_{S^2}e^{-2\pi ir\rho\cos\theta}\,d\sigma=\frac{2\sin(2\pi r\rho)}{r\rho}$). With $f(r)=e^{-r}$ and $b=2\pi\rho$, $\int_0^\infty e^{-r}r\sin(br)\,dr=\frac{2b}{(1+b^2)^2}$, so
$$\hat f(\xi)=\frac2\rho\cdot\frac{2b}{(1+b^2)^2}=\frac{8\pi}{(1+4\pi^2|\xi|^2)^2}.$$
$\blacksquare$

concepts.label: Øving 8 · P1 — Transform of the rescaled Gaussian

content.statement: Fourier transform of the unit-mass Gaussian family (self-dual at $\delta=1$).
content.formal: Let $K(x)=e^{-\pi x^2}$ and $K_\delta(x)=\delta^{-1/2}K(\delta^{-1/2}x)$. Compute $\hat K_\delta$.
content.intuition: Scaling a Gaussian narrows it in space and widens it in frequency; the unit-mass family $K_\delta$ maps to the spreading multiplier $e^{-\pi\delta\xi^2}\to1$.

steps.content:
Start from the self-duality $\hat K=K$ (the Gaussian $e^{-\pi x^2}$ is its own transform) and the scaling law $\widehat{f(ax)}(\xi)=a^{-1}\hat f(a^{-1}\xi)$ for $a>0$. With $a=\delta^{-1/2}$,
$$\widehat{K(\delta^{-1/2}x)}(\xi)=\delta^{1/2}K(\delta^{1/2}\xi)=\delta^{1/2}e^{-\pi\delta\xi^2}.$$
Therefore $\hat K_\delta(\xi)=\delta^{-1/2}\cdot\delta^{1/2}e^{-\pi\delta\xi^2}=e^{-\pi\delta\xi^2}$. In particular at $\delta=1$ this recovers $\hat K=K$ — the Gaussian is exactly self-dual — while for general $\delta$ the unit-mass family $K_\delta$ (with $\int K_\delta=1$) transforms to $e^{-\pi\delta\xi^2}$. $\blacksquare$
(This $\hat K_\delta(\xi)=e^{-\pi\delta\xi^2}$ is precisely the multiplier used in P2.)

concepts.label: Øving 8 · P2 — Gaussian multiplier and Fourier inversion

content.statement: Gaussian regularization and the limit underlying Fourier inversion.
content.formal: (a) Show $\hat K_\delta(\xi)=e^{-\pi\delta\xi^2}\to1$ locally uniformly as $\delta\to0$. (b) For $f\in\mathcal S(\mathbb R)$, show $\int_{\mathbb R}\hat f(\xi)\hat K_\delta(\xi)\,d\xi\to\int_{\mathbb R}\hat f(\xi)\,d\xi$.
content.intuition: The spreading Gaussian multiplier $\to1$ boundedly, so it can be inserted and removed under the integral by dominated convergence — the analytic heart of Fourier inversion.

steps.content:
\textbf{(a)} On a compact set $|\xi|\le R$, $0\le 1-e^{-\pi\delta\xi^2}\le\pi\delta R^2\to0$ as $\delta\to0$, so $\hat K_\delta\to1$ uniformly on $[-R,R]$ (locally uniformly).

\textbf{(b)} For $f\in\mathcal S$, $\hat f\in\mathcal S\subset L^1$. The integrands $\hat f(\xi)\hat K_\delta(\xi)$ converge pointwise to $\hat f(\xi)$ and are dominated by $|\hat f(\xi)|\in L^1$ (since $0<\hat K_\delta\le1$). By dominated convergence,
$$\int_{\mathbb R}\hat f(\xi)\hat K_\delta(\xi)\,d\xi\to\int_{\mathbb R}\hat f(\xi)\,d\xi.\qquad\blacksquare$$
Combined with the multiplication formula $\int\hat f K_\delta=\int f\hat K_\delta$ and $\hat K_\delta=K_\delta$-type approximate identity, this is the standard route to the Fourier inversion theorem (the Gaussian regularization).

content.statement: Fourier transform of the Lorentzian $1/(a^2+x^2)$.
content.formal: Show $\mathcal F\!\big[\tfrac1{a^2+x^2}\big](\xi)=\tfrac\pi a e^{-2\pi a|\xi|}$ for $a>0$.
content.intuition: The Lorentzian and the two-sided exponential are a Fourier pair (the line Poisson kernel); compute the easy direction and invert.

steps.content:
First compute the transform of the (even) two-sided exponential:
$$\mathcal F[e^{-2\pi a|x|}](\xi)=\int_{\mathbb R}e^{-2\pi a|x|}e^{-2\pi ix\xi}\,dx=2\int_0^\infty e^{-2\pi ax}\cos(2\pi\xi x)\,dx=\frac{2\cdot2\pi a}{(2\pi a)^2+(2\pi\xi)^2}=\frac{a/\pi}{a^2+\xi^2}.$$
So $\mathcal F[e^{-2\pi a|x|}](\xi)=\dfrac{a}{\pi(a^2+\xi^2)}$. Both functions are even and nice (moderate decrease / $L^1$), so Fourier inversion applies: applying $\mathcal F$ again (which reflects, but these are even) gives
$$\mathcal F\Big[\frac{a}{\pi(a^2+x^2)}\Big](\xi)=e^{-2\pi a|\xi|}\quad\Longrightarrow\quad \mathcal F\Big[\frac1{a^2+x^2}\Big](\xi)=\frac\pi a\,e^{-2\pi a|\xi|}.\qquad\blacksquare$$
(The pair $(\text{Lorentzian},\text{exponential})$ is the Poisson-kernel pair on the line.)

content.statement: The derivative of a convolution falls on either factor.
content.formal: For $f,g\in\mathcal S(\mathbb R)$ show $\frac{d}{dx}(f*g)(x)=(f*g')(x)$.
content.intuition: Convolution is smoothing by sliding $g$; differentiating the output just differentiates the moving copy of $g$, justified by dominated convergence.

steps.content:
Write $(f*g)(x)=\int_{\mathbb R}f(y)g(x-y)\,dy$. The difference quotient is
$$\frac{(f*g)(x+h)-(f*g)(x)}h=\int_{\mathbb R}f(y)\,\frac{g(x+h-y)-g(x-y)}h\,dy.$$
As $h\to0$ the inner quotient $\to g'(x-y)$ pointwise, and by the mean value theorem it equals $g'(x+\theta h-y)$ for some $|\theta|\le1$; since $g'\in\mathcal S$ is bounded and $f\in\mathcal S\subset L^1$, the integrand is dominated by $|f(y)|\,\|g'\|_\infty\in L^1$. Dominated convergence lets us pass the limit inside:
$$\frac{d}{dx}(f*g)(x)=\int_{\mathbb R}f(y)g'(x-y)\,dy=(f*g')(x).\qquad\blacksquare$$
(By symmetry it also equals $(f'*g)(x)$ — derivatives may be placed on either factor.)

content.statement: Transforms of a parabolic bump, a Hermite-type Gaussian, and $x e^{-\pi a^2x^2}$.
content.formal: Find the Fourier transforms of (a) $f_1(x)=(1-x^2)\chi_{[-1,1]}(x)$; (b) $f_2(x)=(1-x^2)e^{-\pi x^2}$; (c) $f_3(x)=x\,e^{-\pi a^2x^2}$.
content.intuition: Multiplication by $x$ and $x^2$ becomes differentiation on the transform side; combined with the self-dual Gaussian and the box, every transform here is a short calculation.

steps.content:
\textbf{(a)} With $\omega=2\pi\xi$ and the function even, $\hat f_1(\xi)=2\int_0^1(1-x^2)\cos(\omega x)\,dx=\frac{4}{\omega^3}(\sin\omega-\omega\cos\omega)$, i.e.
$$\hat f_1(\xi)=\frac{\sin(2\pi\xi)-2\pi\xi\cos(2\pi\xi)}{2\pi^3\xi^3}.$$

\textbf{(b)} Use $\hat g=g$ for $g(x)=e^{-\pi x^2}$ and the rule $\mathcal F[x^2g]=-\frac1{4\pi^2}\hat g''$. Since $\hat g''=(4\pi^2\xi^2-2\pi)e^{-\pi\xi^2}$, $\mathcal F[x^2g]=(-\xi^2+\tfrac1{2\pi})e^{-\pi\xi^2}$, so
$$\hat f_2(\xi)=\hat g-\mathcal F[x^2g]=\Big(1-\tfrac1{2\pi}+\xi^2\Big)e^{-\pi\xi^2}.$$

\textbf{(c)} For $g_a(x)=e^{-\pi a^2x^2}$, scaling gives $\hat g_a(\xi)=a^{-1}e^{-\pi\xi^2/a^2}$. Using $\mathcal F[xg]=\frac{i}{2\pi}\hat g'$,
$$\hat f_3(\xi)=\frac{i}{2\pi}\frac{d}{d\xi}\big(a^{-1}e^{-\pi\xi^2/a^2}\big)=-\frac{i\,\xi}{a^3}\,e^{-\pi\xi^2/a^2}.$$
$\blacksquare$ (Even real inputs give even real transforms; the odd input $f_3$ gives an odd, purely imaginary transform.)

concepts.label: Øving 9 · P2 — L¹/$L^2$ membership and inverse transforms

content.statement: Classify sinc-type tails in $L^1/L^2$ and evaluate the inverse transform at $0,1$.
content.formal: For (i) $f=\frac{\sin2\pi x}{x}\chi_{[0,\infty)}$, (ii) $f=\frac1x\chi_{[1,\infty)}$, (iii) $f=\frac{\sin2\pi x}{x^2}\chi_{[1,\infty)}$: decide $L^1$/$L^2$ membership; describe $g=\mathcal F^{-1}[\hat f]$ and $g(0),g(1)$.
content.intuition: Membership is decided by tail decay ($1/x$ fails $L^1$ but passes $L^2$); inversion returns $f$ a.e., and at a jump the symmetric inverse lands on the midpoint.

steps.content:
\textbf{Membership.} (i) near $0$, $\frac{\sin2\pi x}{x}\to2\pi$ (bounded); at $\infty$, $|f|\sim\frac1x\notin L^1$ but $|f|^2\sim\frac1{x^2}\in L^1$: so $f\in L^2\setminus L^1$. (ii) $\int_1^\infty\frac1x=\infty$, $\int_1^\infty\frac1{x^2}<\infty$: $f\in L^2\setminus L^1$. (iii) $|f|\le\frac1{x^2}$ on $[1,\infty)$: $f\in L^1\cap L^2$.

\textbf{Inverse transform.} On $L^2$ the transform is invertible, so in every case $g=\mathcal F^{-1}\hat f=f$ as an $L^2$ element (a.e.). Pointwise (symmetric inversion of a piecewise-smooth $f$) the value is the average of one-sided limits, $g(x_0)=\tfrac12\big(f(x_0^+)+f(x_0^-)\big)$:
- (i) $f(0^-)=0,\ f(0^+)=2\pi\Rightarrow g(0)=\pi$; $f$ continuous at $1$ ($\sin2\pi=0$) $\Rightarrow g(1)=0$.
- (ii) $f$ is $0$ near $0$ $\Rightarrow g(0)=0$; jump at $1$: $f(1^-)=0,f(1^+)=1\Rightarrow g(1)=\tfrac12$.
- (iii) $f\in L^1\cap L^2$, continuous (value $0$ at $1$) $\Rightarrow$ inversion holds pointwise, $g(0)=0,\ g(1)=0$. $\blacksquare$

concepts.label: Convolution of Distributions

content.statement: Convolution can be extended to distributions when the support hypotheses make the pairing well-defined.
content.formal: For a distribution $T$ and test function $\varphi$, $(T*\varphi)(x)=T(\tau_x\tilde\varphi)$, where $\tilde\varphi(y)=\varphi(-y)$. More generally, $T*S$ is defined under suitable support conditions, and $\delta*T=T$.
content.intuition: Convolution with a test function smooths a distribution. This lets spikes and generalized sources interact with kernels in PDEs.

assumptions: Convolution of two arbitrary distributions is not always defined; support or regularity hypotheses are required.

concepts.label: Dirac Delta

content.statement: The unit point mass at the origin, acting by evaluation.
content.formal: The Dirac delta is the distribution $\delta(\varphi)=\varphi(0)$. In distributional notation, $\delta*T=T$ when convolution is defined, and $\hat\delta=1$ under the graph's Fourier convention.
content.intuition: The delta is not an ordinary function. It is the idealized point source and the limiting shape of approximate identities.

assumptions: Convolution and Fourier transform identities are interpreted distributionally.

concepts.label: Fourier Transform of Distributions

content.statement: The Fourier transform extends from functions to tempered distributions by duality.
content.formal: For $T\in\mathcal S'(\mathbb R)$, define $\hat T\in\mathcal S'(\mathbb R)$ by $\hat T(\varphi)=T(\hat\varphi)$ for every $\varphi\in\mathcal S(\mathbb R)$, using the graph's Fourier convention.
content.intuition: Instead of transforming $T$ directly, test it against transformed test functions. This makes objects like constants, deltas, and polynomials Fourier-transformable.

assumptions: Uses bilinear distribution pairing and the $e^{-2\pi i x\xi}$ convention.

concepts.label: Fundamental Solution

content.statement: A distribution $E$ with $LE=\delta$, used to solve linear equations by convolution.
content.formal: For a linear constant-coefficient differential operator $L$, a fundamental solution is a distribution $E$ satisfying $LE=\delta$. If convolution is defined, then $u=E*f$ solves $Lu=f$.
content.intuition: A fundamental solution is the response to a point source. Once the point-source response is known, general forcing is obtained by superposition through convolution.

assumptions: The formula $u=E*f$ requires convolution to be defined and $L$ to commute with convolution in the intended setting.

concepts.label: Tempered Distribution

content.statement: A continuous linear functional on Schwartz space.
content.definition: $$\mathcal S'(\mathbb R)=\mathcal L_{\mathrm{cont}}(\mathcal S(\mathbb R),\mathbb C).$$
content.formal: A tempered distribution is an element $T\in\mathcal S'(\mathbb R)$, the continuous dual of $\mathcal S(\mathbb R)$. A locally integrable function of at most polynomial growth defines one by $T_f(\varphi)=\int_{\mathbb R}f(x)\varphi(x)\,dx$.
content.intuition: Tempered distributions let Fourier analysis handle objects such as constants, deltas, and polynomials that are not ordinary integrable functions.

assumptions: Continuity is with respect to the Schwartz-space topology.

content.statement: A smooth compactly supported function used to probe distributions.
content.formal: A test function is $\varphi\in C_c^\infty(\mathbb R)$. The space of test functions is often denoted $\mathcal D(\mathbb R)$, and $C_c^\infty(\mathbb R)\subset\mathcal S(\mathbb R)$.
content.intuition: Test functions are controlled measuring instruments. A distribution is known by how it acts on every such probe.

content.statement: A distributional derivative is defined by transferring the derivative to the test function.
content.formal: For a distribution $T$, its derivative $T'$ is defined by $T'(\varphi)=-T(\varphi')$ for every test function $\varphi$. More generally, $T^{(k)}(\varphi)=(-1)^kT(\varphi^{(k)})$.
content.intuition: Integration by parts moves derivatives off rough objects and onto smooth test functions. This lets jumps and spikes have derivatives in a generalized sense.

concepts.label: Aliasing

content.statement: Undersampling makes different frequencies become indistinguishable.
content.formal: Sampling at rate $f_s$ identifies frequencies modulo $f_s$. Components outside the Nyquist band $[-f_s/2,f_s/2]$ fold back into that band.
content.intuition: Aliasing is not noise; it is ambiguity caused by too few samples. High frequencies impersonate lower ones because the sampled values match.

assumptions: The exact folding description uses ideal uniform sampling.

concepts.label: Characters of Finite Abelian Groups

content.statement: Characters form an orthonormal basis for functions on a finite abelian group.
content.formal: Let $G$ be a finite abelian group and use $\langle f,g\rangle=\frac1{|G|}\sum_{x\in G}f(x)\overline{g(x)}$. Then the characters $\chi\in\widehat G$ form an orthonormal basis of $L^2(G)$. Hence $\hat f(\chi)=\frac1{|G|}\sum_{x\in G}f(x)\overline{\chi(x)}$ and $f(x)=\sum_{\chi\in\widehat G}\hat f(\chi)\chi(x)$.
content.intuition: Every finite abelian group has enough pure frequencies to decompose every function on it. The cyclic DFT is just the special case $G=\mathbb Z/N\mathbb Z$.

assumptions: The group is finite and abelian. Nonabelian Fourier analysis requires representations, not just scalar characters.

source.citation: Stein–Shakarchi: Ch.7 Lemma 2.2; Ch.7 Lemma 2.4; Ch.7 Lemma 2.6; Ch.7 Theorem 2.3; Ch.7 Theorem 2.5; Ch.7 Theorem 2.7; Ch.7 Theorem 2.8
source.ref: Stein–Shakarchi: Ch.7 Lemma 2.2; Ch.7 Lemma 2.4; Ch.7 Lemma 2.6; Ch.7 Theorem 2.3; Ch.7 Theorem 2.5; Ch.7 Theorem 2.7; Ch.7 Theorem 2.8

concepts.label: Circular Convolution

content.statement: Convolution on $\mathbb Z/N\mathbb Z$, where indices wrap around modulo $N$.
content.formal: For $a,b:\mathbb Z/N\mathbb Z\to\mathbb C$, define $(a\circledast b)_k=\sum_{j=0}^{N-1}a_j b_{k-j\,\mathrm{mod}\,N}$. With the unnormalized DFT $\hat a(k)=\sum_j a_j\omega_N^{-jk}$, one has $\widehat{a\circledast b}(k)=\hat a(k)\hat b(k)$.
content.intuition: Circular convolution is sliding-and-averaging on a finite clock. The DFT turns this wrapped mixing operation into pointwise multiplication.

assumptions: The exact constants depend on the DFT normalization. Here the DFT is unnormalized and the inverse carries the factor $1/N$.

content.statement: A group generated by one element; the model group for length-$N$ finite Fourier analysis.
content.formal: $\mathbb Z/N\mathbb Z$ is the additive group of residue classes modulo $N$. Every element is generated by repeatedly adding $1$.
content.intuition: The cyclic group is a finite clock: after $N$ steps you return to the start. Its characters are the finite waves used by the DFT.

concepts.label: Discrete Cosine Transform

content.statement: A cosine-only transform obtained from an even extension of finite data.
content.formal: One common DCT-II convention is $C_k=\sum_{j=0}^{N-1}a_j\cos\!\left(\frac{\pi}{N}(j+\frac12)k\right)$, $k=0,\dots,N-1$. It can be viewed as a DFT of a symmetrically extended sequence.
content.intuition: Even symmetry removes sine components. The DCT often concentrates signal energy into few low-frequency coefficients, which is why it is useful in compression.

assumptions: Several DCT normalizations and types exist; this item states the common DCT-II convention.

concepts.label: Discrete Fourier Transform

content.statement: The Fourier transform of a length-$N$ vector.
content.formal: For $a=(a_0,\dots,a_{N-1})$, $\hat a(k)=\sum_{j=0}^{N-1}a_j\omega_N^{-jk}$, $k=0,\dots,N-1$, where $\omega_N=e^{2\pi i/N}$.
content.intuition: The DFT decomposes finite data into finite cyclic frequencies. It is exact linear algebra, not an approximation by itself.

assumptions: Normalization conventions vary; this graph puts the $1/N$ factor in the inverse transform.

concepts.label: Discrete-Time Fourier Transform

content.statement: The Fourier transform of an infinite sequence, producing a periodic frequency function.
content.definition: $$\widehat a(\xi)=\sum_{n\in\mathbb Z}a_ne^{-2\pi i n\xi},\qquad \xi\in\mathbb R/\mathbb Z.$$
content.formal: For $a\in\ell^1(\mathbb Z)$, $\hat a(\xi)=\sum_{n\in\mathbb Z}a_n e^{-2\pi i n\xi}$, and $\hat a$ is $1$-periodic in $\xi$.
content.intuition: Discrete time creates periodic frequency. This is the dual picture to Fourier series.

assumptions: Absolute summability gives a continuous periodic transform; weaker sequence spaces need weaker interpretations.

concepts.label: Dual Group

content.statement: The group of all characters of a finite abelian group.
content.formal: For a finite abelian group $G$, its dual group is $\widehat G=\operatorname{Hom}(G,\mathbb C^\times)$, with pointwise multiplication. The group $\widehat G$ is finite abelian and is isomorphic to $G$, though not canonically in general.
content.intuition: The dual group is the frequency side of $G$. Its elements are the waves that detect structure in functions on $G$.

assumptions: For finite groups, characters take values among roots of unity.

concepts.label: Fast Fourier Transform

content.statement: An $O(N\log N)$ algorithm for computing the DFT.
content.formal: For composite $N$, Cooley-Tukey factorizes a size-$N$ DFT into smaller DFTs. For $N=2^m$, this gives $O(N\log N)$ operations instead of the direct $O(N^2)$ computation.
content.intuition: The FFT exploits symmetry and repeated roots of unity, avoiding the waste of recomputing the same oscillatory patterns.

assumptions: The cleanest radix-2 statement assumes $N$ is a power of two; other factorizations handle other composite sizes.

content.statement: A finite group whose operation is commutative.
content.formal: A finite abelian group $G$ is a finite set with an associative operation, identity, inverses, and $xy=yx$ for all $x,y\in G$.
content.intuition: Finite abelian groups are the natural finite domains where Fourier analysis works cleanly: the frequencies are group characters.

concepts.label: Fourier Analysis on $\mathbb Z/N\mathbb Z$

content.statement: Fourier analysis on the cyclic group $\mathbb Z/N\mathbb Z$, where all sums are finite.
content.formal: For functions $f:\mathbb Z/N\mathbb Z\to\mathbb C$, the characters $\chi_j(k)=\omega_N^{jk}$, $j=0,\dots,N-1$, form an orthogonal basis of $L^2(\mathbb Z/N\mathbb Z)$ with normalized inner product $\langle f,g\rangle=\frac1N\sum_{k=0}^{N-1}f(k)\overline{g(k)}$.
content.intuition: This is Fourier series with no convergence trouble: finite data decomposes exactly into finite cyclic waves.

assumptions: The normalized inner product uses the factor $1/N$.

content.statement: A multiplicative homomorphism from a group into the unit circle.
content.formal: For a finite abelian group $G$, a character is a homomorphism $\chi:G\to \mathbb C^\times$. Since $G$ is finite, $\chi(x)$ is a root of unity and $|\chi(x)|=1$.
content.intuition: A character is a pure frequency on a group. It turns the group operation into multiplication of phases.

concepts.label: Nyquist–Shannon Sampling

content.statement: A band-limited function can be reconstructed from sufficiently dense samples.
content.formal: If $f\in L^2(\mathbb R)$ and $\operatorname{supp}\hat f\subset[-W,W]$, then $f$ is recovered from samples spaced $1/(2W)$ by $f(x)=\sum_{n\in\mathbb Z}f(\frac{n}{2W})\operatorname{sinc}(2Wx-n)$, with convergence in $L^2$ and, under stronger regularity, pointwise or uniform convergence. Here $\operatorname{sinc}(t)=\frac{\sin(\pi t)}{\pi t}$.
content.intuition: Band limitation means there are no hidden high-frequency wiggles between samples. Sampling above the Nyquist rate captures all degrees of freedom.

assumptions: Band-limited in the graph's Fourier-transform convention; convergence mode depends on regularity.

concepts.label: Parseval for the DFT

content.statement: Energy is conserved by the DFT up to the chosen normalization factor.
content.formal: With $\hat a(k)=\sum_{j=0}^{N-1}a_j\omega_N^{-jk}$, $\sum_{j=0}^{N-1}|a_j|^2=\frac1N\sum_{k=0}^{N-1}|\hat a(k)|^2$.
content.intuition: The DFT matrix is unitary after scaling by $1/\sqrt N$. This is finite-dimensional Plancherel.

assumptions: Depends on the DFT normalization convention.

content.statement: The $N$-th roots of unity are the finite phases used as discrete harmonics.
content.formal: $\omega_N=e^{2\pi i/N}$, and $\omega_N^k$, $k=0,\dots,N-1$, are exactly the solutions of $z^N=1$. Characters of $\mathbb Z/N\mathbb Z$ have the form $k\mapsto\omega_N^{jk}$.
content.intuition: These are the equally spaced points on the unit circle. They are the finite analogue of the continuous waves $e^{inx}$.

concepts.label: Sinc Function

content.statement: The interpolation kernel in the sampling theorem.
content.formal: $\operatorname{sinc}(t)=\frac{\sin(\pi t)}{\pi t}$ for $t\ne0$, with $\operatorname{sinc}(0)=1$.
content.intuition: Sinc is the ideal reconstruction pulse: it equals one at its own sample point and zero at the other integer sample points.

assumptions: This graph uses the normalized sinc convention $\operatorname{sinc}(t)=\sin(\pi t)/(\pi t)$.

concepts.label: SS 7.3.1 — Discrete Fourier coefficients approximate the true ones

content.statement: Discrete Fourier coefficients are $N$-periodic and converge to the true coefficients.
content.formal: Let $f$ be a function on the circle. For each integer $N\ge1$, define the discrete Fourier coefficients $$a_N(n)=\frac1N\sum_{k=1}^{N}f(e^{2\pi i k/N})e^{-2\pi i kn/N},\qquad n\in\mathbb Z.$$ Also define the ordinary Fourier coefficients by $$a(n)=\int_0^1 f(e^{2\pi ix})e^{-2\pi inx}\,dx.$$ (a) Show that $a_N(n)=a_N(n+N)$ for every $n\in\mathbb Z$. (b) Prove that if $f$ is continuous on the circle, then $$a_N(n)\to a(n)\qquad(N\to\infty)$$ for each fixed $n$.
content.intuition: Sampling on $N$ equally spaced points turns the Fourier integral into a Riemann sum; continuity makes the sum converge, while the sampling forces $N$-periodicity (aliasing).

steps.content:
Replacing $n$ by $n+N$ multiplies the $k$-th term by $e^{-2\pi ikN/N}=e^{-2\pi ik}=1$, so
$$a_N(n+N)=\frac1N\sum_{k=1}^N f(e^{2\pi ik/N})e^{-2\pi ik(n+N)/N}=a_N(n).$$
The discrete spectrum is $N$-periodic (aliasing): only $N$ distinct coefficients exist.

Fix $n$. The quantity $a_N(n)=\frac1N\sum_{k=1}^N\Phi(k/N)$ is precisely the right-endpoint Riemann sum, over the uniform partition of $[0,1]$ into $N$ pieces, of $\Phi(x)=f(e^{2\pi ix})e^{-2\pi inx}$. As $f$ is continuous, $\Phi$ is continuous on the compact circle, hence (uniformly continuous) its Riemann sums converge:
$$a_N(n)\to\int_0^1 f(e^{2\pi ix})e^{-2\pi inx}\,dx=a(n).$$

concepts.label: SS 7.3.2 — C¹ decay of discrete coefficients

content.statement: $C^1$ functions have $O(1/|n|)$ discrete Fourier coefficients below Nyquist.
content.formal: Let $f$ be a $C^1$ function on the circle. Prove that there is a constant $c$, independent of $N$ and $n$, such that $$|a_N(n)|\le \frac{c}{|n|}\qquad\text{whenever }0<|n|\le N/2.$$ Hint: use the shifted-difference identity $$a_N(n)\bigl(1-e^{2\pi i\ell n/N}\bigr)=\frac1N\sum_{k=1}^{N}\bigl[f(e^{2\pi ik/N})-f(e^{2\pi i(k+\ell)/N})\bigr]e^{-2\pi ikn/N},$$ and choose $\ell$ so that $\ell n/N$ is close to $1/2$.
content.intuition: The same shift-and-difference trick as for ordinary coefficients: one derivative buys one power of $1/|n|$, valid up to the Nyquist frequency $N/2$.

steps.content:
Shift the summation index by $\ell$: since the sum is over a full period mod $N$,
$$a_N(n)\big[1-e^{2\pi i\ell n/N}\big]=\frac1N\sum_{k=1}^N\big[f(e^{2\pi ik/N})-f(e^{2\pi i(k+\ell)/N})\big]e^{-2\pi ikn/N}.$$
For $0<|n|\le N/2$ choose the integer $\ell\approx N/(2n)$ so that $\ell n/N$ is as close to $\tfrac12$ as possible; then $|1-e^{2\pi i\ell n/N}|\ge c_0>0$ (bounded away from $0$). Because $f\in C^1$, $|f(e^{2\pi ik/N})-f(e^{2\pi i(k+\ell)/N})|\le \|f'\|_\infty\cdot\frac{2\pi\ell}{N}\le C\,\frac{\ell}{N}\le \frac{C'}{|n|}$. Averaging,
$$|a_N(n)|\,c_0\le\frac{C'}{|n|}\ \Longrightarrow\ |a_N(n)|\le\frac{c}{|n|}.$$

concepts.label: SS 7.3.3 — C² decay and the discrete inversion formula

content.statement: $C^2$ decay yields the continuous Fourier inversion as the limit of the discrete one.
