content.formal: Using the same finite-difference method, prove that if $f$ is $C^2$ on the circle, then there is a constant $c$, independent of $N$ and $n$, such that $$|a_N(n)|\le \frac{c}{|n|^2}\qquad\text{whenever }0<|n|\le N/2.$$ Then use this estimate to derive the continuous inversion formula $$f(e^{2\pi ix})=\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty}a(n)e^{2\pi inx}$$ from the finite Fourier inversion formula. Hint: for the decay estimate, use the second symmetric difference $$f(e^{2\pi i(k+\ell)/N})+f(e^{2\pi i(k-\ell)/N})-2f(e^{2\pi ik/N}).$$ For the inversion step, when $N$ is odd, write the finite inversion formula as $$f(e^{2\pi ik/N})=\sum_{|n|<N/2}a_N(n)e^{2\pi ikn/N}.$$
content.intuition: Two derivatives give summable $1/|n|^2$ decay, which makes the finite DFT inversion pass to the limit — the continuous Fourier series emerges from its sampled version.

steps.content:
Use the second symmetric difference: averaging the $+\ell$ and $-\ell$ shifts,
$$a_N(n)\big[2-2\cos(2\pi\ell n/N)\big]=\frac1N\sum_{k=1}^N\big[2f(e^{2\pi ik/N})-f(e^{2\pi i(k+\ell)/N})-f(e^{2\pi i(k-\ell)/N})\big]e^{-2\pi ikn/N}.$$
Choosing $\ell$ with $\ell n/N\approx\tfrac12$ makes $2-2\cos(2\pi\ell n/N)\ge c_0>0$. Since $f\in C^2$, the second difference is $O\big((\ell/N)^2\big)=O(1/|n|^2)$, so $|a_N(n)|\le c/|n|^2$ for $0<|n|\le N/2$.

The finite Fourier inversion on $\mathbb Z(N)$ (take $N$ odd) reads
$$f(e^{2\pi ik/N})=\sum_{|n|<N/2}a_N(n)\,e^{2\pi ikn/N}.$$
By Exercise 1, $a_N(n)\to a(n)$; by the bound $|a_N(n)|\le c/|n|^2$ (uniform in $N$) the tails are uniformly small, so letting $N\to\infty$ (dominated convergence on the summation) yields
$$f(e^{2\pi ix})=\sum_{n=-\infty}^\infty a(n)\,e^{2\pi inx},$$
the classical Fourier inversion recovered as a limit of the discrete one.

concepts.label: Central Limit Theorem (Fourier proof)

content.statement: Fourier methods prove that normalized sums of independent variables converge in distribution to a Gaussian.
content.formal: For i.i.d. random variables with mean $\mu$ and variance $\sigma^2>0$, the characteristic functions of $(X_1+\cdots+X_n-n\mu)/(\sigma\sqrt n)$ converge pointwise to $e^{-t^2/2}$, the characteristic function of the standard normal law.
content.intuition: Independence turns sums into products of characteristic functions. After normalization, those products converge to the Gaussian multiplier.

assumptions: Uses the probability convention $\varphi_X(t)=\mathbb E e^{itX}$, not the graph's $2\pi$-Fourier transform convention.

concepts.label: Dirichlet Problem on the Disk

content.statement: The Poisson kernel gives the harmonic extension of boundary data on the disk.
content.formal: If $f\in C(\mathbb T)$, then $u(r,\theta)=(P_r*f)(\theta)$ is harmonic for $0\le r<1$ and $u(r,\theta)\to f(\theta)$ as $r\to1^-$.
content.intuition: Boundary values are averaged with a kernel that concentrates near the boundary point as one approaches the circle.

assumptions: Boundary convergence is stated for continuous boundary data; weaker data require weaker convergence modes.

concepts.label: Fraunhofer Diffraction

content.statement: In the Fraunhofer far-field approximation, the diffraction amplitude is essentially the Fourier transform of the aperture.
content.formal: For an aperture indicator $\mathbf 1_A$ in the aperture plane, the far-field amplitude is proportional to $\widehat{\mathbf 1_A}(\xi)$, and the observed intensity is proportional to $|\widehat{\mathbf 1_A}(\xi)|^2$.
content.intuition: A lens or far-field propagation physically separates spatial frequencies. A slit produces a sinc pattern; a circular aperture produces an Airy-type pattern.

assumptions: This is the scalar Fraunhofer approximation, not a full electromagnetic model.

concepts.label: Free Schrödinger Equation

content.statement: The free Schrödinger equation is solved by multiplying each Fourier mode by a dispersive phase.
content.formal: For $iu_t=-u_{xx}$ on $\mathbb R$ with initial data $u_0$, the Fourier transform satisfies $\hat u(\xi,t)=e^{-4\pi^2 i\xi^2t}\hat u_0(\xi)$.
content.intuition: The Fourier transform turns the spatial second derivative into multiplication by $-4\pi^2\xi^2$. Unlike heat flow, the multiplier has modulus one, so waves disperse rather than decay.

assumptions: Uses the Fourier convention $e^{-2\pi ix\xi}$. Regularity assumptions depend on whether one wants classical, $L^2$, or distributional solutions.

concepts.label: Heat Equation

content.statement: Diffusion is solved by damping each Fourier mode.
content.formal: On the circle, $u_t=u_{xx}$, $u(x,0)=f(x)$, has formal solution $u(x,t)=\sum_{n\in\mathbb Z}\hat f(n)e^{-n^2t}e^{inx}$. On $\mathbb R$, $u(x,t)=(H_t*f)(x)$.
content.intuition: High frequencies decay faster than low frequencies, so heat flow smooths rough data as time increases.

assumptions: Constants depend on whether the circle basis is $e^{inx}$ or the real-line transform convention is used.

source.citation: Stein–Shakarchi: Ch.5 Corollary 2.2; Ch.5 Theorem 2.1; Ch.5 Theorem 2.3
source.ref: Stein–Shakarchi: Ch.5 Corollary 2.2; Ch.5 Theorem 2.1; Ch.5 Theorem 2.3

concepts.label: Isoperimetric Inequality

content.statement: A simple closed plane curve of fixed length encloses maximal area exactly when it is a circle.
content.formal: If a sufficiently smooth simple closed plane curve has length $L$ and encloses area $A$, then $A\le L^2/(4\pi)$, with equality exactly for circles.
content.intuition: Writing the boundary curve as a Fourier series turns area and length into coefficient sums. Parseval then becomes a geometric inequality.

assumptions: Assume a regular simple closed curve, for example piecewise $C^1$, oriented positively.

concepts.label: Laplacian

content.statement: The second-order spatial differential operator diagonalized by the Fourier transform.
content.formal: On $\mathbb R^d$, $\Delta f=\sum_{j=1}^{d}\partial_{x_j}^2f$. With the graph's Fourier convention, $\widehat{\Delta f}(\xi)=-4\pi^2|\xi|^2\hat f(\xi)$ for Schwartz functions.
content.intuition: The Laplacian measures curvature or diffusion pressure. Fourier transform turns it into multiplication by $-4\pi^2|\xi|^2$, which is why heat, wave, and Schrödinger equations reduce to scalar frequency equations.

assumptions: The multiplier identity is stated first for Schwartz functions; weaker solution theories use density or distributions.

source.ref: Stein–Shakarchi: Ch.5 and Ch.6 Fourier transform applications to PDE

concepts.label: Radon Transform

content.statement: The Radon transform reconstructs a function from its integrals over hyperplanes.
content.formal: For suitable $f$ on $\mathbb R^d$, $Rf(t,\gamma)=\int_{x\cdot\gamma=t}f(x)\,d\sigma(x)$. The Fourier slice theorem says the one-dimensional Fourier transform in $t$ satisfies $\mathcal F_t(Rf)(s,\gamma)=\hat f(s\gamma)$.
content.intuition: Each X-ray projection reveals one slice of the Fourier transform. Combining slices over many directions reconstructs the object.

assumptions: Requires suitable decay or integrability so the hyperplane integrals and transforms are defined.

source.citation: Stein–Shakarchi: Ch.6 Corollary 5.3; Ch.6 Lemma 5.2; Ch.6 Proposition 5.1; Ch.6 Theorem 5.4
source.ref: Stein–Shakarchi: Ch.6 Corollary 5.3; Ch.6 Lemma 5.2; Ch.6 Proposition 5.1; Ch.6 Theorem 5.4

concepts.label: Separation of Variables

content.statement: A method for solving linear PDEs by expanding in spatial eigenfunctions.
content.formal: Seek $u(x,t)=\sum_n c_n(t)e_n(x)$, where $e_n$ are eigenfunctions of the spatial operator. Orthogonality decouples the PDE into ODEs for the coefficients $c_n(t)$.
content.intuition: The method chooses coordinates that match the operator. In those coordinates, the PDE splits into independent one-frequency motions.

assumptions: Requires a suitable eigenfunction expansion for the spatial problem.

concepts.label: Signal Filtering

content.statement: Frequency-selective processing by convolution in time or space.
content.formal: For a linear time-invariant filter with impulse response $h$, $y=h*x$, so $\hat y=\hat h\,\hat x$ whenever the transforms are defined. The multiplier $\hat h$ is the transfer function.
content.intuition: Filtering is easier in frequency: convolutional systems become pointwise multiplication, so passing or rejecting frequency bands becomes a multiplier design problem.

assumptions: Precise hypotheses depend on whether signals are modeled in $L^1$, $L^2$, distributions, or discrete spaces.

concepts.label: SS 4.5.10 — Averaging along an equidistributed sequence

content.statement: Averages of a mean-zero periodic function along an equidistributed sequence vanish.
content.formal:
Suppose that $f$ is a periodic function on $\mathbb R$ of period $1$, and $\{\xi_n\}$ is a sequence which is equidistributed in $[0,1)$. Prove the following.

	extbf{(a)} If $f$ is continuous and satisfies
$$\int_0^1 f(x)\,dx=0,$$
then
$$\lim_{N\to\infty}\frac1N\sum_{n=1}^{N}f(x+\xi_n)=0$$
uniformly in $x$.

Hint: Establish this result first for trigonometric polynomials.

	extbf{(b)} If $f$ is merely integrable on $[0,1]$ and satisfies
$$\int_0^1 f(x)\,dx=0,$$
then
$$\lim_{N\to\infty}\int_0^1\left|\frac1N\sum_{n=1}^{N}f(x+\xi_n)\right|^2 dx=0.$$
content.intuition: Equidistribution makes a moving average against a mean-zero profile cancel — uniformly when $f$ is smooth (Fejér), and in mean-square when $f$ is rough (Parseval + dominated convergence).

steps.content:
First take a trigonometric polynomial $f(y)=\sum_{0<|k|\le M}c_k e^{2\pi iky}$ (no constant term, as $\int f=0$). Then
$$\frac1N\sum_{n=1}^N f(x+\xi_n)=\sum_{0<|k|\le M}c_k e^{2\pi ikx}\Big(\frac1N\sum_{n=1}^N e^{2\pi ik\xi_n}\Big).$$
Each inner Weyl sum $\to0$ (Exercise 7); the outer sum is finite with $|e^{2\pi ikx}|=1$, so the whole expression $\to0$ \emph{uniformly in $x$}. For general continuous $f$, approximate uniformly by such a polynomial $T$ with $\int T=0$ and $\|f-T\|_\infty<\varepsilon$ (Fejér); then $|\frac1N\sum f(x+\xi_n)|\le\|f-T\|_\infty+|\frac1N\sum T(x+\xi_n)|<2\varepsilon$ for large $N$, uniformly in $x$.

Let $g_N(x)=\frac1N\sum_{n=1}^N f(x+\xi_n)$. Its $k$-th Fourier coefficient is $\widehat{g_N}(k)=\hat f(k)\cdot\frac1N\sum_{n=1}^N e^{2\pi ik\xi_n}$. By Parseval,
$$\int_0^1|g_N|^2=\sum_{k\in\mathbb Z}|\hat f(k)|^2\Big|\frac1N\sum_{n=1}^N e^{2\pi ik\xi_n}\Big|^2.$$
The $k=0$ term vanishes ($\hat f(0)=\int f=0$). For $k\neq0$ the bracket $\to0$ (Exercise 7) and is $\le1$; since $\sum_k|\hat f(k)|^2<\infty$ (Bessel), dominated convergence gives $\int_0^1|g_N|^2\to0$.

concepts.label: SS 4.5.11 — Heat flow converges to its data in $L^2$

content.statement: Heat-kernel smoothing converges to the initial data in $L^2$.
content.formal:
Show that if $u(x,t)=(f*H_t)(x)$, where $H_t$ is the heat kernel and $f$ is Riemann integrable, then
$$\int_0^1 |u(x,t)-f(x)|^2\,dx\to0\qquad\text{as }t\to0.$$
content.intuition: On the Fourier side heat flow is the multiplier $e^{-4\pi^2k^2t}\to1$; Parseval turns convergence of the data into termwise convergence dominated by Bessel's bound.

steps.content:
On the circle $H_t(x)=\sum_{k}e^{-4\pi^2k^2t}e^{2\pi ikx}$, so convolution multiplies coefficients: $\widehat{u(\cdot,t)}(k)=\hat f(k)\,e^{-4\pi^2k^2t}$. By Parseval,
$$\int_0^1|u(x,t)-f(x)|^2\,dx=\sum_{k\in\mathbb Z}|\hat f(k)|^2\,\big|e^{-4\pi^2k^2t}-1\big|^2.$$
For each fixed $k$, $e^{-4\pi^2k^2t}-1\to0$ as $t\to0^+$. Moreover $0<e^{-4\pi^2k^2t}\le1$ gives $|e^{-4\pi^2k^2t}-1|^2\le1$, so the summand is dominated by $|\hat f(k)|^2$, which is summable (Bessel, since $f$ is Riemann integrable). By dominated convergence the series $\to0$, i.e.
$$\int_0^1|u(x,t)-f(x)|^2\,dx\to0\quad(t\to0^+).$$
So the heat semigroup recovers the initial data in the mean-square sense for any integrable $f$ — even before pointwise statements (which need $f$ continuous and the good-kernel property).

concepts.label: SS 4.5.5 — Powers of the golden ratio aren't equidistributed

content.statement: The golden-ratio powers cluster at $0$ and $1$, so they are not equidistributed.
content.formal:
Prove that the sequence $\{\gamma_n\}_{n=1}^{\infty}$, where $\gamma_n$ is the fractional part of
$$\left( rac{1+\sqrt5}{2}\right)^n,$$
is not equidistributed in $[0,1]$.

Hint: Show that
$$U_n=\left( rac{1+\sqrt5}{2}\right)^n+\left( rac{1-\sqrt5}{2}\right)^n$$
is the solution of the difference equation $U_{r+1}=U_r+U_{r-1}$ with $U_0=2$ and $U_1=1$. The $U_n$ satisfy the same difference equation as the Fibonacci numbers.
content.intuition: Algebraic integers betray themselves: $\varphi^n$ is an integer minus a vanishing term, so its fractional parts pile up at the ends rather than spreading out.

steps.content:
Let $\varphi=\frac{1+\sqrt5}2$ and $\psi=\frac{1-\sqrt5}2$, the roots of $x^2=x+1$. Then $U_n=\varphi^n+\psi^n$ satisfies $U_{r+1}=U_r+U_{r-1}$ with $U_0=2,\ U_1=1$, so every $U_n$ is an \emph{integer} (the Lucas numbers). Since $|\psi|=\frac{\sqrt5-1}2\approx0.618<1$, we have $\psi^n\to0$, and
$$\varphi^n=U_n-\psi^n\quad\Longrightarrow\quad \langle\varphi^n\rangle=\langle-\psi^n\rangle\pmod1.$$
Now $\psi<0$, so $\psi^n>0$ for even $n$ and $<0$ for odd $n$, both $\to0$:
$$n\ \text{even}:\ \langle\varphi^n\rangle=1-\psi^n\to1^-,\qquad n\ \text{odd}:\ \langle\varphi^n\rangle=-\psi^n=|\psi|^n\to0^+.$$
Thus the sequence accumulates only at the endpoints $0$ and $1$ and never enters, say, $[\tfrac14,\tfrac34]$ for large $n$. Hence it is far from equidistributed (the limiting proportion in $[\tfrac14,\tfrac34]$ is $0\neq\tfrac12$).

content.statement: Equidistribution implies the vanishing of all nonzero Weyl exponential sums.
content.formal:
Prove the second part of Weyl's criterion: if a sequence of numbers $\xi_1,\xi_2,\dots$ in $[0,1)$ is equidistributed, then for every $k\in\mathbb Z\setminus\{0\}$,
$$\frac1N\sum_{n=1}^{N} e^{2\pi i k\xi_n}\to0\qquad\text{as }N\to\infty.$$

Hint: It suffices to show that
$$\frac1N\sum_{n=1}^{N}f(\xi_n)\to\int_0^1 f(x)\,dx$$
for all continuous $f$. Prove this first when $f$ is the characteristic function of an interval.
content.intuition: Equidistribution is exactly 'sample-average = integral' for indicators; squeezing continuous functions between step functions promotes it to all continuous test functions, the exponentials included.

steps.content:
Equidistribution means, for every subinterval $[a,b)\subset[0,1)$,
$$\frac1N\sum_{n=1}^N\chi_{[a,b)}(\xi_n)\to b-a=\int_0^1\chi_{[a,b)}.$$
By linearity this extends to all step functions. Given a continuous $1$-periodic $f$ and $\varepsilon>0$, choose step functions $s_-\le f\le s_+$ with $\int_0^1(s_+-s_-)<\varepsilon$ (uniform continuity). Then
$$\int_0^1 f-\varepsilon\le\liminf_N\frac1N\sum f(\xi_n)\le\limsup_N\frac1N\sum f(\xi_n)\le\int_0^1 f+\varepsilon,$$
so $\frac1N\sum_{n\le N}f(\xi_n)\to\int_0^1 f$ for every continuous $f$. Applying this to the continuous function $f(x)=e^{2\pi ikx}$, whose integral $\int_0^1 e^{2\pi ikx}dx=0$ for $k\neq0$, gives
$$\frac1N\sum_{n=1}^N e^{2\pi ik\xi_n}\to0.$$

content.statement: Fractional parts of $an^\sigma$ ($0<\sigma<1$) are equidistributed.
content.formal:
Show that for any $a\ne0$ and any $\sigma$ with $0<\sigma<1$, the sequence $\langle an^\sigma
angle$ is equidistributed in $[0,1)$.

Hint: Prove that
$$\sum_{n=1}^{N}e^{2\pi i b n^\sigma}=O(N^\sigma)+O(N^{1-\sigma})\qquad(b\ne0).$$
In fact, note the following estimate:
$$\sum_{n=1}^{N} e^{2\pi i b n^\sigma}-\int_1^N e^{2\pi i b x^\sigma}\,dx
=O\left(\sum_{n=1}^{N} n^{-1+\sigma}\right).$$
content.intuition: Sub-linear, slowly turning phases dephase: the Weyl sum is dwarfed by $N$ because neither the discretisation error ($N^\sigma$) nor the oscillatory integral ($N^{1-\sigma}$) keeps up.

steps.content: By Weyl's criterion it suffices to show $\frac1N\sum_{n=1}^N e^{2\pi i b n^\sigma}\to0$ for every $b\neq0$ (with $b=ka$, $k\neq0$). Compare the sum with the integral $I_N=\int_1^N e^{2\pi i b x^\sigma}dx$.

Writing $\phi(x)=e^{2\pi ibx^\sigma}$, $|\phi'(x)|=2\pi|b|\sigma x^{\sigma-1}$, so
$$\Big|\sum_{n=1}^N\phi(n)-\int_1^N\phi(x)\,dx\Big|\le\sum_{n=1}^N\sup_{[n,n+1]}|\phi'|=O\Big(\sum_{n=1}^N n^{\sigma-1}\Big)=O(N^\sigma).$$

The phase derivative $2\pi b\sigma x^{\sigma-1}$ is monotone, so by van der Corput's lemma (integration by parts),
$$|I_N|\le\frac{C}{\min_x|2\pi b\sigma x^{\sigma-1}|}=\frac{C}{2\pi|b|\sigma\,N^{\sigma-1}}=O(N^{1-\sigma}).$$
Hence $\sum_{n=1}^N e^{2\pi ib n^\sigma}=O(N^\sigma)+O(N^{1-\sigma})$. Both exponents are $<1$, so dividing by $N$ gives $\to0$. By Weyl, $\langle an^\sigma\rangle$ is equidistributed.

concepts.label: SS 4.5.9 — ⟨a log n⟩ is never equidistributed

content.statement: Fractional parts of $a\log n$ fail to equidistribute for every $a$.
content.formal:
In contrast with the result in Exercise 8, prove that $\langle a\log n
angle$ is not equidistributed for any $a$.

Hint: Compare the sum
$$\sum_{n=1}^{N}e^{2\pi i b\log n}$$
with the corresponding integral.
content.intuition: A logarithmic phase turns too slowly: the Weyl sum keeps a fixed-size rotating tail instead of averaging away, so the orbit is biased.

steps.content:
Test Weyl's criterion with $k=1$, so we examine $\frac1N\sum_{n=1}^N e^{2\pi i a\log n}=\frac1N\sum_{n=1}^N n^{2\pi i a}$. Compare with the integral $\int_1^N x^{2\pi ia}\,dx$; the difference is $O(\sup|\frac{d}{dx}x^{2\pi ia}|\cdot N)=O(1)$ relative error after dividing by $N$ — more precisely Euler–Maclaurin gives
$$\sum_{n=1}^N n^{2\pi ia}=\int_1^N x^{2\pi ia}\,dx+O(1)=\frac{N^{1+2\pi ia}-1}{1+2\pi ia}+O(1).$$
Therefore
$$\frac1N\sum_{n=1}^N n^{2\pi ia}=\frac{N^{2\pi ia}}{1+2\pi ia}+o(1).$$
The factor $N^{2\pi ia}=e^{2\pi ia\log N}$ has modulus $1$ and rotates around the circle as $N\to\infty$, so the right-hand side has modulus $\to\frac1{|1+2\pi ia|}\neq0$ and does \emph{not} converge to $0$. Weyl's criterion fails, so $\langle a\log n\rangle$ is not equidistributed.

concepts.label: SS 5.6.1 — A logarithmic variant of the heat equation

content.statement: Solve an Euler-type heat equation by reduction to the standard heat equation.
content.formal: The equation $$x^2\frac{\partial^2u}{\partial x^2}+ax\frac{\partial u}{\partial x}=\frac{\partial u}{\partial t},\qquad u(x,0)=f(x),\qquad 0<x<\infty,\ t>0,$$ is a variant of the heat equation. To solve it, make the change of variables $x=e^{-y}$, so $-\infty<y<\infty$, and set $$U(y,t)=u(e^{-y},t),\qquad F(y)=f(e^{-y}).$$ Show that the problem reduces to $$\frac{\partial^2U}{\partial y^2}+(1-a)\frac{\partial U}{\partial y}=\frac{\partial U}{\partial t},\qquad U(y,0)=F(y).$$ Solve this equation by taking the Fourier transform in the $y$ variable, computing $$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}e^{(-4\pi^2\xi^2+(1-a)2\pi i\xi)t}e^{2\pi i\xi v}\,d\xi,$$ and show that the solution of the original problem is $$u(x,t)=\frac1{(4\pi t)^{1/2}}\int_0^\infty e^{-(\log(v/x)+(1-a)t)^2/(4t)}f(v)\,\frac{dv}{v}.$$
content.intuition: A logarithmic change of variable turns the Euler-type operator $x^2\partial_x^2+ax\partial_x$ into the constant-coefficient heat operator, so the solution is Gaussian smoothing in $\log x$.

steps.content:
Put $x=e^{-y}$ ($-\infty<y<\infty$), $U(y,t)=u(e^{-y},t)$, $F(y)=f(e^{-y})$. Since $x\partial_x=-\partial_y$ and $x^2\partial_x^2=\partial_y^2+\partial_y$, the equation becomes the constant-coefficient
$$U_{yy}+(1-a)U_y=U_t,\qquad U(y,0)=F(y).$$

With $\hat U(\xi,t)=\int U(y,t)e^{-2\pi iy\xi}dy$, the symbols give $\hat U_t=\big(-4\pi^2\xi^2+(1-a)2\pi i\xi\big)\hat U$, so
$$\hat U(\xi,t)=\hat F(\xi)\,e^{(-4\pi^2\xi^2+(1-a)2\pi i\xi)t}.$$
The multiplier is the transform of a shifted Gaussian: $\int_{\mathbb R}e^{(-4\pi^2\xi^2+(1-a)2\pi i\xi)t}e^{2\pi i\xi v}d\xi=\frac1{\sqrt{4\pi t}}\,e^{-(v+(1-a)t)^2/(4t)}$ (complete the square). Hence $U=F*G_t$ with $G_t(v)=\frac1{\sqrt{4\pi t}}e^{-(v+(1-a)t)^2/(4t)}$:
$$U(y,t)=\frac1{\sqrt{4\pi t}}\int_{\mathbb R}e^{-(y'+(1-a)t)^2/(4t)}F(y-y')\,dy'.$$

$y=-\log x$, $F(y-y')=f(e^{-(y-y')})=f(x e^{y'})$, and set $v=xe^{y'}$ so $y'=\log(v/x)$, $dy'=dv/v$:
$$u(x,t)=\frac1{(4\pi t)^{1/2}}\int_0^\infty e^{-(\log(v/x)+(1-a)t)^2/(4t)}f(v)\,\frac{dv}{v}.$$

content.statement: Reduce Black–Scholes to the heat equation and read off the option price.
content.formal: The Black--Scholes equation from finance theory is $$\frac{\partial V}{\partial t}+rs\frac{\partial V}{\partial s}+\frac{\sigma^2s^2}{2}\frac{\partial^2V}{\partial s^2}-rV=0,\qquad 0<t<T,$$ with final boundary condition $V(s,T)=F(s)$. An appropriate change of variables reduces this to Problem 1. Alternatively, use the substitution $$V(s,t)=e^{ax+b\tau}U(x,\tau),\qquad x=\log s,\qquad \tau=\frac{\sigma^2}{2}(T-t),$$ with $$a=\frac12-\frac{r}{\sigma^2},\qquad b=-\left(\frac12+\frac{r}{\sigma^2}\right)^2,$$ to reduce the equation to the one-dimensional heat equation with initial condition $$U(x,0)=e^{-ax}F(e^x).$$ Conclude that a solution of the Black--Scholes equation is $$V(s,t)=\frac{e^{-r(T-t)}}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2(T-t)}}\int_0^\infty \exp\left(-\frac{(\log(s/s^*)+(r-\sigma^2/2)(T-t))^2}{2\sigma^2(T-t)}\right)F(s^*)\,ds^*.$$
content.intuition: Option pricing is heat diffusion in disguise: log-price plus a discounting/drift gauge transform turns Black–Scholes into $U_\tau=U_{xx}$, whose Gaussian kernel is the log-normal risk-neutral density.

steps.content:
Let $x=\log s$ and $\tau=\frac{\sigma^2}2(T-t)$ (so $\tau$ runs forward from $0$ at $t=T$). In the $x$ variable, $s\partial_s=\partial_x$ and $s^2\partial_s^2=\partial_x^2-\partial_x$; the final condition becomes an initial condition at $\tau=0$. Substituting $V=e^{ax+b\tau}U(x,\tau)$ with
$$a=\tfrac12-\tfrac r{\sigma^2},\qquad b=-\Big(\tfrac12+\tfrac r{\sigma^2}\Big)^2,$$
the first-order ($V_s$) and zeroth-order ($-rV$) terms are absorbed, leaving the pure heat equation
$$U_\tau=U_{xx},\qquad U(x,0)=e^{-ax}F(e^x).$$

The heat solution is $U(x,\tau)=\frac1{\sqrt{4\pi\tau}}\int_{\mathbb R}e^{-(x-x')^2/(4\tau)}e^{-ax'}F(e^{x'})\,dx'$. Undoing the substitutions ($x=\log s$, $4\tau=2\sigma^2(T-t)$, $s^*=e^{x'}$, $dx'=ds^*/s^*$) and simplifying the exponential prefactors gives
$$V(s,t)=\frac{e^{-r(T-t)}}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2(T-t)}}\int_0^\infty e^{-\big(\log(s/s^*)+(r-\sigma^2/2)(T-t)\big)^2/\big(2\sigma^2(T-t)\big)}\,F(s^*)\,ds^*.$$
The kernel is log-normal: the discounted ($e^{-r(T-t)}$) expectation of the payoff $F$ under the risk-neutral price distribution.

content.statement: Radial initial data produce radial (spherical) solutions of the wave equation, and conversely.
content.formal: A spherical wave is a solution $u(x,t)$ of the Cauchy problem for the wave equation on $\mathbb R^d$ such that, for each fixed $t$, the function $x\mapsto u(x,t)$ is radial. Prove that $u$ is a spherical wave if and only if the initial data $f,g\in\mathcal S(\mathbb R^d)$ are both radial.
content.intuition: Rotational symmetry passes through the Fourier transform and the radial wave multipliers, so the wave is round exactly when its launch data are round.

steps.content:
The Fourier solution of $u_{tt}=\Delta u$, $u(\cdot,0)=f$, $u_t(\cdot,0)=g$ is
$$\hat u(\xi,t)=\cos(2\pi|\xi|t)\,\hat f(\xi)+\frac{\sin(2\pi|\xi|t)}{2\pi|\xi|}\,\hat g(\xi).$$
A function is radial iff its Fourier transform is radial (rotations commute with $\mathcal F$). The multipliers $\cos(2\pi|\xi|t)$ and $\frac{\sin(2\pi|\xi|t)}{2\pi|\xi|}$ are radial in $\xi$.
If $f,g$ are radial, then $\hat f,\hat g$ are radial, so $\hat u(\cdot,t)$ is radial for each $t$, hence $u(\cdot,t)$ is radial: $u$ is a spherical wave.

If $u(\cdot,t)$ is radial for all $t$, then so is $\hat u(\cdot,t)$. Setting $t=0$ gives $\hat f=\hat u(\cdot,0)$ radial; differentiating at $t=0$ gives $\hat g=\partial_t\hat u(\cdot,0)$ radial. Hence $f,g$ are radial.
Equivalently: rotations commute with the wave operator, so by uniqueness $u(R\,\cdot,t)$ solves the problem with data $f(R\cdot),g(R\cdot)$; radial $u$ $\Leftrightarrow$ rotation-invariant data.

concepts.label: Theta Function & Functional Equation

content.statement: Poisson summation applied to the Gaussian gives the modular identity for the theta function.
content.formal: For $t>0$, $\theta(t)=\sum_{n\in\mathbb Z}e^{-\pi n^2t}$ satisfies $\theta(1/t)=\sqrt t\,\theta(t)$.
content.intuition: The Gaussian is essentially fixed by the Fourier transform, so Poisson summation turns a sum at scale $t$ into a sum at reciprocal scale $1/t$.

assumptions: Uses the $2\pi$-Fourier convention where $e^{-\pi x^2}$ is self-dual.

concepts.label: Wave Equation

content.statement: Vibrating strings decompose into standing harmonic waves.
content.formal: For fixed-end data on $[0,\pi]$, formal solutions have the form $u(x,t)=\sum_{n\ge1}(a_n\cos nt+b_n\sin nt)\sin(nx)$. Periodic formulations use complex exponentials instead.
content.intuition: The wave equation preserves oscillation rather than damping it. Each spatial harmonic evolves as a harmonic oscillator in time.

assumptions: Boundary conditions determine whether sine series, cosine series, or complex Fourier series are appropriate.

concepts.label: Wave Equation in $\mathbb R^d$ (Huygens, Kirchhoff)

content.statement: The multidimensional wave equation is solved in frequency by independent oscillators.
content.formal: For $\partial_t^2u=\Delta u$ with $u(\cdot,0)=f$, $u_t(\cdot,0)=g$, the Fourier transform gives $\hat u(\xi,t)=\cos(2\pi|\xi|t)\hat f(\xi)+\frac{\sin(2\pi|\xi|t)}{2\pi|\xi|}\hat g(\xi)$, with the second term interpreted by its limit at $\xi=0$.
content.intuition: Each frequency oscillates at speed proportional to $|\xi|$. Special odd-dimensional formulas reveal Huygens' principle, where waves propagate sharply without a trailing wake.

assumptions: Formula uses the $2\pi$-frequency convention and requires suitable data or distributional interpretation.

source.citation: Stein–Shakarchi: Ch.6 Lemma 3.3; Ch.6 Lemma 3.4; Ch.6 Lemma 3.5; Ch.6 Theorem 2.4; Ch.6 Theorem 3.1; Ch.6 Theorem 3.2; Ch.6 Theorem 3.6; Ch.6 Theorem 3.7
source.ref: Stein–Shakarchi: Ch.6 Lemma 3.3; Ch.6 Lemma 3.4; Ch.6 Lemma 3.5; Ch.6 Theorem 2.4; Ch.6 Theorem 3.1; Ch.6 Theorem 3.2; Ch.6 Theorem 3.6; Ch.6 Theorem 3.7

concepts.label: Weyl Equidistribution

content.statement: Equidistribution can be tested by checking the decay of all nonzero exponential averages.
content.formal: A sequence $(x_n)$ in $\mathbb R/\mathbb Z$ is equidistributed iff $\frac1N\sum_{n=1}^N e^{2\pi i kx_n}\to0$ for every nonzero $k\in\mathbb Z$. In particular, $x_n=n\alpha$ is equidistributed when $\alpha\notin\mathbb Q$.
content.intuition: Intervals can be approximated by trigonometric polynomials, so uniform distribution of points is equivalent to cancellation against every nonzero frequency.

assumptions: The criterion is stated on the circle $\mathbb R/\mathbb Z$.

source.citation: Stein–Shakarchi: Ch.4 Corollary 2.3; Ch.4 Lemma 2.2; Ch.4 Theorem 2.1
source.ref: Stein–Shakarchi: Ch.4 Corollary 2.3; Ch.4 Lemma 2.2; Ch.4 Theorem 2.1

concepts.label: Øving 6 · P1 — A heat equation with a decay term

content.statement: A damped heat equation solved mode-by-mode via Fourier series.
content.formal: For $u(x,t)=\sum_{n\in\mathbb Z}\hat f(n)e^{-ct-\sigma^2n^2t}e^{inx}$: (a) show $u\in C^\infty((0,2\pi)\times(0,T))$; (b) show $u_t+cu-\sigma^2u_{xx}=0$ in $(0,2\pi)\times(0,T)$.
content.intuition: The Gaussian damping $e^{-\sigma^2n^2t}$ smooths instantly (super-polynomial decay), and the PDE is just the eigenvalue relation $\partial_t=\sigma^2\partial_{xx}-c$ acting mode-by-mode.

steps.content:
The coefficients $\hat f(n)$ are bounded, $|\hat f(n)|\le\|f\|_{L^1}$.

\textbf{(a)} Fix $t_0\in(0,T)$. For $t\ge t_0$ the factor $e^{-\sigma^2n^2t}\le e^{-\sigma^2n^2t_0}$ decays faster than any power of $n$, so for all $j,m$,
$$\sum_n\big|\hat f(n)\big|\,|n|^{j}\,(c+\sigma^2n^2)^{m}\,e^{-ct-\sigma^2n^2t}<\infty\quad\text{uniformly on }[t_0,T),$$
which dominates every term-by-term $x$- and $t$-derivative. Hence the series and all its partial derivatives converge uniformly on $(0,2\pi)\times[t_0,T)$, so $u\in C^\infty((0,2\pi)\times(0,T))$.

\textbf{(b)} Differentiating term by term (justified by (a)): $\partial_t$ brings down $(-c-\sigma^2n^2)$ and $\partial_{xx}$ brings down $(in)^2=-n^2$. The operator applied to the $n$-th term gives the factor
$$(-c-\sigma^2n^2)+c-\sigma^2(-n^2)=-c-\sigma^2n^2+c+\sigma^2n^2=0.$$
Every term vanishes, so $u_t+cu-\sigma^2u_{xx}=0$. $\blacksquare$

concepts.label: Øving 9 · P3 — Wave equation and d'Alembert

content.statement: Solve the 1-D wave equation by Fourier methods and derive d'Alembert's formula.
content.formal: For $f\in\mathcal S(\mathbb R)$, $c\in\mathbb R$, let $u(x,t)=\mathcal F^{-1}\big[\hat f(\xi)\cos(2\pi c|\xi|t)\big](x)$. (a) Show $u\in C^\infty(\mathbb R\times(0,\infty))$ and $u_{tt}=c^2u_{xx}$. (b) Show $u\to f$, $u_t\to0$ as $t\to0^+$. (c) Show $u(x,t)=\tfrac12\big(f(x+ct)+f(x-ct)\big)$.
content.intuition: On the Fourier side the wave equation is the oscillator $\partial_{tt}=-(2\pi c\xi)^2$; the cosine multiplier splits into two translations, giving left- and right-moving copies of the data.

steps.content:
\textbf{(a)} $\hat u(\xi,t)=\hat f(\xi)\cos(2\pi c|\xi|t)$. Since $\cos(2\pi c|\xi|t)$ is even and smooth in $\xi$ and bounded by $1$, while $\hat f\in\mathcal S$, the product and all its $x,t$-derivatives are Schwartz in $\xi$ uniformly on $t$-compacts, so $u\in C^\infty$. Computing symbols: $\partial_{tt}\hat u=-(2\pi c|\xi|)^2\hat u=-4\pi^2c^2\xi^2\hat u$, while $\widehat{c^2u_{xx}}=c^2(2\pi i\xi)^2\hat u=-4\pi^2c^2\xi^2\hat u$. Equal, so $u_{tt}=c^2u_{xx}$.

\textbf{(b)} As $t\to0^+$, $\cos(2\pi c|\xi|t)\to1$ so $\hat u\to\hat f$, hence $u\to f$ (dominated convergence, $\hat f\in L^1$); and $\hat{u_t}=-2\pi c|\xi|\sin(2\pi c|\xi|t)\hat f\to0$, so $u_t\to0$.

\textbf{(c)} Write $\cos(2\pi c\xi t)=\tfrac12(e^{2\pi ic\xi t}+e^{-2\pi ic\xi t})$ (even, so $|\xi|\to\xi$ is harmless). Since $\mathcal F^{-1}[\hat f(\xi)e^{2\pi ic\xi t}](x)=f(x+ct)$,
$$u(x,t)=\tfrac12\big(f(x+ct)+f(x-ct)\big),$$
d'Alembert's formula. $\blacksquare$

concepts.label: Dirichlet Character

content.statement: A Dirichlet character is a group character modulo $q$, extended by zero on non-coprime integers.
content.formal: Modulo $q$, a Dirichlet character $\chi:\mathbb Z\to\mathbb C$ is $q$-periodic, completely multiplicative, agrees with a character on $(\mathbb Z/q\mathbb Z)^\times$, and satisfies $\chi(n)=0$ when $\gcd(n,q)>1$. For $\gcd(a,q)=1$, $\frac1{\varphi(q)}\sum_{\chi\bmod q}\overline{\chi(a)}\chi(n)$ detects the reduced residue class $n\equiv a\pmod q$.
content.intuition: Dirichlet characters are multiplicative Fourier modes for reduced residue classes. They convert congruence conditions into character sums.

assumptions: Characters are extended by zero away from integers coprime to $q$.

concepts.label: Dirichlet L-Function

content.statement: A Dirichlet L-function is the zeta function twisted by a Dirichlet character.
content.formal: For $\operatorname{Re}s>1$, $L(s,\chi)=\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\chi(n)n^{-s}=\prod_p(1-\chi(p)p^{-s})^{-1}$. For a nonprincipal character, $L(s,\chi)$ has a finite value at $s=1$ by analytic continuation or Abel limiting.
content.intuition: The character twist filters integers, and especially primes, by residue class. The behavior near $s=1$ is the analytic lever in Dirichlet’s theorem.

assumptions: Euler product identity is initially valid for $\operatorname{Re}s>1$. The principal character gives a zeta-like pole at $s=1$.

source.citation: Stein–Shakarchi: Ch.8 Theorem 2.4; Ch.8 Proposition 3.1; Ch.8 Proposition 3.4; Ch.8 Lemma 3.5; Ch.8 Proposition 3.6
source.ref: Stein–Shakarchi: Ch.8 Theorem 2.4; Ch.8 Proposition 3.1; Ch.8 Proposition 3.4; Ch.8 Lemma 3.5; Ch.8 Proposition 3.6

concepts.label: Divergence of the Prime Sum

content.statement: The reciprocal sum over the primes diverges.
content.formal: $\sum_p\frac1p=\infty$. One proof takes logarithms of the Euler product and compares $\log\zeta(s)$ as $s\to1^+$.
content.intuition: There are not merely infinitely many primes; they are numerous enough that their reciprocal weights still accumulate without bound.

assumptions: The theorem is stronger than Euclid’s infinitude of primes.

concepts.label: Euclidean Algorithm & Bézout

content.statement: The Euclidean algorithm computes greatest common divisors and gives Bézout identities.
content.formal: For integers $a,b$ with $b>0$, there are unique integers $q,r$ such that $a=qb+r$ and $0\le r<b$. Iterating division gives $\gcd(a,b)$, and there exist integers $m,n$ such that $\gcd(a,b)=ma+nb$.
content.formula: $$\begin{aligned} a,b&\in\mathbb Z,\\ b>0&\Longrightarrow \exists q,r\in\mathbb Z:\ a=qb+r,\quad 0\le r<b,\\ \gcd(a,b)&=ma+nb\quad(m,n\in\mathbb Z). \end{aligned}$$
content.intuition: Repeatedly subtract the largest possible multiple until no remainder remains. Running the steps backward expresses the gcd as an integer linear combination.

assumptions: Usually stated for integers $a,b$, not both zero.

source.citation: Stein–Shakarchi: Ch.8 Theorem 1.1; Ch.8 Theorem 1.2; Ch.8 Corollary 1.3; Ch.8 Corollary 1.4
source.ref: Stein–Shakarchi: Ch.8 Theorem 1.1; Ch.8 Theorem 1.2; Ch.8 Corollary 1.3; Ch.8 Corollary 1.4

content.statement: Euler’s phi function counts the invertible residue classes modulo $q$.
content.formal: $\varphi(q)=| (\mathbb Z/q\mathbb Z)^\times |=|\{1\le a\le q:\gcd(a,q)=1\}|$.
content.intuition: The number $\varphi(q)$ is the size of the frequency system used by Dirichlet characters modulo $q$.

concepts.label: Euler Product for the Zeta Function

content.statement: The zeta function factors into local contributions from each prime.
content.formal: For $\operatorname{Re}s>1$, $\zeta(s)=\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}n^{-s}=\prod_{p}(1-p^{-s})^{-1}$, with absolute convergence justifying the rearrangement.
content.intuition: Unique factorization turns the sum over all integers into independent geometric series over primes. This is where arithmetic becomes analysis.

assumptions: Initially valid only in the half-plane $\operatorname{Re}s>1$.

source.citation: Stein–Shakarchi: Ch.8 Lemma 1.8; Ch.8 Proposition 1.9; Ch.8 Theorem 1.10; Ch.8 Proposition 3.2
source.ref: Stein–Shakarchi: Ch.8 Lemma 1.8; Ch.8 Proposition 1.9; Ch.8 Theorem 1.10; Ch.8 Proposition 3.2

concepts.label: Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic

content.statement: Every integer greater than $1$ factors uniquely into primes.
content.formal: For every integer $n>1$, there exist primes $p_1<\cdots<p_k$ and positive integers $\alpha_1,\dots,\alpha_k$ such that $n=p_1^{\alpha_1}\cdots p_k^{\alpha_k}$. This factorization is unique.
content.intuition: Primes are the multiplicative atoms of the integers. Unique factorization lets analytic sums over integers become products over primes.

assumptions: Units and ordering are ignored: uniqueness means the same primes occur with the same exponents.

content.statement: There are infinitely many primes.
content.formal: If $p_1,\dots,p_k$ are finitely many primes, then any prime divisor of $p_1\cdots p_k+1$ is not among $p_1,\dots,p_k$. Hence no finite list contains all primes.
content.intuition: Any alleged complete list of primes manufactures a new number that escapes divisibility by every prime on the list.

concepts.label: Non-Vanishing of $L(1,\chi)$

content.statement: Nonprincipal Dirichlet L-functions do not vanish at $s=1$.
content.formal: If $\chi$ is a nonprincipal Dirichlet character modulo $q$, then $L(1,\chi)\ne0$.
content.intuition: The character decomposition of residue classes would fail to force primes in every class if these values could vanish. Non-vanishing blocks that cancellation.

assumptions: The principal character is excluded; its $L$-function has a pole at $s=1$, not a finite nonzero value.

source.citation: Stein–Shakarchi: Ch.8 Theorem 3.7; Ch.8 Theorem 2.3; Ch.8 Lemma 3.8; Ch.8 Lemma 3.9; Ch.8 Proposition 3.10; Ch.8 Proposition 3.11; Ch.8 Proposition 3.13; Ch.8 Theorem 3.12; Ch.8 Lemma 3.14; Ch.8 Lemma 3.15
source.ref: Stein–Shakarchi: Ch.8 Theorem 3.7; Ch.8 Theorem 2.3; Ch.8 Lemma 3.8; Ch.8 Lemma 3.9; Ch.8 Proposition 3.10; Ch.8 Proposition 3.11; Ch.8 Proposition 3.13; Ch.8 Theorem 3.12; Ch.8 Lemma 3.14; Ch.8 Lemma 3.15

concepts.label: Primes in Arithmetic Progressions

content.statement: Every reduced arithmetic progression contains infinitely many primes.
content.formal: If $q\ge2$ and $\gcd(a,q)=1$, then infinitely many primes $p$ satisfy $p\equiv a\pmod q$. The proof expands residue-class indicators using Dirichlet characters and uses $L(1,\chi)\ne0$ for nonprincipal characters.
content.intuition: Finite Fourier analysis separates residue classes; analytic information about the resulting $L$-functions prevents any reduced class from running out of primes.

assumptions: The condition $\gcd(a,q)=1$ is necessary: otherwise every number in the progression shares a fixed divisor with $q$.

content.statement: Integers are grouped by having the same remainder modulo $q$.
content.definition: $$a\equiv b\pmod q\Longleftrightarrow q\mid(a-b),\qquad [a]_q=\{n\in\mathbb Z:n\equiv a\pmod q\}.$$
content.formal: For $q\ge2$, $a\equiv b\pmod q$ means $q\mid(a-b)$. The residue class of $a$ is $[a]_q=\{n\in\mathbb Z:n\equiv a\pmod q\}$.
content.intuition: A residue class is one lane in modular arithmetic. Dirichlet’s theorem asks whether each reduced lane contains infinitely many primes.

concepts.label: Riemann Zeta Function

content.statement: The Dirichlet series whose Euler product encodes the primes.
content.formal: For $\operatorname{Re}s>1$, $\zeta(s)=\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}n^{-s}$. It has Euler product $\zeta(s)=\prod_p(1-p^{-s})^{-1}$.
content.intuition: The zeta function packages all positive integers into one analytic object; unique factorization then rewrites it as a product over primes.

assumptions: The displayed Dirichlet series and Euler product are valid for $\operatorname{Re}s>1$.

content.statement: The Mellin-transform identity linking $\zeta$, $\vartheta$, and $\Gamma$.
content.formal: The zeta function is defined for $s>1$ by $$\zeta(s)=\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\frac1{n^s}.$$ Verify the identity $$\pi^{-s/2}\Gamma(s/2)\zeta(s)=\frac12\int_0^\infty t^{s/2-1}(\vartheta(t)-1)\,dt\qquad(s>1),$$ where $\Gamma$ and $\vartheta$ are the gamma and theta functions, respectively: $$\Gamma(s)=\int_0^\infty e^{-t}t^{s-1}\,dt,\qquad \vartheta(t)=\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty}e^{-\pi n^2t}.$$
content.intuition: Each $n^{-s}$ is a Gamma integral in disguise; summing them assembles $\zeta$, while the tail $\vartheta-1$ packages the whole series — the bridge from theta's modularity to $\zeta$'s functional equation.

steps.content:
For each $n\ge1$ substitute $u=\pi n^2 t$ in the Gamma integral:
$$\int_0^\infty t^{s/2-1}e^{-\pi n^2 t}\,dt=(\pi n^2)^{-s/2}\int_0^\infty u^{s/2-1}e^{-u}\,du=\pi^{-s/2}\Gamma(\tfrac s2)\,n^{-s}.$$
Now $\vartheta(t)-1=2\sum_{n\ge1}e^{-\pi n^2 t}$, so
$$\frac12\int_0^\infty t^{s/2-1}(\vartheta(t)-1)\,dt=\sum_{n\ge1}\int_0^\infty t^{s/2-1}e^{-\pi n^2 t}\,dt=\pi^{-s/2}\Gamma(\tfrac s2)\sum_{n\ge1}n^{-s}=\pi^{-s/2}\Gamma(\tfrac s2)\zeta(s).$$
Interchanging sum and integral is justified for $s>1$ by Tonelli (all terms positive, total finite).
This integral is the gateway to the analytic continuation and functional equation of $\zeta$, via the modular transformation $\vartheta(1/t)=\sqrt t\,\vartheta(t)$.
